cases.title: New Headache and High Blood Pressure at 34 Weeks
cases.chiefComplaint: A 34-week pregnant woman presents with a persistent headache and elevated blood pressure noted at her clinic visit.
cases.history: A 31-year-old G2P1 at 34 weeks 2 days by reliable first-trimester dating presents to L&D triage after her obstetrician noted a blood pressure of 166/108 mmHg at clinic. For the past two days she has had a frontal, throbbing headache that is not relieved by acetaminophen, along with intermittent blurred vision and 'flashing lights.' She denies abdominal pain, but reports new swelling of her hands and face over the past week. Fetal movement is normal. Her first pregnancy was uncomplicated with a term vaginal delivery. PMH is otherwise unremarkable. Her only medications are a prenatal vitamin and ferrous sulfate 325 mg daily. She has no known drug allergies. She does not smoke, drink alcohol, or use drugs. Family history is notable for a mother with chronic hypertension and a sister who had 'high blood pressure' during pregnancy. She works as a teacher and lives with her partner. Her booking blood pressure at 9 weeks was 112/70 mmHg, and prior visits showed normal pressures with no proteinuria.
cases.exam: The patient is alert and uncomfortable from headache. Fundal height is appropriate for 34 weeks. The uterus is soft and nontender. Cardiopulmonary exam is normal with clear lungs. There is 2+ pitting edema of the lower extremities and mild periorbital and facial edema. Neurologic exam shows brisk patellar reflexes (3+) with 2 beats of clonus bilaterally; cranial nerves are intact and there is no focal deficit. Right upper quadrant is nontender. Fetal heart tones are 140 bpm and reactive. No vaginal bleeding or leakage of fluid is noted on external inspection.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: After confirming the elevated blood pressure on repeat measurement, what is the most appropriate immediate set of investigations?
options: Reassure and discharge with a 1-week clinic follow-up as the first step today
Send a CBC, creatinine, AST/ALT, LDH,
Obtain a CT head before any further workup as the first step today
Start oral nifedipine and discharge home once the pressure normalizes
stages.explanation: Severe-range hypertension with neurologic symptoms in pregnancy mandates an evaluation for preeclampsia with severe features. The correct step is laboratory assessment for end-organ involvement (CBC for platelets and hemolysis, creatinine for renal function, transaminases for hepatic involvement, LDH for hemolysis) plus a urine protein/creatinine ratio, alongside continuous fetal monitoring. Discharge with routine follow-up is unsafe given severe-range pressures and symptoms. CT head is not first-line; her symptoms are explained by preeclampsia and imaging is reserved for focal deficits or atypical features. Treating the pressure then discharging ignores the need for inpatient evaluation, magnesium, and a delivery plan in a symptomatic patient with severe-range hypertension.

stages.label: LABS & URINE STUDIES
stages.content:
Hemoglobin 12.8 g/dL
Platelets 188 ×10³/µL
Creatinine 0.8 mg/dL
AST 24 U/L
ALT 21 U/L
LDH 210 U/L
Uric acid 6.2 mg/dL
Urine protein/creatinine ratio 0.6 mg/mg
Fetal monitor: category I tracing, no contractions

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Based on the presentation and results, what is the most accurate diagnosis?
options: Gestational hypertension
HELLP syndrome
Preeclampsia with severe features
Chronic hypertension
stages.explanation: She has new-onset hypertension after 20 weeks (booking BP was normal) with proteinuria (protein/creatinine ratio greater than 0.3) and, critically, severe-range blood pressure (greater than or equal to 160/110) plus cerebral symptoms (headache, visual changes) — these severe features define preeclampsia with severe features even independent of the proteinuria. Gestational hypertension is excluded by the presence of proteinuria and severe features. Chronic hypertension would have been present before 20 weeks; her early pressures were normal. HELLP requires hemolysis, elevated liver enzymes, and low platelets — her platelets, AST/ALT, and LDH are all normal, so HELLP is not present.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which combination of therapies should be initiated now?
options: IV magnesium sulfate alone, deferring antihypertensives until BP exceeds 180/120
IV labetalol for the severe-range BP
Oral methyldopa alone with outpatient monitoring as initial therapy
Furosemide and fluid restriction to reduce edema as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Severe-range hypertension requires prompt antihypertensive treatment to prevent maternal stroke; IV labetalol (or hydralazine, or oral immediate-release nifedipine) is first-line. Magnesium sulfate is given concurrently for seizure prophylaxis in preeclampsia with severe features. Methyldopa with outpatient management is inappropriate for a symptomatic patient with severe features. Withholding antihypertensives until 180/120 is dangerous — the threshold for acute treatment is 160/110. Furosemide is not used to treat preeclampsia edema and can worsen the intravascular volume depletion already present in these patients; diuretics are reserved for pulmonary edema.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Regarding timing of delivery, what is the most appropriate plan?
options: Administer betamethasone and, after maternal stabilization, proceed toward delivery at this gestation rather than prolonging the pregnancy
Immediate cesarean delivery regardless of fetal status with appropriate follow-up
Discharge on oral labetalol once BP is controlled and continue the pregnancy to 37 weeks at home with appropriate follow-up
Expectant management until 39 weeks with weekly BP checks with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: Preeclampsia with severe features at 34 weeks generally warrants delivery after maternal stabilization; the definitive treatment is delivery. A course of betamethasone is given for fetal lung maturity since she is under 34 weeks 6 days, and delivery is then pursued (vaginal delivery is acceptable if no obstetric contraindication — cesarean is not automatic). Expectant management to 39 weeks is unsafe with severe features. Immediate cesarean 'regardless of fetal status' is wrong because route of delivery is individualized and the fetus is currently reassuring. Home management is contraindicated — severe features require inpatient care and delivery, not outpatient continuation to 37 weeks.

pearls: Severe features include BP greater than or equal to 160/110, cerebral or visual symptoms, RUQ/epigastric pain, thrombocytopenia, elevated transaminases, renal insufficiency, or pulmonary edema — any one suffices, even without proteinuria.
Severe-range BP (greater than or equal to 160/110) is an emergency: treat within 30-60 minutes with IV labetalol, IV hydralazine, or oral immediate-release nifedipine.
Magnesium sulfate is for seizure prophylaxis, not blood pressure control; monitor for loss of reflexes, respiratory depression, and give calcium gluconate for toxicity.
Definitive treatment is delivery; preeclampsia with severe features at or beyond 34 weeks generally warrants delivery after stabilization and steroids if under 34w6d.

cases.title: Witnessed Convulsion in a Late-Pregnancy Patient
cases.chiefComplaint: A pregnant woman is brought in by EMS after a witnessed whole-body convulsion at home.
cases.history: A 22-year-old G1P0 estimated to be near term is brought to the ED by ambulance after her partner witnessed a generalized tonic-clonic seizure lasting about 90 seconds, followed by confusion. She has had no prenatal care and is unsure of her exact gestational age; fundal measurement and her partner suggest roughly 37 weeks. Over the preceding day she complained of a severe headache and 'spots' in her vision, which they attributed to stress. She has no known history of epilepsy, head trauma, drug use, or prior seizures. She takes no medications and reports no allergies. She recently immigrated and has limited English; collateral history is obtained through her partner and an interpreter. There is no history of fever, neck stiffness, or sick contacts. She does not smoke or drink. Family history is unknown. On EMS arrival she was postictal with a blood pressure of 178/114 mmHg. She is now arousable but lethargic and reports a pounding headache.
cases.exam: The patient is postictal, drowsy but rousable, and oriented to person only. There is no tongue laceration but mild urinary incontinence is noted. Pupils are equal and reactive; there is no focal motor deficit or facial droop. Deep tendon reflexes are 4+ with sustained clonus at both ankles. The neck is supple. The gravid uterus is consistent with a term pregnancy, firm but nontender, with no vaginal bleeding. Fetal heart tones are present at 148 bpm. There is marked facial and lower-extremity edema. Lungs are clear and the abdomen aside from the gravid uterus is benign.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: While protecting the airway and positioning the patient, what is the single most important pharmacologic intervention for this seizure?
options: IV phenytoin loading dose as the first step today
IV lorazepam as the primary agent to stop and prevent seizures
IV levetiracetam as the first step today
IV magnesium sulfate loading dose followed by maintenance infusion
stages.explanation: In a pregnant patient near term with severe-range hypertension, headache, and visual symptoms, a generalized seizure is eclampsia until proven otherwise. Magnesium sulfate is the first-line agent to both treat and prevent recurrent eclamptic seizures and is superior to standard antiepileptics in this setting. Lorazepam may be used as a temporizing measure for active status seizures but is not the definitive therapy and risks neonatal/maternal respiratory depression. Phenytoin and levetiracetam are inferior to magnesium for eclampsia prophylaxis and treatment, as demonstrated by the Collaborative Eclampsia Trial. Magnesium also requires monitoring of reflexes and respiratory status.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Her blood pressure remains 182/116 mmHg after the seizure stops. What is the next priority?
options: Proceed directly to emergent cesarean delivery before controlling the blood pressure
Administer IV labetalol or hydralazine to bring the severe-range pressure into a safer range
Give a fluid bolus of 2 liters of normal saline as initial therapy
Order an emergent CT head before any further treatment as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Persistent severe-range hypertension after an eclamptic seizure must be treated promptly with IV labetalol or hydralazine to reduce the risk of maternal hemorrhagic stroke, which is the leading cause of death in eclampsia. Rushing to cesarean before stabilizing the mother is dangerous — the mother must first be stabilized (seizure controlled, BP lowered, oxygenation ensured); route and timing of delivery follow. A 2-liter fluid bolus risks pulmonary edema in a preeclamptic patient with capillary leak. CT head is not required for a typical eclamptic seizure and should not delay BP control; it is reserved for focal deficits, prolonged coma, or atypical features.

stages.label: LABS & MONITORING
stages.content:
Hemoglobin 11.9 g/dL
Platelets 142 ×10³/µL
Creatinine 1.0 mg/dL
AST 38 U/L
ALT 41 U/L
LDH 260 U/L
Uric acid 7.1 mg/dL
Urine dipstick: 3+ protein
Fetal monitor: baseline 150 bpm with recurrent late decelerations, recovering after maternal stabilization

stages.label: DEFINITIVE TREATMENT
stages.question: Once the seizure is controlled, the blood pressure is treated, and the patient is stabilized, what is the definitive next step?
options: Proceed with delivery now that the mother is stabilized
Expectant management with serial labs until 39 weeks in this setting
Repeat magnesium bolus and observe for 48 hours without delivery
Discharge home on oral antihypertensives with close follow-up
stages.explanation: Eclampsia is an indication for delivery once the mother is stabilized, regardless of gestational age — and at approximately 37 weeks the fetus is term. The definitive treatment of eclampsia/preeclampsia is delivery of the fetus and placenta. Discharge home is never appropriate after an eclamptic seizure. Expectant management to 39 weeks is contraindicated; eclampsia at term mandates delivery. Simply repeating magnesium and observing for 48 hours without delivering fails to address the underlying disease and exposes both mother and fetus (note the late decelerations) to ongoing risk. Magnesium is continued through delivery and for at least 24 hours postpartum.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Two hours after the magnesium infusion is running, the nurse notes the patient's deep tendon reflexes are now absent and her respiratory rate has fallen to 9/min. What is the immediate management?
options: Increase the magnesium infusion rate to better control symptoms
Stop the magnesium infusion
Administer IV flumazenil as the leading concern
Administer IV naloxone as the leading concern
stages.explanation: Loss of deep tendon reflexes and respiratory depression are classic signs of magnesium toxicity. The immediate steps are to stop the magnesium infusion and give IV calcium gluconate, which directly antagonizes magnesium at the neuromuscular junction; provide respiratory support as needed. Increasing the infusion would worsen toxicity and could cause cardiac arrest. Naloxone reverses opioids, which are not the cause here. Flumazenil reverses benzodiazepines and is not indicated; it can also precipitate seizures, which is especially dangerous in eclampsia. Magnesium levels and urine output should be monitored, as toxicity is more likely with renal impairment.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After delivery and stabilization, how long should magnesium sulfate be continued and why?
options: Stop magnesium immediately at delivery since the placenta is removed with appropriate follow-up
Continue magnesium for a full 7 days regardless of clinical status with appropriate follow-up
Continue magnesium for at least 24 hours postpartum because seizures can still occur after delivery
Switch to oral phenytoin for 6 months of maintenance therapy with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: Although delivery is the definitive treatment, the risk of eclamptic seizures persists into the postpartum period, with a substantial fraction of eclampsia occurring after delivery. Magnesium sulfate is therefore continued for at least 24 hours postpartum (and for 24 hours after the last seizure if seizures occurred later), with monitoring of reflexes, respiratory rate, and urine output. Stopping magnesium immediately at delivery leaves the patient unprotected during the highest-risk window. A fixed 7-day course is not standard and risks unnecessary toxicity. Long-term oral antiepileptics like phenytoin are not indicated, because eclampsia is not a chronic seizure disorder — it resolves as the postpartum physiology normalizes; ongoing blood pressure control is the relevant longer-term concern.

pearls: A generalized seizure in a hypertensive pregnant or recently postpartum patient is eclampsia until proven otherwise; magnesium sulfate is both treatment and prophylaxis.
Stabilize the mother first — airway, control the seizure, lower severe-range BP — before delivering; the mother is the priority.
Magnesium toxicity progresses from loss of deep tendon reflexes to respiratory depression to cardiac arrest; treat with calcium gluconate and stop the infusion.
Eclampsia is an indication for delivery once stabilized; magnesium continues for at least 24 hours postpartum because seizures can occur after delivery.

cases.title: Right Upper Quadrant Pain and Malaise in the Third Trimester
cases.chiefComplaint: A 35-year-old pregnant woman is admitted with right upper quadrant pain, nausea, and feeling generally unwell.
cases.history: A 35-year-old G3P2 at 36 weeks 0 days is admitted to the antepartum ward with two days of right upper quadrant and epigastric pain, nausea, and profound malaise. She describes the pain as a constant ache 'under the ribs' and reports one episode of vomiting. She has had a mild headache but denies visual changes or seizures. Over the past day she noticed her urine is darker. Her prior two pregnancies were complicated by gestational hypertension. At a clinic visit one week ago her BP was 138/88 mmHg with trace proteinuria. PMH is otherwise unremarkable. Medications are a prenatal vitamin and labetalol 200 mg twice daily started for borderline pressures. She has no allergies. She does not smoke, drink, or use drugs. Family history includes preeclampsia in her mother. She works as an accountant. She denies right-sided abdominal trauma, gallstones, or prior hepatobiliary disease. Fetal movement remains present but she feels it is slightly reduced today.
cases.exam: The patient appears unwell and mildly jaundiced with scleral icterus. The abdomen is gravid; there is marked tenderness in the right upper quadrant and epigastrium without rebound or guarding, and no Murphy sign elicited cleanly. The uterus is soft and nontender to palpation, with fundal height appropriate. There is 2+ lower-extremity edema. Deep tendon reflexes are 3+ without clonus. There are scattered petechiae over the forearms and a small ecchymosis at a prior venipuncture site. Lungs are clear. Fetal heart tones are 145 bpm. No vaginal bleeding or fluid leakage is noted.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given RUQ pain, malaise, and petechiae in a hypertensive third-trimester patient, what is the most appropriate initial laboratory workup?
options: Right upper quadrant ultrasound only, to evaluate for cholelithiasis
Lipase and a hepatitis viral panel only as the first step today
Reassurance with repeat labs in one week as the first step today
CBC with peripheral smear, AST/ALT, total/direct bilirubin, LDH, haptoglobin,
stages.explanation: The combination of hypertension, RUQ/epigastric pain, jaundice, and bleeding signs raises strong suspicion for HELLP syndrome. The appropriate workup evaluates for its three components: Hemolysis (smear for schistocytes, LDH, haptoglobin, indirect bilirubin), Elevated Liver enzymes (AST/ALT), and Low Platelets (CBC); a coagulation panel screens for DIC. A RUQ ultrasound alone misses the hematologic and hepatic derangement and would not diagnose HELLP. Lipase and viral hepatitis testing address other causes but are not the priority given the clinical picture. Waiting a week is unsafe given an acutely unwell, potentially deteriorating patient and fetus.

stages.label: LABS & SMEAR
stages.content:
Hemoglobin 9.4 g/dL
Platelets 70 ×10³/µL
Peripheral smear: schistocytes and burr cells present
AST 245 U/L
ALT 198 U/L
LDH 760 U/L
Total bilirubin 2.4 mg/dL (indirect predominant)
Haptoglobin <10 mg/dL
Creatinine 1.1 mg/dL
INR 1.1, fibrinogen 280 mg/dL
Urine protein/creatinine ratio 0.9 mg/mg

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which diagnosis best fits this constellation of findings?
options: HELLP syndrome
Acute cholecystitis
Acute fatty liver of pregnancy
Thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura
stages.explanation: The findings meet criteria for HELLP syndrome: Hemolysis (schistocytes, LDH 760, low haptoglobin, indirect hyperbilirubinemia), Elevated Liver enzymes (AST 245, ALT 198), and Low Platelets (70k), in a hypertensive preeclamptic patient. Acute fatty liver of pregnancy typically presents with hypoglycemia, marked coagulopathy, elevated ammonia, and renal failure (Swansea criteria), which are not the dominant picture here (fibrinogen and INR are near-normal). TTP classically shows the pentad with more profound thrombocytopenia, prominent neurologic signs, fever, and normal liver enzymes and is not associated with hypertension/proteinuria. Acute cholecystitis would not explain the hemolysis, thrombocytopenia, transaminitis, and proteinuria.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management plan at 36 weeks with these findings?
options: Start magnesium sulfate, control blood pressure,
Platelet transfusion to normalize the count, then discharge
Expectant management with serial labs until 39 weeks as initial therapy
High-dose corticosteroids as definitive therapy to avoid delivery
stages.explanation: HELLP is a severe form of preeclampsia, and delivery is the definitive treatment — especially at 36 weeks, which is term-enough that prolonging the pregnancy offers no fetal benefit and risks maternal deterioration (hepatic rupture, DIC, abruption). Magnesium sulfate is given for seizure prophylaxis and antihypertensives for severe-range pressures. Expectant management to 39 weeks is contraindicated. Prophylactic platelet transfusion to 'normalize' the count is not indicated unless there is active bleeding or counts are very low before a procedure such as cesarean; routine normalization is not the goal. Corticosteroids may transiently raise platelets and are used for fetal lung maturity if preterm, but they are not definitive therapy and do not replace delivery.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: During delivery preparation she develops sudden severe RUQ pain, hypotension to 84/50 mmHg, and a falling hemoglobin. What complication must be ruled out immediately?
options: Round ligament pain as the leading concern
Subcapsular hepatic hematoma with possible rupture
Pyelonephritis as the leading concern
Uncomplicated reflux esophagitis
stages.explanation: Sudden worsening RUQ pain with hemodynamic instability and a dropping hemoglobin in HELLP is a hepatic subcapsular hematoma with possible rupture — a life-threatening emergency requiring urgent imaging, massive transfusion preparation, and surgical/interventional radiology involvement. Reflux esophagitis does not cause hypotension or a falling hemoglobin. Pyelonephritis presents with fever, costovertebral angle tenderness, and pyuria, not acute hemorrhagic shock. Round ligament pain is a benign, positional, sharp groin pain of pregnancy and never causes hemodynamic collapse. Recognizing hepatic rupture early is critical because it carries high maternal and fetal mortality.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After delivery, the patient's platelet count and liver enzymes stabilize. What is the expected postpartum course and monitoring plan?
options: Laboratory abnormalities typically reach their nadir in the first 1-2 days postpartum
HELLP always requires lifelong immunosuppression with appropriate follow-up
Plasma exchange is mandatory for all cases of HELLP with appropriate follow-up
She should be discharged on postpartum day 1 regardless of labs with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: In most cases of HELLP, the hematologic and hepatic abnormalities transiently worsen and reach their nadir within the first 24-48 hours after delivery before steadily improving, with platelets recovering and transaminases trending down thereafter. Management is supportive: continue magnesium for seizure prophylaxis for at least 24 hours postpartum, control blood pressure, and follow serial CBC and LFTs until clear improvement. HELLP does not require lifelong immunosuppression, as it resolves after delivery. Discharging on postpartum day 1 regardless of labs is unsafe during the nadir window. Plasma exchange is reserved for atypical or persistent cases or when TTP/HUS is suspected, not mandatory for routine HELLP that improves after delivery.

pearls: HELLP = Hemolysis (schistocytes, high LDH, low haptoglobin), Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets; it is a severe variant of preeclampsia and can occur with only mildly elevated BP.
Delivery is the definitive treatment; magnesium for seizure prophylaxis and antihypertensives for severe-range BP are adjuncts.
Sudden RUQ pain with shock and falling hemoglobin suggests hepatic subcapsular hematoma or rupture — a surgical emergency.
Distinguish HELLP from AFLP (hypoglycemia, coagulopathy, high ammonia) and TTP/HUS (neurologic signs, normal LFTs, more severe thrombocytopenia).

cases.title: Elevated Blood Pressure at a Routine Late-Pregnancy Visit
cases.chiefComplaint: A 28-year-old pregnant woman has a blood pressure reading above normal at her routine prenatal visit.
cases.history: A 28-year-old G1P0 at 29 weeks 3 days presents for a routine prenatal visit and is found to have a blood pressure of 144/92 mmHg. A repeat measurement after 15 minutes of rest, using an appropriately sized cuff, is 146/94 mmHg. She feels well, with no headache, visual changes, epigastric or right upper quadrant pain, or shortness of breath, and reports normal fetal movement. She has no swelling beyond mild ankle edema. Her booking blood pressure at 8 weeks was 118/74 mmHg and all subsequent readings through 20 weeks were normal. PMH is unremarkable; she has never been told she has high blood pressure. Her only medication is a prenatal vitamin; she has no allergies. She does not smoke, drink alcohol, or use drugs. Family history is notable for type 2 diabetes in her father. She works in retail and exercises occasionally. She has had no prior pregnancies and no history of renal disease.
cases.exam: The patient is well-appearing and in no distress. Fundal height is appropriate for 29 weeks and the uterus is soft and nontender. Cardiopulmonary exam is normal with clear lungs and no murmurs. There is trace bilateral ankle edema but no facial or hand swelling. Deep tendon reflexes are 2+ and symmetric without clonus. There is no right upper quadrant tenderness. Fundoscopic exam shows no hypertensive retinopathy. Fetal heart tones are 142 bpm and reactive. The remainder of the exam is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next step to characterize her elevated blood pressure?
options: Obtain a urine protein assessment and preeclampsia labs
Schedule an immediate cesarean delivery as the first step today
Diagnose chronic hypertension and start an antihypertensive immediately
Reassure and recheck only at the next routine visit in 4 weeks as the first step today
stages.explanation: New hypertension after 20 weeks requires evaluation for preeclampsia, so the immediate step is to assess for proteinuria and end-organ involvement (urine protein/creatinine ratio, CBC, creatinine, transaminases). This distinguishes gestational hypertension from preeclampsia. It cannot be chronic hypertension because her pressures before 20 weeks were normal, so labeling her chronic and treating without evaluation is incorrect. Reassuring and waiting four weeks is unsafe because new hypertension can herald preeclampsia and needs prompt assessment. Cesarean delivery is not indicated for an asymptomatic patient at 29 weeks with a reassuring fetus and no diagnosis of severe disease.

stages.label: LABS & URINE STUDIES
stages.content:
Urine dipstick: negative for protein
Urine protein/creatinine ratio 0.15 mg/mg
Hemoglobin 12.5 g/dL
Platelets 230 ×10³/µL
Creatinine 0.7 mg/dL
AST 19 U/L
ALT 17 U/L
Uric acid 4.5 mg/dL

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given new hypertension after 20 weeks with no proteinuria and no end-organ findings, what is the diagnosis?
options: Chronic hypertension in this clinical context
White coat hypertension requiring no follow-up
Gestational hypertension
Preeclampsia with severe features
stages.explanation: Gestational hypertension is defined as new-onset hypertension (greater than or equal to 140/90 on two occasions at least 4 hours apart) after 20 weeks WITHOUT proteinuria or severe features or other end-organ involvement. Her normal urine protein/creatinine ratio and normal labs exclude preeclampsia, and her normal pressures before 20 weeks exclude chronic hypertension. Preeclampsia with severe features requires either severe-range BP or end-organ involvement, neither of which is present. Calling it 'white coat' and dispensing with follow-up is wrong because confirmed elevated readings after 20 weeks meet the definition of gestational hypertension and require monitoring, since up to a quarter progress to preeclampsia.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management for this patient?
options: Outpatient surveillance with serial BP checks, symptom education, fetal monitoring,
Strict bed rest and salt restriction as primary therapy as initial therapy
Start magnesium sulfate now for seizure prophylaxis as initial therapy
Immediate delivery at 29 weeks as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Gestational hypertension without severe features is managed expectantly with close outpatient surveillance: serial blood pressure measurements, education about preeclampsia warning symptoms, antenatal fetal testing, and serial labs to detect progression to preeclampsia. Delivery at 29 weeks is not indicated for an asymptomatic patient without severe features; delivery is typically planned around 37 weeks. Bed rest and salt restriction are not effective therapies and bed rest carries thromboembolic risk. Magnesium sulfate is reserved for preeclampsia with severe features or eclampsia for seizure prophylaxis, not uncomplicated gestational hypertension.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: In the absence of progression to severe disease, when is delivery generally recommended for gestational hypertension?
options: At 37 weeks (37w0d)
At 40 weeks based on current risk
Only after spontaneous labor at any gestation
At 34 weeks based on current risk
stages.explanation: For gestational hypertension without severe features, delivery is generally recommended at 37 weeks (37w0d), balancing the risks of prematurity against the maternal-fetal risks of continuing the pregnancy with hypertensive disease. Delivery at 34 weeks would be appropriate only if severe features develop. Awaiting 40 weeks or relying solely on spontaneous labor increases the risk of progression to preeclampsia, abruption, and stillbirth. If severe features develop earlier, delivery timing is moved up accordingly (around 34 weeks with severe features), and stabilization with magnesium and antihypertensives is added.

pearls: Gestational hypertension: new BP greater than or equal to 140/90 after 20 weeks, no proteinuria, no severe features — confirm on two readings at least 4 hours apart.
Chronic hypertension is present before 20 weeks (or persists beyond 12 weeks postpartum); preeclampsia adds proteinuria or end-organ involvement.
Up to 25% of gestational hypertension progresses to preeclampsia, so serial labs and symptom surveillance are essential.
Deliver gestational hypertension without severe features at 37 weeks; move up to ~34 weeks if severe features develop.

cases.title: Abnormal Glucose Screen at a Routine Prenatal Visit
cases.chiefComplaint: A 33-year-old pregnant woman returns to discuss an abnormal glucose screening test.
cases.history: A 33-year-old G2P1 at 26 weeks returns to the prenatal clinic after a routine 50-gram 1-hour glucose challenge test (glucola) drawn at 25 weeks resulted at 162 mg/dL, above the screening threshold. She feels well and denies polyuria, polydipsia, or blurred vision. Her first pregnancy ended in a term delivery of a 4,200-gram infant, and she recalls being told her 'sugar was a little high' but does not think she was formally treated. Her pre-pregnancy BMI was 31 kg/m². PMH is otherwise unremarkable. Medications are a prenatal vitamin only; she has no allergies. She does not smoke, drink, or use drugs. Family history is significant for type 2 diabetes in both her mother and a maternal aunt. She works a desk job and reports limited physical activity and a diet high in refined carbohydrates. She has no personal history of polycystic ovary syndrome or prior gestational diabetes that was formally diagnosed. Fetal movement is normal.
cases.exam: The patient is well-appearing and in no distress, with a body habitus consistent with obesity. Fundal height measures slightly larger than dates at 28 cm. The uterus is soft and nontender. Cardiopulmonary exam is normal. There is no acanthosis nigricans noted at the neck on this visit, and no peripheral edema. There is no costovertebral angle tenderness. Fetal heart tones are 148 bpm and reactive. The remainder of the general physical exam is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given a 1-hour 50-gram glucola of 162 mg/dL, what is the most appropriate next step?
options: Proceed to a diagnostic 100-gram 3-hour oral glucose tolerance test
Repeat the 1-hour 50-gram test in two weeks as the first step today
Order a hemoglobin A1c and treat only if it exceeds 6.5%
Diagnose gestational diabetes and start insulin immediately
stages.explanation: The 50-gram 1-hour glucola is a screening test; a value above the threshold (commonly 130-140 mg/dL) is abnormal but not diagnostic. The correct next step (two-step approach) is the diagnostic 100-gram 3-hour oral glucose tolerance test. A single abnormal screen does not by itself diagnose GDM, so starting insulin immediately is premature. Repeating the same screening test does not provide diagnostic information. Hemoglobin A1c is not used to diagnose gestational diabetes because pregnancy alters red-cell turnover and A1c lacks sensitivity for the postprandial hyperglycemia of GDM. (Note: a 1-hour value greater than or equal to ~200 mg/dL is sometimes treated as diagnostic, but 162 warrants the confirmatory 3-hour test.)

stages.label: 3-HOUR OGTT RESULTS
stages.content:
100-gram 3-hour oral glucose tolerance test (Carpenter-Coustan thresholds):
Fasting 98 mg/dL (threshold 95)
1-hour 192 mg/dL (threshold 180)
2-hour 168 mg/dL (threshold 155)
3-hour 130 mg/dL (threshold 140)

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Using Carpenter-Coustan criteria, how do you interpret this 3-hour OGTT?
options: Normal, because the 3-hour value is below threshold as the primary diagnosis
Gestational diabetes mellitus, because two or more values meet or exceed the thresholds
Impaired glucose tolerance only, requiring no treatment as the primary diagnosis
Overt (pre-gestational) type 2 diabetes as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Gestational diabetes is diagnosed by the 3-hour OGTT when two or more values meet or exceed the thresholds. Here, the fasting (98 ≥ 95), 1-hour (192 ≥ 180), and 2-hour (168 ≥ 155) values are all elevated — three abnormal values — confirming GDM. It is not normal simply because the 3-hour value is below its threshold; only one normal value among four does not exclude the diagnosis. 'Impaired glucose tolerance requiring no treatment' is incorrect; meeting criteria mandates management. Overt/pre-gestational diabetes is suggested by very high fasting glucose or an early-pregnancy A1c greater than or equal to 6.5%, which is not demonstrated here.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the appropriate first-line management after diagnosing gestational diabetes?
options: No intervention; recheck glucose postpartum only as initial therapy
Begin a sulfonylurea as first-line and avoid dietary changes as initial therapy
Start basal-bolus insulin immediately for all patients as initial therapy
Medical nutrition therapy
stages.explanation: First-line management of GDM is medical nutrition therapy (carbohydrate-controlled diet), physical activity, and self-monitoring of fasting and postprandial blood glucose. If glycemic targets (commonly fasting less than 95, 1-hour postprandial less than 140, or 2-hour less than 120 mg/dL) are not met with lifestyle measures, pharmacotherapy is added, with insulin considered the preferred agent. Starting insulin in every patient is unnecessary, as many achieve control with diet alone. A sulfonylurea is not first-line and dietary therapy should never be skipped. Doing nothing until postpartum ignores the fetal and maternal risks (macrosomia, shoulder dystocia, neonatal hypoglycemia, preeclampsia) of untreated GDM.

stages.label: FOLLOW-UP
stages.question: What postpartum recommendation is essential for this patient?
options: Lifelong daily insulin regardless of postpartum glucose values as the immediate next step
Immediate repeat 3-hour OGTT on postpartum day 1 as the immediate next step
A 75-gram 2-hour oral glucose tolerance test at 4-12 weeks postpartum to screen for persistent diabetes
No further glucose testing is needed once delivered as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Women with GDM are at elevated lifetime risk of type 2 diabetes, so a 75-gram 2-hour OGTT (or fasting glucose/A1c per guideline) at 4-12 weeks postpartum is recommended to detect persistent diabetes or prediabetes, with periodic screening thereafter. Saying no testing is needed misses this important opportunity for early detection. Lifelong insulin 'regardless of values' is incorrect — GDM typically resolves after delivery, and management depends on postpartum results. An OGTT on postpartum day 1 is too early; glucose tolerance is assessed several weeks out once the physiologic changes of pregnancy have resolved.

pearls: Two-step screening: a 50-g 1-hour glucola above threshold (130-140 mg/dL) is followed by the diagnostic 100-g 3-hour OGTT; GDM requires 2 or more abnormal values.
Hemoglobin A1c is not used to diagnose GDM; an early-pregnancy A1c greater than or equal to 6.5% instead suggests overt/pre-existing diabetes.
First-line therapy is diet and exercise with glucose self-monitoring; add insulin (preferred) when targets are not met.
Screen for persistent diabetes with a 75-g 2-hour OGTT at 4-12 weeks postpartum; GDM markedly raises lifetime type 2 diabetes risk.

cases.title: Painful Vaginal Bleeding After a Fall in Late Pregnancy
cases.chiefComplaint: A 29-year-old pregnant woman presents with vaginal bleeding and abdominal pain after a fall.
cases.history: A 29-year-old G3P2 at 33 weeks presents to the ED with vaginal bleeding and constant lower abdominal pain that began about an hour after she slipped and fell onto her abdomen on a wet floor at home. She describes the bleeding as dark red and moderate in amount, and the pain as severe, constant, and worsening, unlike the intermittent tightening she felt with prior labors. She reports decreased fetal movement since the fall. Her two prior pregnancies were uncomplicated vaginal deliveries. PMH is notable for chronic hypertension treated with labetalol 200 mg twice daily. She has no allergies. She smokes about half a pack of cigarettes per day and denies alcohol or drug use, though she admits to occasional cocaine use in the past. Family history is noncontributory. She has had routine prenatal care; a 20-week ultrasound documented a normally located, posterior placenta. She is anxious and reports the pain is now nearly constant.
cases.exam: The patient is anxious and in pain. The abdomen reveals a firm, rigid, exquisitely tender uterus that does not relax between palpations ('woody' consistency). Fundal height is slightly larger than expected. There is dark red blood at the introitus on external inspection; a sterile speculum exam shows blood coming from the cervical os with a closed cervix. No digital exam is performed until placental location is reconsidered, but prior ultrasound confirmed a non-previa placenta. Fetal heart tones are detected at 110 bpm with reduced variability on the monitor. Skin is cool. There is no fluid leakage consistent with rupture of membranes.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: While establishing IV access and sending labs, what is the most likely diagnosis driving your management?
options: Bloody show of normal labor
Placental abruption
Placenta previa
Uterine rupture of a prior scar
stages.explanation: Painful third-trimester bleeding with a rigid, tender, non-relaxing ('woody') uterus following abdominal trauma is the classic presentation of placental abruption, especially with her risk factors (chronic hypertension, smoking, cocaine use, trauma). Placenta previa causes painless bleeding and was excluded by a documented non-previa placenta on ultrasound. Uterine rupture is a consideration with abdominal pain but typically occurs in patients with a prior uterine scar (she had vaginal deliveries, no cesarean) and often presents with loss of fetal station and a change in uterine contour. Bloody show is a small amount of blood-tinged mucus, not moderate dark bleeding with a tense, painful uterus and fetal distress.

stages.label: LABS & MONITORING
stages.content:
Hemoglobin 9.8 g/dL (baseline 11.6)
Platelets 110 ×10³/µL
Fibrinogen 160 mg/dL
INR 1.4
Type and screen: O positive
Fetal monitor: baseline 110 bpm, recurrent late decelerations, minimal variability
Tocometer: elevated baseline tone with frequent low-amplitude contractions

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: The fetal tracing shows recurrent late decelerations and the fibrinogen is falling. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Discharge home with pelvic rest after the bleeding slows as initial therapy
Administer betamethasone and observe for 48 hours before any delivery
Proceed to emergent cesarean delivery with maternal resuscitation
Perform amniotomy to augment labor for a rapid vaginal delivery as initial therapy
stages.explanation: A non-reassuring fetal tracing (recurrent late decelerations, minimal variability) with evidence of significant abruption and developing coagulopathy (falling fibrinogen, rising INR, dropping hemoglobin) mandates emergent delivery — cesarean is appropriate given fetal compromise and an unstable situation. Maternal resuscitation with blood products (including cryoprecipitate/fibrinogen for DIC) proceeds in parallel. Waiting 48 hours for steroids is inappropriate when the fetus is compromised and the mother is hemorrhaging. Amniotomy to pursue vaginal delivery is too slow and risky with fetal distress and coagulopathy. Discharge is dangerous; this is a life-threatening obstetric emergency for both mother and fetus.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: The laboratory results reflect which serious systemic complication of severe abruption?
options: Hereditary thrombophilia
Iron deficiency anemia alone
Immune thrombocytopenic purpura
Disseminated intravascular coagulation
stages.explanation: The combination of thrombocytopenia, a falling fibrinogen, and a prolonged INR in the setting of a large abruption indicates disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC). Abruption releases tissue factor (thromboplastin) into the maternal circulation, triggering consumptive coagulopathy; abruption is the most common cause of DIC in pregnancy. Immune thrombocytopenic purpura causes isolated low platelets with normal fibrinogen and coagulation studies. Iron deficiency anemia does not cause hypofibrinogenemia or coagulopathy. Hereditary thrombophilia predisposes to clotting, not to an acute consumptive bleeding picture. Management of the DIC centers on delivery, transfusion of red cells, fresh frozen plasma, platelets, and fibrinogen replacement (cryoprecipitate).

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After delivery and resuscitation, which additional intervention should be confirmed for this Rh-status assessment?
options: No additional testing is required since she is O positive with appropriate follow-up
Confirm she is Rh-positive, so anti-D immune globulin is not indicated,
Administer anti-D immune globulin because she is Rh-negative with appropriate follow-up
Administer high-dose corticosteroids to the mother to treat the coagulopathy based on current risk
stages.explanation: Her type and screen shows O positive, meaning she is Rh(D)-positive, so anti-D immune globulin (RhoGAM) is not indicated — that prophylaxis is for Rh-negative mothers after a sensitizing event such as abruption. The key ongoing concern after a large abruption and DIC is postpartum hemorrhage and continued correction of coagulopathy, so monitoring uterine tone, bleeding, and coagulation parameters is essential. Saying 'no testing required' ignores the need for ongoing hemorrhage surveillance. Administering anti-D would be wrong in an Rh-positive patient. Corticosteroids do not treat consumptive coagulopathy; the treatment is delivery and blood product replacement.

pearls: Abruption = painful third-trimester bleeding with a firm, tender, hypertonic uterus; risk factors include hypertension, trauma, cocaine, smoking, and prior abruption.
Bleeding may be concealed behind the placenta, so the degree of vaginal bleeding can underestimate blood loss and maternal compromise.
Abruption is the most common cause of DIC in pregnancy — watch the fibrinogen, which falls before other coagulation tests change.
Management depends on maternal and fetal stability: emergent (often cesarean) delivery for fetal distress or coagulopathy, with aggressive blood product resuscitation.

cases.title: Painless Bright-Red Bleeding at 30 Weeks
cases.chiefComplaint: A 34-year-old pregnant woman presents with sudden painless vaginal bleeding.
cases.history: A 34-year-old G3P2 at 30 weeks presents to L&D with a sudden gush of bright-red vaginal blood that began while she was resting. The bleeding soaked a pad but is now slowing. She has no abdominal pain and denies contractions, and reports normal fetal movement. She has had no trauma or recent intercourse. Her two prior children were delivered by cesarean section. PMH is otherwise unremarkable. Medications are a prenatal vitamin and iron; she has no allergies. She does not smoke, drink, or use drugs. Family history is noncontributory. She missed her anatomy ultrasound due to insurance issues and has not had imaging since the first trimester. She is hemodynamically stable on arrival and is more frightened than uncomfortable. There is no leakage of fluid suggesting ruptured membranes. She reports one small episode of spotting two weeks ago that resolved on its own and was not evaluated.
cases.exam: The patient is comfortable and in no distress. The abdomen is soft and nontender, with the uterus relaxed and not irritable; fundal height is appropriate for 30 weeks. There is bright-red blood on the perineal pad. A gentle sterile speculum exam (performed only after ultrasound) would be considered, but on initial assessment external inspection shows active bright-red bleeding from the vagina without clots of tissue. Fetal heart tones are 144 bpm and reactive on the monitor, with no contractions on tocometry. The cervix is not examined digitally. Extremities are warm and well perfused, and there are no signs of hemodynamic instability.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Before performing any cervical examination in this patient with painless bleeding, what is the most important next step?
options: Immediate digital cervical exam to assess dilation
Transabdominal/transvaginal ultrasound to localize the placenta
Immediate manual exploration of the uterus in this clinical context
Oxytocin augmentation to expedite delivery in this clinical context
stages.explanation: Painless bright-red third-trimester bleeding suggests placenta previa, and a digital cervical exam can provoke catastrophic hemorrhage if the placenta covers the os. Therefore ultrasound to localize the placenta must be done BEFORE any digital exam — this is the core teaching point. A digital exam first is exactly what is contraindicated. Oxytocin augmentation is inappropriate without a diagnosis and could worsen bleeding. Manual uterine exploration is not indicated and would be dangerous. Transvaginal ultrasound is safe and is actually the most accurate way to localize a low or covering placenta; the probe does not need to contact the cervix.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
Transvaginal ultrasound: placenta completely covering the internal cervical os (complete placenta previa)
Estimated fetal weight: 1,500 g, appropriate for gestational age
Amniotic fluid index: normal
No retroplacental clot or hematoma identified
Cervical length 3.2 cm, os closed
Fetal monitor: category I tracing

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Why is a digital cervical examination contraindicated in this patient?
options: It would introduce infection and cause chorioamnionitis
It is uncomfortable for the patient as the primary diagnosis
It could disrupt the placenta overlying the cervix
It could rupture the membranes prematurely as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: With a complete placenta previa, the placenta lies over the internal os; a digital exam can dislodge or tear placental tissue and provoke life-threatening hemorrhage. This is the central reason the digital exam is avoided until previa is excluded by ultrasound. Premature rupture of membranes is a general concern with exams but is not the specific catastrophic risk here. Patient discomfort is not the reason a clinically necessary exam would be withheld. While any instrumentation carries some infection risk, infection/chorioamnionitis is not the primary reason the digital exam is contraindicated in previa — the dominant danger is torrential bleeding.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: The bleeding has stopped, the mother is stable, and the fetus is reassuring at 30 weeks. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Discharge home immediately with no restrictions as initial therapy
Immediate cesarean delivery now as initial therapy
Induction of labor for vaginal delivery as initial therapy
Expectant inpatient management with betamethasone for fetal lung maturity, pelvic rest,
stages.explanation: In a stable mother with a reassuring preterm fetus and bleeding that has stopped, the goal is to prolong the pregnancy safely. This means expectant management (often inpatient after a sentinel bleed), administration of betamethasone for fetal lung maturity at this gestation, pelvic rest, and a planned cesarean delivery later (typically around 36-37 weeks, or sooner if recurrent hemorrhage). Immediate cesarean is not needed when both mother and fetus are stable and the fetus is very preterm. Vaginal delivery is contraindicated with complete previa — cesarean is mandatory. Discharge with no restrictions is unsafe after a significant sentinel bleed from a complete previa.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Given her two prior cesareans and a placenta now covering the os, what additional condition must be actively evaluated?
options: Ectopic pregnancy
Vasa previa only
Placenta accreta spectrum
Gestational trophoblastic disease
stages.explanation: A placenta previa overlying prior cesarean scars markedly increases the risk of placenta accreta spectrum (abnormal placental invasion into the myometrium). She should undergo detailed ultrasound (and possibly MRI) to evaluate for accreta and be managed at a center prepared for massive hemorrhage and possible cesarean hysterectomy. Gestational trophoblastic disease presents earlier with markedly elevated hCG and a characteristic 'snowstorm' appearance, not a normal third-trimester fetus. Vasa previa involves fetal vessels crossing the os and is a separate (though serious) entity; it is not the principal concern given her scarred uterus and covering placenta. Ectopic pregnancy is a first-trimester diagnosis and irrelevant at 30 weeks with an intrauterine fetus.

pearls: Placenta previa = painless, bright-red third-trimester bleeding; placental abruption is painful with a tense uterus.
Localize the placenta with ultrasound BEFORE any digital cervical exam — a digital exam over a previa can cause catastrophic hemorrhage.
Complete previa mandates cesarean delivery; plan delivery around 36-37 weeks (sooner if recurrent bleeding) with steroids if preterm.
Previa plus prior cesarean scars sharply raises the risk of placenta accreta spectrum — evaluate proactively and deliver at a prepared center.

cases.title: Previa Over an Old Cesarean Scar in the Operating Room
cases.chiefComplaint: A 38-year-old pregnant woman with multiple prior cesareans and a low-lying placenta presents for planned delivery.
cases.history: A 38-year-old G4P3 at 35 weeks 4 days is taken to the operating room for a planned delivery after antenatal imaging raised concern for abnormal placentation. She has had three prior cesarean sections. Recent ultrasound showed a complete anterior placenta previa overlying the prior uterine scar with loss of the retroplacental clear zone, increased placental lacunae ('Swiss cheese' appearance), and bridging vessels; MRI corroborated probable myometrial invasion. She has had two episodes of painless bleeding in the past three weeks, both self-limited, prompting inpatient admission. PMH is otherwise unremarkable. Medications include a prenatal vitamin and iron; she received betamethasone. She has no allergies. She does not smoke, drink, or use drugs. Family history is noncontributory. She has been counseled extensively about the likelihood of cesarean hysterectomy and massive transfusion. Two large-bore IVs are placed, blood products are cross-matched, and interventional radiology and gynecologic oncology are on standby.
cases.exam: The patient is stable in the operating room with regional then likely general anesthesia planned. The abdomen reveals a Pfannenstiel scar from prior cesareans. The gravid uterus is nontender. There is no active vaginal bleeding at this moment. Fetal heart tones are 146 bpm. On ultrasound at the bedside, the placenta is anterior and covers the internal os, with numerous vascular lacunae and absence of the normal hypoechoic myometrial-placental interface. Distended bridging vessels are seen extending toward the bladder serosa. Peripheral perfusion is normal, and there are no current signs of hemorrhage or hemodynamic instability.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which element of this patient's history most strongly raised antenatal suspicion for abnormal placentation and prompted this multidisciplinary planning?
options: Her use of iron supplementation as the first step today
The combination of a placenta previa overlying multiple prior cesarean scars
A single prior episode of first-trimester nausea as the first step today
Her maternal age of 38 years alone as the first step today
stages.explanation: The single most powerful clinical predictor of placenta accreta spectrum is a placenta previa lying over one or more prior cesarean scars, with risk rising steeply as the number of prior cesareans increases — exactly this patient's situation (three prior cesareans plus a covering anterior previa). Recognizing this combination antenatally is what triggers detailed imaging and assembly of a multidisciplinary team. Advanced maternal age is a minor, nonspecific risk factor and not the driver here. Iron supplementation is routine and unrelated to placental invasion. First-trimester nausea is a normal pregnancy symptom with no bearing on placentation. Early identification allows planned delivery at a prepared center, which improves outcomes.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which diagnosis is most strongly suggested by previa over a prior cesarean scar with lacunae and loss of the clear zone?
options: Placenta accreta spectrum
Uterine fibroid degeneration
Chorioamnionitis in this clinical context
Simple placenta previa without invasion
stages.explanation: Anterior previa over prior cesarean scars with sonographic loss of the retroplacental hypoechoic zone, multiple placental lacunae, and bridging vessels toward the bladder are hallmark features of placenta accreta spectrum (abnormally adherent/invasive placenta). The number of prior cesareans plus a covering previa is the strongest clinical risk factor. Simple previa without invasion would retain the normal myometrial-placental interface and lack lacunae and bridging vessels. Fibroid degeneration causes focal pain and a heterogeneous myometrial mass, not these placental findings. Chorioamnionitis is an infectious diagnosis (fever, uterine tenderness, maternal/fetal tachycardia) and does not produce these imaging features.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the recommended delivery plan for a patient with suspected placenta accreta spectrum?
options: Cesarean delivery with aggressive manual removal of the placenta as initial therapy
Expectant management at home until spontaneous labor as initial therapy
Attempt vaginal delivery with active management of the third stage as initial therapy
Cesarean delivery with the placenta left in situ
stages.explanation: The recommended approach for placenta accreta spectrum is a scheduled cesarean delivery (often via a hysterotomy that avoids the placenta) with the placenta LEFT IN SITU, followed by planned cesarean hysterectomy, performed at a center with a multidisciplinary team and massive transfusion capability. Attempting to forcibly remove an adherent placenta provokes torrential, often uncontrollable hemorrhage — this is why aggressive manual removal is wrong. Vaginal delivery is contraindicated with a covering previa and invasive placenta. Expectant management at home is dangerous given the high risk of catastrophic bleeding; these patients require planned, hospital-based, team-managed delivery.

stages.label: INTRAOPERATIVE FINDINGS
stages.content:
Estimated blood loss climbing rapidly after hysterotomy and delivery of the infant
Uterus: placenta densely invading the lower segment with extension toward the bladder serosa
Vital signs: BP 88/52 mmHg, HR 128 bpm
Labs: Hemoglobin 7.2 g/dL, fibrinogen 140 mg/dL, platelets 95 ×10³/µL
Massive transfusion protocol activated

stages.label: INTRAOPERATIVE MANAGEMENT
stages.question: With brisk hemorrhage and developing coagulopathy, what is the most appropriate transfusion strategy?
options: Packed red cells only, withholding plasma and platelets as the immediate next step
Withhold all blood products and give tranexamic acid alone as the immediate next step
Crystalloid resuscitation alone until the bleeding stops as the immediate next step
Balanced massive transfusion with fibrinogen replacement
stages.explanation: Massive obstetric hemorrhage with coagulopathy is managed with a balanced massive transfusion protocol delivering red cells, plasma, and platelets in a fixed ratio (e.g., 1:1:1), plus fibrinogen replacement (cryoprecipitate or fibrinogen concentrate, targeting greater than 150-200 mg/dL) and monitoring of ionized calcium, since citrate chelates calcium. Crystalloid alone causes dilutional coagulopathy and worsens bleeding. Giving red cells only, without plasma and platelets, fails to correct the coagulopathy. Tranexamic acid is a helpful adjunct (best given early) but is not a substitute for blood products in a patient already losing large volumes and becoming coagulopathic — products are essential.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: What is the most appropriate counseling point for this patient regarding future fertility after this delivery?
options: She can attempt another pregnancy in the same uterus next year with appropriate follow-up
Cesarean hysterectomy is often required
She should pursue uterine artery embolization instead of any surgery to preserve the uterus in all cases
Future pregnancies carry no increased risk with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: Cesarean hysterectomy is frequently the definitive, life-saving treatment for placenta accreta spectrum and permanently ends fertility, which is why thorough antenatal counseling and consent are essential before delivery. Telling her she can use the same uterus next year is incorrect once a hysterectomy has been performed. Future pregnancies do not carry 'no increased risk' — accreta itself reflects prior uterine surgery and the recurrence/complication risk is significant. While uterine-conserving approaches (leaving the placenta in situ, embolization, or focal resection) exist for highly selected stable cases, they are not universally appropriate and do not replace hysterectomy 'in all cases,' especially with active massive hemorrhage as seen here.

pearls: The biggest risk factor for accreta is placenta previa over one or more prior cesarean scars; risk rises with each additional cesarean.
Sonographic clues: loss of the retroplacental clear zone, placental lacunae ('Swiss cheese'), bridging vessels, and bladder-wall interruption.
Plan a scheduled cesarean with the placenta left in situ and cesarean hysterectomy at a center with a multidisciplinary team and massive transfusion capability.
Resuscitate with balanced blood products (e.g., 1:1:1), replace fibrinogen, give early tranexamic acid, and monitor ionized calcium during massive transfusion.

cases.title: Heavy Bleeding Just After Delivery
cases.chiefComplaint: A 27-year-old woman has heavy vaginal bleeding immediately after delivering her baby.
cases.history: A 27-year-old G1P1 has just delivered a 4,100-gram infant vaginally after a prolonged labor that included oxytocin augmentation for over 18 hours and a second stage requiring vacuum assistance. Within minutes of placental delivery she has brisk, continuous vaginal bleeding. The placenta appears intact on inspection. She received epidural anesthesia. PMH is unremarkable. Medications during labor were oxytocin and the epidural; she has no allergies. She does not smoke, drink, or use drugs. Family history is noncontributory. Risk factors of note include a macrosomic infant, prolonged and augmented labor, and operative vaginal delivery — all predisposing to uterine atony. There were no obvious extensive perineal lacerations on initial inspection, and she has no known bleeding disorder. Estimated blood loss is already approaching 1,000 mL within the first several minutes and continues. She is becoming lightheaded.
cases.exam: On bimanual examination the uterus is enlarged, soft, and 'boggy,' palpable above the umbilicus, and firms only transiently with massage before relaxing again. There is a steady flow of dark red blood from the vagina with some clots. Inspection of the cervix and vaginal walls reveals no significant lacerations, and the perineum is intact aside from a small first-degree tear that is not actively bleeding. The placenta and membranes appear complete on re-inspection, with no obvious retained fragments. The patient is pale and anxious; extremities are cool. There are no signs of expanding hematoma in the perineum or vulva.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given a boggy uterus and complete placenta, what is the most likely cause of this postpartum hemorrhage and the first intervention?
options: Retained placenta — proceed directly to manual extraction in this clinical context
Coagulopathy — transfuse fresh frozen plasma before anything else
Genital tract laceration — take immediately to the OR for repair
Uterine atony — begin bimanual uterine massage
stages.explanation: Uterine atony is the most common cause of postpartum hemorrhage (about 80%), and the boggy, poorly contracting uterus with an intact placenta and no lacerations points to it directly. The first interventions are bimanual uterine massage and ensuring uterotonics (oxytocin) are infusing, alongside IV access, fluids, and calling for help. Retained placenta is less likely because the placenta appears complete, so immediate manual extraction is not the first step. A genital tract laceration was excluded on careful inspection. Coagulopathy is possible with ongoing massive loss but is not the initial cause here; transfusing plasma first, before addressing atony, would not stop the bleeding.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Massage and oxytocin have not controlled the bleeding. Which sequence of additional uterotonics is appropriate, keeping her history in mind?
options: Carboprost first in a known asthmatic regardless of risk as initial therapy
Methylergonovine, but avoid it if she were hypertensive
Repeat the same dose of oxytocin every 5 minutes indefinitely without adding other agents
Methylergonovine in any patient including those with severe hypertension as initial therapy
stages.explanation: When oxytocin and massage fail, additional uterotonics are added in stepwise fashion with attention to contraindications: methylergonovine (an ergot) is contraindicated in hypertension; carboprost (15-methyl PGF2-alpha) is contraindicated in asthma; and misoprostol (a prostaglandin E1 analog) is a useful adjunct. The correct answer reflects choosing agents while respecting those contraindications. Giving carboprost to a known asthmatic 'regardless of risk' can precipitate bronchospasm. Giving methylergonovine to a severely hypertensive patient can cause dangerous vasoconstriction and stroke. Simply repeating oxytocin indefinitely without escalating is inadequate when bleeding continues and wastes critical time.

stages.label: RESPONSE & LABS
stages.content:
After massage, oxytocin, and a second uterotonic: uterus remains soft, bleeding continues
Estimated blood loss now ~1,800 mL
Hemoglobin 7.6 g/dL (from 12.0 antepartum)
Platelets 140 ×10³/µL, fibrinogen 240 mg/dL, INR 1.2
Heart rate 124 bpm, BP 96/58 mmHg
Two units of packed red cells started; tranexamic acid administered

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Medical management has failed and bleeding persists from atony. What is the most appropriate next intervention before laparotomy?
options: Observation with continued massage only in this setting
Intrauterine balloon tamponade (e.g., Bakri balloon)
Immediate emergency hysterectomy as the first escalation
Discharge to the postpartum floor with oral iron
stages.explanation: When uterotonics fail, the next step in the stepwise approach is a tamponade procedure — intrauterine balloon tamponade (such as a Bakri balloon) — which applies direct pressure to the uterine cavity and controls bleeding in many cases while resuscitation continues; uterine artery embolization is another option if available. Immediate hysterectomy is the definitive last resort, not the first escalation after medical therapy. Observation with massage alone is inadequate when bleeding persists and the patient is becoming unstable. Discharge is obviously inappropriate in a hemorrhaging, tachycardic, hypotensive patient. If tamponade and embolization fail, surgical options (uterine compression sutures, vessel ligation) and ultimately hysterectomy follow.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which factor in this patient's labor most strongly predisposed her to uterine atony?
options: Prolonged, oxytocin-augmented labor with a macrosomic infant causing an overdistended, fatigued uterus
Her age of 27 years with appropriate follow-up
Use of an epidural for analgesia with appropriate follow-up
Delivery of a single fetus with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: Uterine atony is promoted by anything that overdistends or exhausts the myometrium: a prolonged and oxytocin-augmented labor and a macrosomic (4,100 g) infant both fatigue and overstretch the uterus, impairing its ability to contract after delivery. Maternal age of 27 is unremarkable and not a major risk factor. Epidural analgesia provides pain control and is not a recognized cause of atony. A single fetus is the norm and is not a risk factor — by contrast, multiple gestation (which overdistends the uterus) would be. Recognizing these risk factors helps anticipate and prepare for postpartum hemorrhage.

pearls: Atony causes ~80% of postpartum hemorrhage; the classic finding is a soft, boggy, poorly contracting uterus with an intact placenta.
Stepwise management: massage and oxytocin, then methylergonovine (avoid in HTN), carboprost (avoid in asthma), misoprostol; add tranexamic acid early.
If medical therapy fails: balloon tamponade or embolization, then compression sutures/vessel ligation, with hysterectomy as the definitive last resort.
Risk factors include overdistension (macrosomia, multiple gestation, polyhydramnios) and a tired uterus (prolonged or augmented labor, chorioamnionitis).

cases.title: Abdominal Pain and a Positive Pregnancy Test with an Empty Uterus
cases.chiefComplaint: A 26-year-old woman presents with lower abdominal pain and a positive pregnancy test.
cases.history: A 26-year-old G2P1 presents to the ED with three days of progressively worsening right-sided lower abdominal pain and light vaginal spotting. Her last menstrual period was about 7 weeks ago, and a home pregnancy test was positive. The pain is sharp and intermittent, worse with movement, and she reports some right shoulder discomfort today. She has not yet had any prenatal care or ultrasound this pregnancy. Her obstetric history includes one prior term vaginal delivery. PMH is notable for chlamydial pelvic inflammatory disease treated two years ago and a prior episode of pelvic surgery for an ovarian cyst. She uses no regular contraception but had an intrauterine device removed several months ago. Medications: prenatal vitamin only; no allergies. She smokes half a pack per day and denies alcohol or drugs. Family history is noncontributory. She denies fever, urinary symptoms, or heavy bleeding, but feels lightheaded when standing.
cases.exam: The patient is uncomfortable and mildly tachycardic. The abdomen is soft with focal tenderness in the right lower quadrant and mild voluntary guarding, without rebound at rest. On pelvic exam there is a closed cervix with scant dark blood in the vault; there is right adnexal tenderness and a sense of fullness in the right adnexa, with cervical motion tenderness. The uterus is mildly enlarged but smaller than expected for 7 weeks. There is no frank peritonitis at this point. Bowel sounds are present. There is no costovertebral angle tenderness. She has mild pallor but is currently hemodynamically stable aside from borderline tachycardia.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What two initial investigations are most important in this woman with pain, spotting, and a positive pregnancy test?
options: A quantitative serum beta-hCG
A CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast and a urinalysis
A repeat urine pregnancy test and a plain abdominal radiograph
Serum CA-125 and a Pap smear as the first step today
stages.explanation: In a hemodynamically stable woman with first-trimester pain and bleeding, the essential studies are a quantitative serum beta-hCG and a transvaginal ultrasound to locate the pregnancy and assess for an ectopic. A repeat urine test adds nothing, and a plain radiograph exposes the pregnancy to radiation without useful information. CT is not first-line in early pregnancy due to radiation and because ultrasound is the diagnostic modality of choice for pregnancy location. CA-125 and a Pap smear are irrelevant to the acute evaluation of a possible ectopic pregnancy. The hCG value is interpreted alongside the ultrasound using the discriminatory zone.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Quantitative beta-hCG 3,200 mIU/mL
Transvaginal ultrasound: empty uterus (no intrauterine gestational sac)
Right adnexal mass measuring 3.0 cm with a tubal ring, separate from the ovary
Small amount of free fluid in the cul-de-sac
Hemoglobin 11.8 g/dL
Blood type: O negative

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: A beta-hCG of 3,200 mIU/mL with an empty uterus and an adnexal mass on transvaginal ultrasound is most consistent with what?
options: Ectopic pregnancy
A normal early intrauterine pregnancy too small to see
A complete molar pregnancy in this clinical context
A corpus luteum cyst of a normal pregnancy
stages.explanation: Above the discriminatory zone (a beta-hCG roughly 1,500-2,000 mIU/mL by transvaginal ultrasound, often up to 3,500 depending on the institution), an intrauterine pregnancy should be visible. With a beta-hCG of 3,200 and NO intrauterine sac but a right adnexal mass with a tubal ring and free fluid, ectopic pregnancy is the leading diagnosis. A normal IUP would generally be visible at this hCG level, making 'too small to see' unlikely. A complete mole typically shows a markedly elevated hCG with a 'snowstorm' intrauterine appearance, not an adnexal tubal ring. A corpus luteum cyst would be within or on the ovary, whereas this mass is described as separate from the ovary with a tubal ring.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: She is hemodynamically stable, the mass is 3 cm with no fetal cardiac activity, hCG is 3,200, and she has normal liver/renal function with no contraindications. Which management is appropriate?
options: Immediate exploratory laparotomy with hysterectomy
High-dose estrogen therapy to resolve the pregnancy
Expectant management with no follow-up as initial therapy
Methotrexate, since she meets criteria for medical management
stages.explanation: Methotrexate is appropriate for an unruptured ectopic in a reliable, hemodynamically stable patient with a relatively small mass, no fetal cardiac activity, an hCG below roughly 5,000 mIU/mL, and no contraindications (normal hepatic, renal, and hematologic function; no active pulmonary or immunodeficiency disease). She fits these criteria. Laparotomy with hysterectomy is far too aggressive for a stable, unruptured ectopic; surgery, when needed, is usually salpingostomy or salpingectomy via laparoscopy. Expectant management without follow-up is unsafe — even expectant approaches require strict serial hCG monitoring. Estrogen therapy plays no role in treating ectopic pregnancy. After methotrexate, hCG levels are followed to ensure they fall appropriately.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: While awaiting treatment she suddenly develops severe diffuse abdominal pain, becomes diaphoretic, and her BP falls to 80/48 mmHg with HR 130. What is the immediate management?
options: Emergent surgical intervention with resuscitation for a ruptured ectopic
Proceed with the planned outpatient methotrexate dose as the leading concern
Observe and recheck the hCG in 48 hours as the leading concern
Administer oral analgesics and discharge as the leading concern
stages.explanation: Sudden severe pain with hypotension and tachycardia signals tubal rupture with intraperitoneal hemorrhage — a surgical emergency. Management is immediate operative intervention (laparoscopy or laparotomy, often salpingectomy) with aggressive resuscitation and blood products, plus anti-D immune globulin given she is Rh-negative. Proceeding with outpatient methotrexate is contraindicated once rupture/instability occurs — medical management requires a stable patient. Observing and rechecking hCG in 48 hours would be fatal in active hemorrhagic shock. Oral analgesics and discharge are dangerously inappropriate. Hemodynamic instability or rupture is an absolute indication for surgery rather than methotrexate.

pearls: Suspect ectopic with first-trimester pain/bleeding; an hCG above the discriminatory zone (~1,500-2,000+ by TVUS) without an intrauterine sac is highly concerning.
Risk factors: prior PID/chlamydia, prior ectopic, tubal surgery, IUD in place, smoking, and assisted reproduction.
Methotrexate suits a stable, reliable patient with an unruptured small ectopic, no fetal cardiac activity, hCG below ~5,000, and no contraindications.
Hemodynamic instability or rupture mandates surgery (usually salpingectomy/salpingostomy); give anti-D immune globulin to Rh-negative patients.

cases.title: A Smooth, Movable Lump in a College Student
cases.chiefComplaint: A young woman presents after feeling a painless lump in her breast during a shower.
cases.history: A 22-year-old nulliparous woman presents to the breast clinic after noticing a lump in the upper outer quadrant of her left breast about six weeks ago while showering. She describes it as a firm, smooth marble that slides under her fingers. It is painless and has not visibly changed in size, though she feels it may be slightly more prominent just before her periods. She denies nipple discharge, skin changes, or breast pain. Her menarche was at age 12, and her cycles are regular every 28 days. She has never been pregnant and takes a combined oral contraceptive pill. She has no other medical history and takes no other medications. She has no known drug allergies. She drinks alcohol socially on weekends and does not smoke. Family history is negative for breast or ovarian cancer; her mother and two maternal aunts are healthy. She is a full-time student and is anxious because a friend was recently diagnosed with breast cancer.
cases.exam: The patient is well-appearing and in no distress. Inspection of both breasts shows symmetric contour with no skin dimpling, retraction, erythema, or peau d'orange, and no nipple inversion or discharge. In the upper outer quadrant of the left breast there is a single, well-circumscribed, firm but rubbery, smooth mass approximately 2 cm in diameter that is highly mobile and slips away under palpating fingers. It is nontender. There is no overlying skin change. The right breast is unremarkable. There is no axillary, supraclavicular, or cervical lymphadenopathy. The remainder of the exam is normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first-line imaging study to evaluate this palpable breast mass?
options: PET-CT of the chest
Breast ultrasound
Bilateral diagnostic mammography
Breast MRI with contrast
stages.explanation: In a woman under 30 with a palpable breast mass, ultrasound is the preferred initial imaging modality because dense fibroglandular tissue in young breasts limits mammographic sensitivity, and ultrasound reliably distinguishes solid from cystic lesions without ionizing radiation. Diagnostic mammography is first-line in women 30 and older or when ultrasound is indeterminate, but it is not preferred as the sole initial test in a 22-year-old. Breast MRI is reserved for high-risk screening, problem-solving, or staging known cancer, not for initial evaluation of a low-suspicion mass. PET-CT has no role in the workup of a primary palpable breast lump and would expose the patient to unnecessary radiation.

stages.label: ULTRASOUND FINDINGS
stages.content:
Breast ultrasound (left, upper outer quadrant):
Well-circumscribed, oval, homogeneously hypoechoic solid mass
Dimensions 2.1 x 1.4 x 1.8 cm
Wider-than-tall orientation, parallel to skin
Circumscribed margins, thin echogenic pseudocapsule
No posterior acoustic shadowing; mild posterior enhancement
No internal microcalcifications or vascularity on Doppler
BI-RADS category 3 (probably benign)
No axillary lymphadenopathy

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given the clinical and ultrasound findings, what is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Phyllodes tumor
Fibroadenoma
Simple breast cyst
Invasive ductal carcinoma
stages.explanation: A mobile, rubbery, well-circumscribed solid mass in a woman in her early 20s, with an oval, parallel, circumscribed hypoechoic appearance on ultrasound, is the classic picture of a fibroadenoma, the most common benign breast tumor in young women. Phyllodes tumors can look similar but typically present in the 40s, grow rapidly, and often become large; they are far less common. Invasive ductal carcinoma is rare at this age and usually appears as an irregular, taller-than-wide, spiculated hypoechoic mass with possible shadowing and nodal involvement. A simple cyst would be anechoic with posterior enhancement and imperceptible walls on ultrasound, not a solid hypoechoic mass.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: The mass has classic benign features and BI-RADS 3 characteristics. What is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Immediate surgical excisional biopsy as the immediate next step
Core needle biopsy of the mass as the immediate next step
Start tamoxifen to shrink the lesion as the immediate next step
Clinical and ultrasound surveillance with short-interval follow-up, with biopsy if it grows or features change
