stages.explanation: A small, classic-appearing probably-benign (BI-RADS 3) fibroadenoma in a young woman can be safely managed with short-interval clinical and imaging surveillance (typically ultrasound at 6, 12, and 24 months), reserving biopsy for lesions that enlarge, develop suspicious features, or cause patient concern. Immediate excisional biopsy is overtreatment for a low-risk lesion and leaves a scar. Core needle biopsy is reasonable if there is diagnostic uncertainty or the mass is large/growing, but it is not mandatory for a classic small fibroadenoma. Tamoxifen has no established role in shrinking fibroadenomas and exposes a young woman to unnecessary risks.

stages.label: PATIENT COUNSELING
stages.question: The patient asks whether having this lesion increases her chance of getting breast cancer. What is the most accurate counseling?
options: It will reliably transform into cancer within 10 years in this setting
It guarantees she will need prophylactic mastectomy in this setting
It is precancerous and should be removed to prevent malignancy
A simple fibroadenoma confers little to no increased breast cancer risk
stages.explanation: Simple fibroadenomas are benign and carry little to no increased risk of subsequent breast cancer; only complex fibroadenomas or those with adjacent proliferative changes confer a modest relative risk increase. Fibroadenomas are not precancerous lesions and do not require removal solely to prevent cancer. They do not reliably transform into malignancy; the classic teaching is that they tend to remain stable, shrink after menopause, or grow modestly under hormonal influence. There is no indication for prophylactic mastectomy based on a simple fibroadenoma, which would be drastic overtreatment and is not supported by any guideline.

pearls: Ultrasound is the first-line imaging test for a palpable breast mass in women under 30 because of dense fibroglandular tissue.
A mobile, rubbery, well-circumscribed, nontender mass in a young woman is the classic presentation of a fibroadenoma.
On ultrasound, fibroadenomas are oval, parallel (wider-than-tall), circumscribed, homogeneously hypoechoic masses (BI-RADS 3).
Simple fibroadenomas confer little to no increased breast cancer risk and can be followed with surveillance rather than excised.

cases.title: A Hard Lump with Skin Dimpling
cases.chiefComplaint: A postmenopausal woman presents with a firm breast lump and an area of skin pulling inward.
cases.history: A 57-year-old woman presents to the breast clinic after noticing a hard lump in her right breast about two months ago. She also reports that the skin over the area seems to pull inward, which she noticed when raising her arm in the mirror. The lump is painless and has not changed with her cycle, and she went through menopause at age 52. She denies nipple discharge but notes mild flattening of the right nipple over recent weeks. She has not had a mammogram in four years. Her menarche was at age 11 and she had her first child at age 33. She had no breastfeeding. PMH includes hypertension treated with lisinopril 20 mg daily and hyperlipidemia on atorvastatin 20 mg nightly. She took combined hormone replacement therapy for five years for menopausal symptoms, stopping last year. She has no known drug allergies. She drinks two glasses of wine most evenings and does not smoke. Her mother was diagnosed with breast cancer at age 64. She works as an accountant and is concerned about the changes.
cases.exam: The patient is well-appearing and anxious. Inspection of the right breast with the arms raised reveals an area of skin dimpling (tethering) in the upper outer quadrant and mild flattening of the right nipple compared with the left. There is no erythema, warmth, ulceration, or peau d'orange. Palpation reveals a hard, irregular, poorly mobile mass roughly 2.5 cm in the right upper outer quadrant that feels fixed to the surrounding tissue and is nontender. The left breast is normal. There is a single firm, 1 cm mobile right axillary lymph node; no supraclavicular or cervical nodes are palpable. The remainder of the exam, including lungs and abdomen, is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial diagnostic workup for this palpable, clinically suspicious mass?
options: Reassurance and repeat exam in 6 months
Excisional biopsy without prior imaging
Breast MRI alone as the first step today
Diagnostic mammography
stages.explanation: In a woman 30 or older with a clinically suspicious palpable mass, the standard initial workup is diagnostic mammography plus targeted ultrasound of the breast and the axilla; this triple-assessment imaging guides the subsequent biopsy. Reassurance and delayed follow-up is unsafe given the hard, fixed mass with skin dimpling and an axillary node, all features highly suspicious for malignancy. Breast MRI is an adjunct (e.g., for extent of disease or dense breasts) but is not the standalone first test. Excisional biopsy before imaging is inappropriate; tissue diagnosis should be obtained by image-guided core biopsy after imaging defines the lesion, preserving information needed for surgical planning.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
Diagnostic mammography (right breast):
Irregular high-density mass, 2.4 cm, upper outer quadrant
Spiculated margins with associated skin thickening and retraction
Clustered pleomorphic microcalcifications within the mass
BI-RADS category 5 (highly suggestive of malignancy)
Targeted ultrasound:
Irregular, taller-than-wide hypoechoic mass with posterior acoustic shadowing
One abnormal right axillary node, cortical thickening 5 mm, effaced fatty hilum

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next step to establish a tissue diagnosis?
options: Fine-needle aspiration cytology of the mass only as the primary diagnosis
Image-guided core needle biopsy of the breast mass
Proceed directly to mastectomy as the primary diagnosis
Repeat mammogram in 3 months to confirm growth as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: A BI-RADS 5 mass requires histologic confirmation by image-guided core needle biopsy, which provides architecture, grade, and receptor status (ER/PR/HER2). The abnormal axillary node should also be sampled (ultrasound-guided core or FNA) to document nodal involvement and guide staging and neoadjuvant decisions. FNA cytology of the mass alone cannot distinguish invasive from in situ disease and does not provide receptor status, so it is inadequate here. Repeating the mammogram in 3 months dangerously delays diagnosis of an obviously suspicious lesion. Proceeding directly to mastectomy without a tissue diagnosis is never appropriate, as it forfeits options like neoadjuvant therapy and breast conservation.

stages.label: PATHOLOGY
stages.content:
Core needle biopsy, right breast mass:
Invasive ductal carcinoma (no special type), Nottingham grade 2
ER positive (95%), PR positive (80%)
HER2 negative (IHC 1+)
Ki-67 18%
Axillary node core biopsy: metastatic carcinoma present

stages.label: STAGING
stages.question: Before deciding on definitive therapy, which additional evaluation is most appropriate for this biopsy-proven, node-positive invasive cancer?
options: Empiric whole-brain radiation as the immediate next step
Genetic karyotyping of peripheral blood as the immediate next step
No further workup; proceed straight to surgery as the immediate next step
Systemic staging assessment
stages.explanation: With clinically node-positive invasive breast cancer, systemic staging for distant metastases (CT chest/abdomen/pelvis plus bone scan, or PET-CT) is appropriate, and the case should be reviewed at a multidisciplinary tumor board to plan surgery, systemic therapy, and radiation sequencing. Proceeding straight to surgery skips staging and ignores the strong role for neoadjuvant chemotherapy in node-positive disease. Peripheral-blood karyotyping is not relevant; if hereditary risk is suspected, germline BRCA gene testing (not karyotyping) would be considered. Empiric whole-brain radiation is inappropriate without evidence of brain metastases and would expose her to unnecessary harm.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Staging shows no distant disease (cT2N1M0). Her tumor is ER/PR positive, HER2 negative. Which adjuvant systemic therapy is essential regardless of whether chemotherapy is given?
options: Trastuzumab as initial therapy
Combination antiretroviral therapy as initial therapy
Lifelong bisphosphonates as the only systemic therapy
Endocrine therapy for at least 5 years
stages.explanation: Hormone-receptor-positive breast cancer requires adjuvant endocrine therapy; in a postmenopausal woman an aromatase inhibitor (or tamoxifen) for at least 5 years substantially reduces recurrence and mortality and is given regardless of whether chemotherapy is also indicated. Trastuzumab targets HER2 and is not indicated because her tumor is HER2 negative. Antiretroviral therapy treats HIV and has no role in breast cancer. Bisphosphonates can reduce bone recurrence in some postmenopausal patients and protect bone density during AI therapy, but they are an adjunct, not a substitute for endocrine therapy, and would never be the sole systemic treatment for an ER-positive cancer.

cases.diagnosis: Invasive ductal carcinoma of the breast
pearls: Skin dimpling, nipple retraction, and a hard fixed mass are classic clinical red flags for invasive breast carcinoma.
A spiculated mass with pleomorphic microcalcifications on mammography is BI-RADS 5 and requires image-guided core biopsy.
Always sample a clinically or radiologically abnormal axillary node, as nodal status drives staging and neoadjuvant decisions.
ER/PR-positive, HER2-negative tumors require adjuvant endocrine therapy (aromatase inhibitor in postmenopausal women) regardless of chemotherapy.

cases.title: A Red, Swollen Breast That Antibiotics Won't Fix
cases.chiefComplaint: A woman presents with a rapidly enlarging, red, swollen breast that did not improve on antibiotics.
cases.history: A 49-year-old woman presents with three weeks of progressive redness, swelling, and heaviness of her left breast. Her primary care physician diagnosed mastitis and prescribed cephalexin, then amoxicillin-clavulanate, but the breast has continued to worsen, becoming larger, firmer, and dimpled like an orange peel. She reports no fever and is not breastfeeding; her last child was 15 years ago. She has noticed the skin feels warm and tight and that the nipple has begun to turn inward. There is no discrete lump she can identify, just diffuse firmness. She is perimenopausal with irregular cycles. PMH is unremarkable and she takes only a multivitamin. She has no known drug allergies. She has never smoked and drinks occasionally. Family history includes a maternal grandmother with breast cancer at age 70. She works as a nurse and is frustrated that two courses of antibiotics have failed. She denies weight loss, but feels generally fatigued.
cases.exam: The patient is afebrile and not toxic-appearing. The left breast is diffusely enlarged, erythematous, warm, and edematous, with prominent peau d'orange (skin pitting like an orange peel) involving more than one third of the breast. The skin is thickened and there is mild nipple retraction. No fluctuant abscess is palpable, and no discrete dominant mass is clearly defined within the diffusely indurated tissue. There is firm, matted left axillary lymphadenopathy. The right breast is normal. There is no supraclavicular adenopathy. Lungs are clear and the abdomen is soft and nontender. There is no fluctuance to suggest a drainable collection.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Two antibiotic courses have failed in this non-lactating perimenopausal woman with a rapidly progressive red, edematous breast. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Incision and drainage of the breast as the first step today
Promptly evaluate for malignancy with diagnostic mammography/ultrasound
Prescribe a third, broader-spectrum antibiotic course as the first step today
Reassure that resolution takes time and recheck in 6 weeks as the first step today
stages.explanation: A red, swollen breast with peau d'orange that fails to respond to antibiotics, especially in a non-lactating perimenopausal woman, must be evaluated urgently for inflammatory breast cancer. The correct step is diagnostic imaging plus tissue sampling including full-thickness skin punch biopsy (to capture dermal lymphatic invasion) and core biopsy of any underlying mass. Another antibiotic course only delays a potentially life-threatening diagnosis. Incision and drainage is wrong because there is no fluctuant abscess, and cutting into an undiagnosed cancer is harmful. Continued reassurance and watchful waiting is dangerous, as inflammatory breast cancer is aggressive and progresses rapidly, and any presumed mastitis not resolving within a short period mandates biopsy.

stages.label: IMAGING & PATHOLOGY
stages.content:
Diagnostic mammography (left): diffuse skin thickening, trabecular coarsening, global increased density; no discrete mass
Ultrasound: skin thickening up to 8 mm, dilated dermal lymphatics, enlarged matted axillary nodes
Skin punch biopsy: invasive carcinoma with tumor emboli in dermal lymphatics
Core biopsy of breast parenchyma: invasive ductal carcinoma, grade 3
Receptors: ER negative, PR negative, HER2 positive (IHC 3+)

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which finding most specifically establishes the diagnosis of inflammatory breast cancer rather than a simple infection or locally advanced non-inflammatory cancer?
options: Absence of fever as the primary diagnosis
Presence of axillary lymphadenopathy as the primary diagnosis
Tumor emboli within dermal lymphatics on skin biopsy in the setting of the clinical inflammatory picture
ER-negative receptor status as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Inflammatory breast cancer is a clinicopathologic diagnosis: the clinical hallmark is rapid-onset erythema and edema (peau d'orange) over at least one third of the breast, and the pathologic correlate is invasion of dermal lymphatics by tumor emboli, which causes the lymphedematous skin changes. Demonstrating dermal lymphatic tumor emboli on skin biopsy in this clinical context is what confirms IBC. Axillary lymphadenopathy is common in many breast cancers and is not specific. ER-negative status reflects an aggressive biology often seen in IBC but does not define it. Absence of fever helps distinguish it from infection clinically but is not diagnostic on its own.

stages.label: STAGING
stages.question: By definition, what is the minimum clinical T stage of inflammatory breast cancer, and what does this imply for staging workup?
options: It is always early-stage (stage I) and needs no systemic staging in this setting
It cannot be staged until after surgery as the immediate next step
It is staged purely by tumor size, like other cancers as the immediate next step
It is classified as at least T4d, is locally advanced,
stages.explanation: Inflammatory breast cancer is classified as T4d by definition because of the dermal involvement, making it at minimum stage III (locally advanced) and frequently stage IV at presentation due to its aggressive behavior. Therefore full systemic staging (CT chest/abdomen/pelvis and bone scan, or PET-CT) is mandatory to detect distant metastases before treatment. It is never early-stage by definition. It is not staged by tumor size alone, since the T category is driven by the inflammatory/dermal features (T4d) rather than measured diameter. Clinical staging is performed up front, before surgery, precisely because neoadjuvant systemic therapy is the cornerstone of treatment.

stages.label: STAGING RESULTS
stages.content:
PET-CT: hypermetabolic left breast and matted left axillary nodes
No distant metastatic uptake in liver, lung, or bone
Clinical stage: cT4d N2 M0 (stage IIIB)
LVEF on echocardiogram 60% (baseline for HER2-directed therapy)

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: For this non-metastatic, HER2-positive inflammatory breast cancer, what is the correct treatment sequence?
options: Endocrine therapy alone with no surgery as initial therapy
Neoadjuvant systemic therapy followed by modified radical mastectomy
Radiation alone to the breast as initial therapy
Upfront breast-conserving lumpectomy, then chemotherapy as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Inflammatory breast cancer is treated with a trimodality approach beginning with neoadjuvant systemic therapy; for HER2-positive disease this is chemotherapy combined with HER2-directed agents (trastuzumab and pertuzumab). This is followed by a modified radical mastectomy (not breast-conserving surgery, which is contraindicated in IBC) and then postmastectomy radiation. Upfront lumpectomy is contraindicated because IBC involves diffuse dermal lymphatics and breast conservation yields unacceptable local recurrence. Radiation alone or endocrine therapy alone is inadequate; her tumor is ER negative, so endocrine therapy would be ineffective, and neither modality addresses the systemic and locoregional disease burden of this aggressive cancer.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Because her regimen includes a HER2-targeted antibody, which toxicity must be monitored during treatment, and how?
options: Hyperthyroidism, monitored with TSH as the leading concern
Pulmonary fibrosis, monitored with spirometry as the leading concern
Cardiotoxicity , monitored with serial echocardiograms
Renal failure, monitored with weekly dialysis as the leading concern
stages.explanation: Trastuzumab (and other HER2-directed antibodies) can cause cardiotoxicity, characteristically a reversible decline in left ventricular ejection fraction, so a baseline echocardiogram and serial LVEF monitoring during therapy are required; this risk is increased when combined with anthracyclines. Pulmonary fibrosis monitored by spirometry relates more to agents like bleomycin, not HER2 antibodies. Renal failure requiring dialysis is not the characteristic trastuzumab toxicity and is not monitored this way. Hyperthyroidism is not a recognized trastuzumab effect; thyroid monitoring is relevant to certain immunotherapies, not HER2-directed antibodies. Recognizing cardiac surveillance with serial echocardiography is the key safety point for HER2-targeted therapy.

pearls: Any erythematous, edematous breast (peau d'orange) not resolving after a short antibiotic course must be biopsied to exclude inflammatory breast cancer.
IBC is a clinicopathologic diagnosis: rapid skin erythema/edema plus dermal lymphatic tumor emboli on skin punch biopsy.
Inflammatory breast cancer is staged as T4d (at least stage III) by definition and requires full systemic staging.
Treatment is trimodality: neoadjuvant systemic therapy first, then modified radical mastectomy (not lumpectomy), then radiation.

cases.title: A Scaly, Itchy Nipple That Won't Heal
cases.chiefComplaint: A woman is referred for a persistent scaly, crusting rash on her nipple that topical steroids did not cure.
cases.history: A 60-year-old woman is referred to the breast clinic for a six-month history of an eczematous, scaling, crusting lesion of her right nipple. It began as itching and mild redness and progressed to flaking, oozing, and intermittent bleeding. Her dermatologist diagnosed eczema and prescribed a medium-potency topical corticosteroid, which provided only transient relief; the lesion has persisted and slowly enlarged to involve the areola. She denies a palpable lump but has noticed occasional serosanguineous discharge from the nipple. She went through menopause at 50 and has no breast pain. PMH includes well-controlled type 2 diabetes on metformin 1000 mg twice daily and osteoarthritis. She takes no hormone therapy. She has no known drug allergies. She does not smoke and drinks rarely. Family history is unremarkable for breast or ovarian cancer. She is a retired librarian. She is frustrated that the rash has not healed and now worries because it keeps spreading and bleeding despite treatment.
cases.exam: The patient is well-appearing. Examination of the right breast reveals a unilateral, sharply demarcated, erythematous, scaling and crusted lesion centered on the nipple and extending onto the areola, with areas of erosion and serosanguineous crust. The nipple architecture is partly distorted but not frankly inverted. There is no surrounding cellulitis. No discrete mass is palpable in the right breast on careful examination, though the tissue beneath the nipple feels mildly thickened. The left breast and nipple are normal. There is no axillary or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy. The lesion involves the nipple first and spreads outward to the areola, in contrast to eczema which typically spares the nipple.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A unilateral, non-healing eczematous nipple lesion has failed topical steroids over months. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Prescribe topical antifungal cream as the first step today
Perform a full-thickness wedge or punch biopsy of the nipple lesion
Reassure and discharge as resolving eczema as the first step today
Switch to a high-potency topical steroid and reassess in 3 months
stages.explanation: A unilateral, persistent, eczematous nipple lesion that fails appropriate topical therapy must raise concern for Paget disease of the breast, and the definitive step is a biopsy of the nipple lesion (punch or wedge including epidermis) plus bilateral mammography to detect an underlying malignancy. Escalating to a stronger steroid only delays diagnosis of a potentially malignant process. Topical antifungal therapy is inappropriate because the lesion is not fungal and again delays diagnosis. Reassurance and discharge is unsafe; the classic teaching is that any unilateral nipple eczema unresponsive to a short trial of topical treatment warrants biopsy because Paget disease mimics benign dermatitis.

stages.label: BIOPSY & IMAGING
stages.content:
Nipple punch biopsy:
Large atypical epithelial cells with abundant pale cytoplasm and pleomorphic nuclei within the epidermis (Paget cells)
Immunohistochemistry: cells CK7 positive, HER2 positive
Mammography (right): segmental pleomorphic microcalcifications in the retroareolar region, no discrete mass
Ultrasound: subtle retroareolar ductal changes, no solid mass; no abnormal nodes

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The biopsy shows intraepidermal Paget cells. What does this finding most importantly imply about the rest of the breast?
options: It is a purely skin condition with no link to internal disease as the primary diagnosis
It indicates a benign nevus as the primary diagnosis
It is strongly associated with an underlying breast carcinoma , present in the large majority of cases
It is contagious and requires isolation as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Paget disease of the nipple represents epidermal involvement by malignant glandular (Paget) cells and is associated with an underlying breast carcinoma, most often ductal carcinoma in situ, and frequently an invasive ductal carcinoma, in the large majority of cases. Thus finding Paget cells mandates a thorough search of the underlying breast (mammography, often MRI) for the associated tumor. It is not merely a superficial skin condition; treating only the skin would miss the underlying cancer. It is not a benign nevus, given the atypical CK7-positive Paget cells. It is a malignancy, not an infection, and is in no way contagious.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Mammography shows retroareolar microcalcifications but no discrete mass, and physical exam reveals no palpable lump. Which additional imaging is most useful to define the extent of underlying disease?
options: Plain chest radiograph
DEXA bone densitometry
Abdominal ultrasound
Breast MRI
stages.explanation: When Paget disease is confirmed but mammography shows no discrete mass, breast MRI is the most useful additional study because it is highly sensitive for detecting and mapping occult underlying carcinoma and defining disease extent, which guides surgical planning (breast conservation vs mastectomy). A plain chest radiograph evaluates the lungs, not breast parenchyma, and is irrelevant here. Abdominal ultrasound evaluates intra-abdominal organs and has no role in defining breast disease extent. DEXA assesses bone mineral density and would only become relevant later if endocrine (aromatase inhibitor) therapy were planned, not for delineating the primary tumor.

stages.label: MRI FINDINGS
stages.content:
Breast MRI (right):
Nipple-areolar enhancement consistent with Paget involvement
Segmental clumped non-mass enhancement extending 4 cm from the nipple into the upper retroareolar breast
Findings consistent with extensive underlying ductal carcinoma in situ
No enlarged axillary nodes; left breast normal

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given Paget disease with extensive (4 cm segmental) underlying DCIS involving the nipple-areolar complex, what is the most appropriate surgical approach?
options: Mastectomy , given extensive disease involving the nipple-areolar complex
Topical chemotherapy cream to the nipple only as initial therapy
Simple nipple excision alone with no further treatment as initial therapy
Observation with serial photographs as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Paget disease with extensive underlying disease involving the nipple-areolar complex over a wide segment is best treated with mastectomy, because breast conservation would require removing the nipple-areolar complex and achieving clear margins across a large area, which is not feasible with 4 cm of disease and yields poor cosmesis and high recurrence. Sentinel node biopsy is considered, particularly if invasive disease is found or mastectomy is performed. Topical chemotherapy treats only the visible skin and ignores the underlying carcinoma. Nipple excision alone leaves residual segmental disease. Observation is unacceptable for a confirmed malignancy with extensive DCIS.

stages.label: KEY DISTINCTION
stages.question: A colleague asks how to clinically distinguish early Paget disease from benign nipple eczema. Which feature best favors Paget disease?
options: Bilateral involvement that resolves promptly with topical steroids as the immediate next step
Sparing of the nipple with disease confined to the areola as the immediate next step
Itching alone with no other findings and rapid response to emollients in this setting
Unilateral involvement beginning on the nipple
stages.explanation: Paget disease is classically unilateral and begins on the nipple, then spreads outward to the areola, and it persists or progresses despite appropriate topical steroid therapy, which is the key clinical clue to biopsy. Benign nipple eczema is typically bilateral, tends to start on the areola and spare the nipple, and usually responds to topical steroids or emollients. Bilateral involvement that resolves with steroids points to eczema, not Paget. Sparing of the nipple with areola-only disease is the eczema pattern. Itching alone resolving rapidly with emollients describes benign dermatitis. The nipple-first, unilateral, steroid-refractory pattern is the hallmark that should prompt biopsy for Paget disease.

pearls: Any unilateral, persistent eczematous nipple lesion that fails a short topical-steroid trial must be biopsied to exclude Paget disease.
Paget disease starts on the nipple and spreads to the areola, whereas benign eczema typically starts on the areola and spares the nipple.
Paget cells are large, pale, CK7-positive intraepidermal cells; the disease is associated with an underlying carcinoma (usually DCIS) in most cases.
Breast MRI helps map occult underlying disease when mammography shows no discrete mass, guiding the choice between conservation and mastectomy.

cases.title: Cyclic Lumpy Breasts and Premenstrual Pain
cases.chiefComplaint: A woman presents with bilateral breast pain and lumpiness that worsens before her periods.
cases.history: A 38-year-old woman presents with about a year of bilateral breast pain and a sense of generalized lumpiness. The discomfort is heaviest and most tender in the week before each period and improves substantially once menstruation begins. She feels multiple small, somewhat mobile lumps in both breasts, more pronounced in the upper outer quadrants, that seem to swell and recede with her cycle. She denies a single dominant fixed lump, nipple discharge, or skin changes. She has regular 29-day menstrual cycles. She has two children, both breastfed, and uses condoms for contraception. PMH is unremarkable; she takes no regular medications other than occasional ibuprofen and drinks several cups of coffee daily. She has no known drug allergies. She does not smoke and drinks alcohol occasionally. Family history is negative for breast or ovarian cancer. She works as a graphic designer. She is worried because the lumpiness and pain are bothersome and she fears cancer, though she notices the symptoms clearly track with her cycle.
cases.exam: The patient is well-appearing and comfortable. Both breasts show symmetric contour with no skin dimpling, retraction, erythema, or peau d'orange, and no nipple inversion or spontaneous discharge. Palpation reveals diffuse, bilateral nodularity that is most prominent in the upper outer quadrants, with several small, ill-defined, mildly tender areas of rope-like and granular thickening that are roughly symmetric between the two breasts. There is no discrete, hard, dominant or fixed mass. There is no axillary, supraclavicular, or cervical lymphadenopathy. The remainder of the examination is normal. The findings are consistent with the cyclic, bilateral pattern she describes.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given bilateral, cyclic breast pain and diffuse nodularity without a dominant mass in a 38-year-old, what is the most appropriate initial step?
options: PET-CT to screen for cancer as the first step today
Careful clinical breast exam
Immediate bilateral mastectomy as the first step today
Start chemotherapy empirically as the first step today
stages.explanation: Bilateral, cyclic breast pain and symmetric diffuse nodularity without a dominant mass are the hallmark of benign fibrocystic changes, and the appropriate initial step is a careful clinical breast exam plus reassurance, reserving targeted imaging (ultrasound or, given her age, mammography) for any discrete, dominant, or suspicious finding. Mastectomy is grossly inappropriate for a benign, bilateral, hormonally driven condition. Empiric chemotherapy is absurd without any diagnosis of malignancy. PET-CT has no role in evaluating diffuse benign breast symptoms and would cause needless radiation and false positives; imaging should be targeted and proportionate to clinical suspicion.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which feature most strongly supports a benign fibrocystic etiology rather than malignancy in this patient?
options: A fixed supraclavicular lymph node as the primary diagnosis
Bloody unilateral nipple discharge as the primary diagnosis
A single hard, fixed mass with overlying skin dimpling
Bilateral, symmetric nodularity
stages.explanation: The defining feature of fibrocystic change is bilateral, symmetric breast nodularity and tenderness that wax and wane with the menstrual cycle, reflecting hormonal influence on the breast stroma and ducts. A single hard, fixed mass with skin dimpling is a classic red flag for carcinoma, not fibrocystic disease. Bloody unilateral nipple discharge suggests an intraductal pathology such as papilloma or carcinoma and warrants workup, not reassurance. A fixed supraclavicular node raises concern for metastatic malignancy. None of these worrisome features are present here; the cyclic, bilateral, symmetric pattern is reassuring.

stages.label: TARGETED IMAGING
stages.content:
Bilateral breast ultrasound (for symptom reassurance):
Multiple small, simple anechoic cysts bilaterally, largest 1.0 cm
Thin imperceptible walls with posterior acoustic enhancement
Scattered areas of fibroglandular nodularity, no solid mass
No suspicious calcifications, no abnormal lymph nodes
BI-RADS category 2 (benign findings)

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Imaging confirms benign fibrocystic changes with simple cysts. Which conservative measures are most appropriate for symptom relief?
options: Long-term high-dose opioids as initial therapy
Supportive measures: a well-fitted supportive bra, NSAIDs,
Aspiration of every cyst routinely regardless of symptoms as initial therapy
Bilateral mastectomy to remove all cysts as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Fibrocystic change is benign and managed conservatively: a well-fitted supportive bra, NSAIDs for pain, and a trial of caffeine reduction or other lifestyle measures, together with reassurance, relieve symptoms in most women; some clinicians add a trial of evening primrose oil or, for refractory mastalgia, topical/oral agents. Mastectomy is never indicated for benign fibrocystic disease. Long-term high-dose opioids are inappropriate for a benign, self-limited condition and carry addiction risk. Routine aspiration of every cyst is unnecessary; aspiration is reserved for symptomatic or complex cysts, or to confirm a cyst is simple when imaging is indeterminate.

stages.label: PATIENT COUNSELING
stages.question: The patient asks if fibrocystic changes mean she will get breast cancer. What is the most accurate statement?
options: Fibrocystic changes always become cancer as the immediate next step
Non-proliferative fibrocystic changes do not meaningfully increase breast cancer risk
She should never have a mammogram again as the immediate next step
Cysts must be surgically removed to prevent cancer as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Most fibrocystic changes are non-proliferative and do not meaningfully increase breast cancer risk; risk increases only with specific proliferative findings on biopsy, particularly atypical ductal or lobular hyperplasia, and to a lesser degree proliferative lesions without atypia. Fibrocystic changes do not always become cancer, and the cyclic symmetric pattern is reassuring. She should continue age-appropriate screening mammography, not avoid it. Surgical removal of simple cysts is not required to prevent cancer; simple cysts are benign, and excision is reserved for symptomatic or suspicious lesions. Accurate, reassuring counseling about risk stratification is the key teaching point.

pearls: Bilateral, symmetric breast nodularity and tenderness that fluctuate with the menstrual cycle are the hallmark of benign fibrocystic change.
Reassurance plus a supportive bra, NSAIDs, and caffeine reduction relieves symptoms in most women; reserve imaging for discrete or suspicious findings.
Simple cysts on ultrasound (anechoic, thin-walled, posterior enhancement) are BI-RADS 2 and benign; aspirate only if symptomatic or complex.
Non-proliferative fibrocystic changes do not raise cancer risk; only proliferative lesions, especially atypical hyperplasia, increase it.

cases.title: Bleeding Again After Menopause
cases.chiefComplaint: A postmenopausal woman presents with new vaginal bleeding and a sensation of pelvic fullness.
cases.history: A 52-year-old woman presents with three months of intermittent vaginal bleeding. She completed menopause two years ago and had no bleeding until recently. She also reports vague lower abdominal fullness and notes that her breasts feel tender and fuller, which surprises her since she is postmenopausal. She denies fever, weight loss, or bowel changes. She is G2P2 with prior normal vaginal deliveries. PMH includes hypertension on amlodipine 5 mg daily; she takes no hormone therapy and is not on tamoxifen. She has no known drug allergies. She does not smoke and drinks wine occasionally. Family history is unremarkable for gynecologic cancers. She is a high school teacher. She is concerned because postmenopausal bleeding feels abnormal, and her primary care doctor noted her uterus felt bulky and possibly an adnexal fullness on a brief exam, prompting referral. She has not had a Pap test or pelvic ultrasound in several years.
cases.exam: The patient is well-appearing. The abdomen is soft with mild fullness in the lower pelvis but no guarding or rebound. On pelvic examination the vaginal mucosa appears well estrogenized (rugated, moist) rather than atrophic, which is unexpected in a postmenopausal woman. The cervix appears normal with a small amount of blood at the os. Bimanual exam reveals a mildly enlarged uterus and a palpable, firm, non-tender left adnexal mass approximately 8 cm. There is no cervical motion tenderness. The right adnexa is normal. Breast exam shows bilateral fullness and mild tenderness without a discrete mass. There is no inguinal or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A postmenopausal woman has new bleeding plus an adnexal mass and signs of estrogen excess. What is the most appropriate initial workup?
options: Transvaginal ultrasound to assess endometrial thickness
Begin empiric antibiotics for presumed endometritis as the first step today
Start hormone replacement therapy as the first step today
Reassure that postmenopausal bleeding is normal as the first step today
stages.explanation: Postmenopausal bleeding is abnormal until proven otherwise and mandates evaluation for endometrial pathology. With a concurrent adnexal mass and clinical estrogen excess, the appropriate workup is transvaginal ultrasound to measure endometrial thickness and characterize the mass, combined with endometrial biopsy to exclude hyperplasia or carcinoma. Reassurance is wrong because postmenopausal bleeding is never normal. Starting hormone replacement would worsen any estrogen-driven pathology and obscure the diagnosis. Empiric antibiotics for endometritis are inappropriate in an afebrile woman without infectious signs; the clinical picture points to an estrogen-secreting process, not infection, and would delay diagnosis of a potential malignancy.

stages.label: ULTRASOUND & BIOPSY
stages.content:
Transvaginal ultrasound:
Endometrial stripe thickened at 14 mm, heterogeneous
Left adnexal mass 8.2 cm, solid with cystic spaces, mild internal vascularity
Right ovary normal; no ascites
Endometrial biopsy: complex endometrial hyperplasia with focal atypia
Tumor markers: inhibin B markedly elevated; CA-125 mildly elevated; beta-hCG negative; AFP normal

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: An estrogen-secreting solid ovarian mass with elevated inhibin and concurrent endometrial hyperplasia in this age group most likely represents which tumor?
options: Serous cystadenocarcinoma
Mature cystic teratoma (dermoid)
Adult granulosa cell tumor
Mucinous cystadenoma as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: An estrogen-producing solid ovarian tumor causing postmenopausal bleeding and endometrial hyperplasia, with markedly elevated inhibin, is classic for an adult granulosa cell tumor, a sex cord-stromal neoplasm that secretes estrogen and uses inhibin as a tumor marker. A mucinous cystadenoma is a benign, typically multiloculated cystic, non-hormonal tumor and would not cause estrogen excess. A mature cystic teratoma (dermoid) is a germ cell tumor containing tissue from multiple layers and is not estrogenic. Serous cystadenocarcinoma is an epithelial malignancy associated with elevated CA-125 but not inhibin and does not characteristically secrete estrogen or cause the hormonal picture seen here.

stages.label: PATHOPHYSIOLOGY
stages.question: Why does this tumor produce endometrial hyperplasia and breast fullness?
options: It produces beta-hCG that stimulates the thyroid as the immediate next step
It secretes cortisol causing Cushing syndrome as the immediate next step
It secretes androgens that cause virilization as the immediate next step
It autonomously secretes estrogen, causing unopposed estrogenic stimulation of the endometrium
stages.explanation: Adult granulosa cell tumors autonomously secrete estrogen, and in a postmenopausal woman this unopposed estrogen stimulates the endometrium (producing hyperplasia, bleeding, and an increased risk of concurrent endometrial carcinoma) and the breast tissue (fullness/tenderness). They do not characteristically secrete androgens; virilization is the hallmark of androgen-secreting tumors such as Sertoli-Leydig cell tumors. They do not produce beta-hCG (a marker of gestational/germ cell trophoblastic processes), and the negative beta-hCG here is consistent. They do not secrete cortisol; Cushing syndrome arises from adrenal or pituitary/ectopic ACTH sources, not granulosa cell tumors.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate definitive management for this estrogen-secreting ovarian tumor with atypical endometrial hyperplasia?
options: Combined oral contraceptive pills as initial therapy
Observation with repeat ultrasound in 1 year as initial therapy
Surgical staging including total hysterectomy with bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy
Pelvic radiation alone as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Granulosa cell tumors are managed surgically. In a postmenopausal woman, total hysterectomy with bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy plus surgical staging is appropriate; this removes the estrogen source and simultaneously treats the atypical endometrial hyperplasia (which itself carries malignant potential and is treated definitively by hysterectomy). Observation is inappropriate for a solid estrogen-secreting tumor with atypical hyperplasia, given malignant potential and late recurrence risk. Pelvic radiation alone is not standard primary therapy. Combined oral contraceptives are not used to treat an estrogen-secreting malignancy and would add estrogen, which is precisely the opposite of what is needed. Inhibin can be followed postoperatively as a recurrence marker.

stages.label: FOLLOW-UP
stages.question: Which serum marker is most useful for monitoring recurrence of this tumor after surgery?
options: Inhibin
Lactate dehydrogenase (LDH)
Alpha-fetoprotein (AFP)
Carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA)
stages.explanation: Inhibin (along with anti-Mullerian hormone in some centers) is the tumor marker for granulosa cell tumors and is used to monitor for recurrence, which can occur many years after initial treatment. AFP is a marker for yolk sac tumors and hepatocellular carcinoma, not granulosa cell tumors. CEA is associated with colorectal and some mucinous tumors and is not specific here. LDH is a nonspecific marker elevated in dysgerminomas and other high-turnover tumors but is not the marker of choice for granulosa cell tumor surveillance. Following inhibin allows early detection of the characteristically late recurrences of this neoplasm.

cases.diagnosis: Granulosa cell tumor of the ovary
pearls: Postmenopausal bleeding is abnormal until proven otherwise and requires endometrial assessment, regardless of any adnexal findings.
An estrogen-secreting solid ovarian mass with elevated inhibin and endometrial hyperplasia is classic for an adult granulosa cell tumor.
Granulosa cell tumors cause unopposed estrogen effects (endometrial hyperplasia/carcinoma, breast fullness); always evaluate the endometrium.
Inhibin is the marker used to monitor granulosa cell tumors, which are notorious for late recurrences.

cases.title: A Deepening Voice and Unwanted Hair Growth
cases.chiefComplaint: A young woman presents with rapidly progressive masculinizing changes over a few months.
cases.history: A 26-year-old woman presents with a four-month history of rapidly progressive masculinizing changes. She has noticed coarse dark hair on her chin, chest, and abdomen, a deepening of her voice, increased acne, and thinning of her scalp hair at the temples. Her periods, previously regular, have stopped over the past three months. She also reports increased muscle bulk and that her clitoris seems enlarged. The onset has been alarmingly fast, unlike the gradual changes friends with PCOS describe. She is nulligravid and not using hormonal contraception. PMH is unremarkable and she takes no medications, supplements, or anabolic steroids. She has no known drug allergies. She does not smoke and drinks rarely. Family history is unremarkable; no relatives have similar symptoms or known endocrine tumors. She works as a software engineer. She is distressed by how quickly the changes are occurring and specifically denies any use of testosterone or performance-enhancing drugs.
cases.exam: The patient is well-appearing with obvious virilization. There is coarse terminal hair on the chin, upper lip, chest, and lower abdomen (marked hirsutism), temporal scalp hair recession, and moderate facial acne. The voice is notably deep. Breast exam shows no galactorrhea and no masses. Abdominal exam is soft; deep palpation suggests fullness in the right lower quadrant without tenderness. Pelvic examination reveals clitoromegaly. Bimanual exam identifies a firm, mobile right adnexal mass approximately 6 cm; the left adnexa is normal and there is no cervical motion tenderness. There are no Cushingoid features such as striae, central obesity, or a dorsocervical fat pad. There is no thyromegaly.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Rapid-onset virilization in a young woman points to an androgen excess source. What is the most appropriate initial laboratory evaluation?
options: Serum total testosterone
Thyroid-stimulating hormone alone as the first step today
Only a urine pregnancy test as the first step today
Random cortisol only as the first step today
stages.explanation: Rapid-onset virilization suggests an androgen-secreting tumor, and the key initial step is measuring serum total testosterone and DHEA-S to localize the source: markedly elevated testosterone with normal/low DHEA-S points to an ovarian source, whereas markedly elevated DHEA-S points to an adrenal source. A pregnancy test is reasonable before imaging or treatment but does not address the androgen excess workup. TSH evaluates thyroid disease, which does not cause virilization. Random cortisol alone screens for hypercortisolism, but she has no Cushingoid features, and cortisol does not explain androgenic virilization. Measuring testosterone and DHEA-S together is the high-yield first step to localize the lesion.

stages.label: HORMONE PANEL & IMAGING
stages.content:
Urine pregnancy test: negative
Total testosterone 320 ng/dL (markedly elevated; normal female <50)
DHEA-S: normal
17-hydroxyprogesterone: normal
LH/FSH: not consistent with PCOS pattern
Transvaginal ultrasound: solid right ovarian mass 6 cm with internal vascularity; left ovary normal
AFP normal; beta-hCG negative; inhibin mildly elevated

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Markedly elevated testosterone with normal DHEA-S and a solid ovarian mass in a young woman with virilization best fits which tumor?
options: Sertoli-Leydig cell tumor
Adrenal carcinoma as the primary diagnosis
Polycystic ovary syndrome as the primary diagnosis
Granulosa cell tumor as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Rapid virilization with markedly elevated testosterone, normal DHEA-S (excluding an adrenal source), and a solid ovarian mass in a young woman is the classic picture of a Sertoli-Leydig cell tumor, an androgen-secreting sex cord-stromal tumor that often arises in the second to fourth decades. PCOS causes gradual, mild hyperandrogenism with polycystic ovaries, not rapid virilization with clitoromegaly and a solid mass. Adrenal carcinoma would typically elevate DHEA-S (and often cortisol), which is normal here. A granulosa cell tumor secretes estrogen and causes feminizing effects/postmenopausal bleeding, not virilization, so it does not fit this androgenic presentation despite being in the same sex cord-stromal family.

stages.label: PATHOPHYSIOLOGY
stages.question: Which cell type within this tumor is chiefly responsible for the androgen production driving her symptoms?
options: Leydig cells
Adrenal zona glomerulosa cells
Granulosa cells in this setting
Theca lutein cells of pregnancy
stages.explanation: Sertoli-Leydig cell tumors contain Sertoli cells and Leydig cells; the Leydig cells produce testosterone, driving the virilization (hirsutism, clitoromegaly, voice deepening, amenorrhea). Granulosa cells are estrogen-producing cells characteristic of granulosa cell tumors, not the androgen source here. Theca lutein cells relate to pregnancy/hCG-driven ovarian changes and are not the mechanism in this nonpregnant patient. Adrenal zona glomerulosa cells produce aldosterone, not androgens, and the normal DHEA-S already excludes a significant adrenal androgen contribution. Recognizing Leydig cells as the testosterone-producing component explains the clinical androgen excess.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: She wishes to preserve fertility and the tumor appears confined to one ovary. What is the most appropriate surgical management?
options: Pelvic radiation as primary treatment as initial therapy
Bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy and hysterectomy as initial therapy
Long-term GnRH agonist therapy instead of surgery as initial therapy
Fertility-sparing unilateral salpingo-oophorectomy with surgical staging
stages.explanation: For a young woman with an apparently unilateral, confined sex cord-stromal tumor who desires fertility, fertility-sparing surgery with unilateral salpingo-oophorectomy and surgical staging is appropriate; removing the affected ovary halts androgen production and typically allows virilizing signs to regress. Bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy with hysterectomy sacrifices fertility unnecessarily when disease is confined to one ovary in a patient who wishes to conceive. GnRH agonist therapy does not remove the tumor and is not a substitute for surgical excision of an androgen-secreting neoplasm. Primary pelvic radiation is not standard treatment for a localized Sertoli-Leydig cell tumor and would impair fertility while leaving the tumor in place.

stages.label: FOLLOW-UP
stages.question: After successful removal of the tumor, what should the patient be counseled to expect regarding her virilizing symptoms?
options: Many androgenic signs regress over time, though voice deepening
They indicate the tumor was benign and need no monitoring as the immediate next step
They will worsen progressively despite surgery as the immediate next step
They are permanent and irreversible in all cases as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: After removal of an androgen-secreting tumor, the androgen-driven changes typically improve: menses usually resume, and acne and hirsutism tend to regress over months as testosterone normalizes. However, some changes that reflect more fixed tissue effects, such as voice deepening and clitoromegaly, may only partially regress or persist. The symptoms are not uniformly permanent, so telling her they are irreversible in all cases is incorrect. They should not worsen if the source is removed; progression would suggest residual or recurrent disease. Regardless of grade, she still requires postoperative surveillance (including testosterone levels), so assuming no monitoring is needed is wrong.

pearls: Rapid-onset virilization (hirsutism, voice deepening, clitoromegaly, amenorrhea) suggests an androgen-secreting tumor, not PCOS.
Testosterone plus DHEA-S localizes androgen excess: high testosterone with normal DHEA-S points to an ovarian source.
Sertoli-Leydig cell tumors are androgen-secreting sex cord-stromal tumors; the Leydig cells produce testosterone.
Fertility-sparing unilateral salpingo-oophorectomy is appropriate for unilateral confined disease; many virilizing signs regress after excision.

cases.title: A Pelvic Mass in a Teenager
cases.chiefComplaint: An adolescent presents with a few weeks of abdominal swelling and pelvic pressure.
cases.history: An 18-year-old woman presents with three weeks of increasing lower abdominal swelling and a sensation of pelvic pressure. She has noticed her lower abdomen looks distended and feels a firm fullness. She has mild dull pelvic discomfort but no severe pain, and denies fever, vaginal bleeding, or bowel or bladder changes. Her menarche was at age 13 and her cycles are regular; her last period was two weeks ago. She is not sexually active and has never been pregnant. PMH is unremarkable and she takes no medications. She has no known drug allergies. She does not smoke, drink, or use drugs. Family history is unremarkable for ovarian or other cancers. She is a high school senior preparing for college. She is anxious about the rapidly growing mass and reports she feels otherwise well, with normal appetite and no weight loss. There is no history of abnormal sexual development or ambiguous genitalia.
cases.exam: The patient is well-appearing and in no acute distress. The abdomen is mildly distended with a palpable, firm, smooth, non-tender mass arising from the pelvis extending to just below the umbilicus; there is no rebound or guarding and no shifting dullness to suggest large ascites. On limited pelvic/rectoabdominal exam (she is not sexually active), a large, firm, mobile midline-to-right pelvic mass is appreciated, distinct from a normal-feeling uterus. There is no inguinal lymphadenopathy. Secondary sexual characteristics are normal (normal breast and pubic hair development), with no signs of virilization or precocious changes. Cardiopulmonary exam is normal and there is no supraclavicular adenopathy.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: An adolescent presents with a rapidly growing pelvic mass. What is the most appropriate initial diagnostic step?
options: Immediate exploratory laparotomy without imaging as the first step today
Empiric antibiotics for pelvic inflammatory disease in this clinical context
Pelvic ultrasound plus serum tumor markers including beta-hCG, AFP, LDH,
Colonoscopy as the first step today
stages.explanation: A rapidly enlarging pelvic mass in an adolescent raises concern for an ovarian germ cell tumor, and the appropriate initial workup is pelvic ultrasound to characterize the mass plus serum tumor markers (beta-hCG, AFP, LDH) and a pregnancy test, since germ cell tumors produce specific markers that guide diagnosis and monitoring. Exploratory laparotomy before imaging and markers is premature and forfeits diagnostic information that shapes the surgical approach. Colonoscopy is not indicated for an adnexal/ovarian mass without GI symptoms. Empiric antibiotics for PID are inappropriate in an afebrile, sexually inactive adolescent with a solid mass and no infectious signs, and would dangerously delay diagnosis of a possible malignancy.

stages.label: ULTRASOUND & MARKERS
stages.content:
Transabdominal/transvaginal ultrasound:
Large solid right ovarian mass 12 cm, predominantly solid with internal flow on Doppler
No large ascites; uterus normal; left ovary normal
Pregnancy test: negative
LDH: markedly elevated
beta-hCG: minimally elevated
AFP: normal
CA-125: mildly elevated (nonspecific)

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: A solid ovarian mass in an 18-year-old with markedly elevated LDH and a normal AFP most likely represents which tumor?
options: Yolk sac (endodermal sinus) tumor
Dysgerminoma
Serous cystadenocarcinoma as the primary diagnosis
Mature cystic teratoma as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: A solid ovarian germ cell tumor in an adolescent with a markedly elevated LDH and normal AFP is classic for a dysgerminoma, the most common malignant ovarian germ cell tumor, which characteristically elevates LDH (and sometimes mildly beta-hCG via syncytiotrophoblastic giant cells). A mature cystic teratoma is benign and typically cystic with fat/calcification, not a markedly LDH-elevating solid malignancy. A yolk sac tumor classically elevates AFP, which is normal here. Serous cystadenocarcinoma is an epithelial cancer of older women associated with elevated CA-125, not the germ cell marker pattern, and is uncommon in an 18-year-old. The LDH-predominant, AFP-normal pattern strongly favors dysgerminoma.

stages.label: PATHOLOGY
stages.question: Which histologic and clinical features are characteristic of this tumor?
options: Sheets of uniform cells with lymphocytic infiltrate
Psammoma bodies and BRCA association as the immediate next step
Schiller-Duval bodies and high AFP as the immediate next step
Reinke crystals and androgen secretion as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Dysgerminoma histology shows sheets of uniform, large cells with clear cytoplasm separated by fibrous septa infiltrated by lymphocytes; clinically these tumors are notably radiosensitive and highly chemosensitive (excellent response to platinum-based regimens), which underlies their generally favorable prognosis. Schiller-Duval bodies with high AFP are features of yolk sac tumors, not dysgerminoma. Psammoma bodies and BRCA association characterize serous epithelial ovarian carcinoma. Reinke crystals and androgen secretion are features of Leydig-cell-containing tumors (such as Sertoli-Leydig cell tumors), not germinomatous dysgerminoma. The lymphocyte-rich, septate sheets of uniform cells are the hallmark.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Disease appears confined to one ovary in this 18-year-old who wishes to preserve fertility. What is the most appropriate surgical management?
options: Fertility-sparing unilateral salpingo-oophorectomy with surgical staging
Pelvic exenteration as initial therapy
Mass biopsy only, leaving the tumor in place as initial therapy
Bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy with hysterectomy as initial therapy
stages.explanation: For a young woman with an apparently unilateral malignant germ cell tumor who desires future fertility, fertility-sparing surgery, namely unilateral salpingo-oophorectomy with surgical staging, is the standard approach; the contralateral ovary and uterus are preserved because these tumors are usually unilateral and highly chemosensitive, allowing safe conservation. Bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy with hysterectomy needlessly destroys fertility for confined disease. Biopsy alone, leaving the tumor in situ, is inadequate oncologic management. Pelvic exenteration is a radical procedure for advanced pelvic malignancy and is wholly inappropriate for a localized, fertility-desiring patient with a chemosensitive germ cell tumor.

stages.label: ADJUVANT THERAPY
stages.question: If staging reveals disease beyond the ovary, which systemic therapy is the mainstay for dysgerminoma?
options: Trastuzumab as the immediate next step
No therapy is ever needed because all germ cell tumors are benign
Platinum-based combination chemotherapy
Tamoxifen as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: When dysgerminoma extends beyond the ovary, platinum-based combination chemotherapy, classically BEP (bleomycin, etoposide, cisplatin), is the mainstay and produces excellent cure rates given the tumor's marked chemosensitivity. Tamoxifen is an endocrine therapy for hormone-receptor-positive breast cancer and has no role here. Trastuzumab targets HER2-positive breast/gastric cancers and is irrelevant to germ cell tumors. The notion that all germ cell tumors are benign and never need therapy is false; dysgerminoma is malignant, and while early confined disease may be managed with surgery and surveillance, more advanced disease requires chemotherapy. LDH can be followed to monitor response.

pearls: A rapidly growing pelvic mass in an adolescent warrants ultrasound plus germ cell tumor markers (beta-hCG, AFP, LDH) and a pregnancy test.
Dysgerminoma is the most common malignant ovarian germ cell tumor and classically elevates LDH with a normal AFP.
Histology shows sheets of uniform cells with lymphocytic infiltrate; the tumor is exquisitely radiosensitive and chemosensitive.
Fertility-sparing unilateral salpingo-oophorectomy with staging is standard for confined disease; advanced disease responds well to BEP chemotherapy.

cases.title: Bilateral Ovarian Masses and Months of Indigestion
cases.chiefComplaint: A woman is referred for bilateral solid ovarian masses found during a workup for abdominal bloating and early satiety.
cases.history: A 48-year-old woman is referred to oncology after imaging for several months of abdominal bloating, early satiety, and a 6 kg unintentional weight loss revealed bilateral solid ovarian masses. She has also had vague epigastric discomfort, nausea, and intermittent dark stools that she attributed to indigestion. She is premenopausal with somewhat irregular recent cycles. She is G3P3. PMH includes iron-deficiency anemia diagnosed last year, for which she takes ferrous sulfate, and reflux treated with omeprazole 20 mg daily. She has no known drug allergies. She does not smoke and drinks rarely. Family history is notable for a father with gastric cancer. She emigrated from a region with high gastric cancer prevalence. She works in retail. She is worried about the ovarian findings, but on questioning her most prominent symptoms have actually been upper-GI: epigastric pain, fullness, and the dark stools, which prompted her primary doctor to order the imaging in the first place.
cases.exam: The patient appears pale and mildly cachectic. Conjunctivae are pale, consistent with anemia. The abdomen is mildly distended with shifting dullness suggesting ascites; there is vague epigastric tenderness without rebound. Bimanual pelvic exam reveals bilateral firm, solid adnexal masses, the larger approximately 9 cm, with relatively smooth surfaces; the uterus is normal-sized. Rectal exam shows dark, guaiac-positive stool. There is a firm, enlarged left supraclavicular lymph node (Virchow node) and mild periumbilical firmness. There is no breast mass. The combination of bilateral solid ovarian masses, GI symptoms, anemia, and a left supraclavicular node points away from a primary ovarian process.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Bilateral solid ovarian masses with prominent GI symptoms, weight loss, anemia, and a Virchow node raise concern for which scenario?
options: Functional ovarian cysts as the first step today
Benign bilateral fibromas as the first step today
Metastatic disease to the ovaries from a GI primary
Primary epithelial ovarian cancer with no need to evaluate the GI tract
stages.explanation: Bilateral solid ovarian masses accompanied by dominant upper-GI symptoms, weight loss, iron-deficiency anemia, melena, and a left supraclavicular (Virchow) node strongly suggest metastatic disease to the ovaries from a gastrointestinal primary (a Krukenberg tumor), so the GI tract must be evaluated. Assuming primary epithelial ovarian cancer and ignoring the GI tract would miss the actual primary; bilaterality and the GI/constitutional features argue against a primary ovarian process. Benign bilateral fibromas would not cause weight loss, melena, anemia, and a Virchow node. Functional cysts are physiologic, typically cystic, and do not produce this systemic, metastatic-appearing picture. The clinical gestalt mandates a search for a GI primary.

stages.label: IMAGING & ENDOSCOPY
stages.content:
CT abdomen/pelvis:
Bilateral solid enhancing ovarian masses (9 cm and 6 cm)
Gastric wall thickening along the antrum; perigastric and para-aortic lymphadenopathy
Moderate ascites; peritoneal nodularity
Upper endoscopy: infiltrative, rigid (linitis plastica-type) gastric lesion; biopsy obtained
Labs: hemoglobin 8.9 g/dL, microcytic; CA-125 mildly elevated; CEA elevated

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Gastric biopsy and ovarian pathology are pending. Which histologic finding would confirm a Krukenberg tumor in the ovaries?
options: Mucin-filled signet-ring cells infiltrating a cellular stroma
Schiller-Duval bodies as the primary diagnosis
Psammoma bodies in a serous pattern as the primary diagnosis
Call-Exner bodies as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: A Krukenberg tumor is defined by metastatic mucin-secreting signet-ring cells (cells with mucin pushing the nucleus to the periphery) infiltrating the ovarian stroma, most commonly from a gastric (or other GI) primary. Schiller-Duval bodies are pathognomonic of yolk sac tumors, not metastatic signet-ring carcinoma. Call-Exner bodies are characteristic of granulosa cell tumors. Psammoma bodies in a serous pattern point to serous epithelial ovarian (or other serous) carcinoma. The identification of signet-ring cells in the ovarian stroma, together with the gastric primary, confirms the diagnosis of a metastatic Krukenberg tumor.

stages.label: PATHOPHYSIOLOGY
stages.question: What is the most common primary site for a Krukenberg tumor, and what is the typical route of spread?
options: Brain primary via CSF seeding as the immediate next step
Lung primary via hematogenous spread as the immediate next step
Bladder primary via direct extension only as the immediate next step
Gastric primary, classically via transcoelomic/lymphatic spread to bilateral ovaries
stages.explanation: Krukenberg tumors most commonly originate from a gastric primary (signet-ring/diffuse-type gastric adenocarcinoma), with colorectal and other GI sites also contributing; spread to bilateral ovaries is classically attributed to transcoelomic dissemination and lymphatic routes, explaining the characteristic bilaterality. A lung primary with hematogenous spread is not the classic source of a Krukenberg tumor. Brain primaries with CSF seeding do not metastasize to the ovaries in this fashion. A bladder primary by direct extension would not produce bilateral ovarian signet-ring metastases. Recognizing the GI (especially gastric) origin and transcoelomic/lymphatic spread explains both the bilaterality and the accompanying GI symptoms.

stages.label: PATHOLOGY
stages.content:
Gastric biopsy: poorly differentiated adenocarcinoma with signet-ring cells (diffuse type)
Ovarian biopsy: metastatic signet-ring cell adenocarcinoma infiltrating ovarian stroma
Immunohistochemistry: CK20 and CDX2 patterns consistent with a GI primary
Staging: metastatic (stage IV) gastric cancer with bilateral ovarian and peritoneal involvement

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given biopsy-proven metastatic (stage IV) gastric adenocarcinoma with bilateral ovarian Krukenberg metastases and peritoneal disease, what is the most appropriate overall management approach?
options: Observation, since ovarian metastases are harmless as initial therapy
Curative bilateral oophorectomy alone will cure the patient as initial therapy
Pelvic radiation alone to the ovaries as initial therapy
Systemic chemotherapy directed at the gastric primary, with multidisciplinary
stages.explanation: Krukenberg tumors represent metastatic (stage IV) disease, so management centers on systemic chemotherapy directed at the primary gastric adenocarcinoma, with multidisciplinary and palliative care involvement; any surgery (such as oophorectomy for symptomatic ovarian masses or obstruction) is generally palliative rather than curative, and overall prognosis is poor. Believing bilateral oophorectomy alone is curative is incorrect because the disease is systemic and the primary remains. Pelvic radiation to the ovaries alone does not address the gastric primary or widespread peritoneal/nodal disease. Observation is inappropriate, since the metastatic cancer is progressive and life-limiting and the patient needs active oncologic and supportive care.

stages.label: PROGNOSIS
stages.question: How should the team counsel the patient and family about her overall prognosis?
options: Prognosis is excellent because germ cell tumors are highly curable as the immediate next step
Prognosis is generally poor, as a Krukenberg tumor reflects metastatic gastric adenocarcinoma
The ovarian masses are the only problem, so prognosis is excellent after their removal as the immediate next step
She is likely cured by the antibiotics she has already received as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: A Krukenberg tumor signifies stage IV gastric adenocarcinoma with peritoneal and ovarian spread, which carries a generally poor prognosis; honest counseling centers on realistic expectations, disease control with systemic therapy, symptom and palliative management, and an early goals-of-care discussion. Claiming the ovarian masses are the only problem ignores the underlying metastatic gastric primary and peritoneal disease. The tumor is not a germ cell tumor, so the favorable germ cell prognosis does not apply. Antibiotics have no role in treating this malignancy and could not cure it. Compassionate, accurate prognostic counseling with involvement of palliative care is the appropriate approach.

pearls: Bilateral solid ovarian masses with GI symptoms, weight loss, and anemia should prompt a search for a GI (often gastric) primary.
A Krukenberg tumor is defined by metastatic mucin-filled signet-ring cells infiltrating the ovarian stroma.
The classic primary is diffuse-type (signet-ring) gastric adenocarcinoma, spreading transcoelomically/lymphatically to both ovaries.
Krukenberg tumors are stage IV disease; treatment is systemic chemotherapy and palliative care, with surgery generally palliative.

cases.title: Fever and Collapse in a Young Woman on Her Period
cases.chiefComplaint: A young woman is brought to the ED with high fever, a diffuse rash, and near-fainting.
cases.history: A 20-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department by her roommate with two days of high fever, severe myalgias, vomiting, watery diarrhea, and a diffuse red rash, and she nearly fainted when standing this morning. She is on the third day of her menstrual period and has been using high-absorbency tampons, sometimes leaving them in overnight. Over the past 12 hours she has become confused and lightheaded. She denies recent travel, sick contacts, or new medications. PMH is unremarkable; she takes only an oral contraceptive pill and has no recent surgeries or skin wounds. She has no known drug allergies. She does not smoke, drinks occasionally, and denies illicit drug use. She is sexually active with one partner and uses condoms intermittently. She is a college sophomore. Her roommate notes she normally is healthy and that the illness came on rapidly with the rash spreading over her whole body, including her palms.
cases.exam: The patient is ill-appearing, flushed, and confused (oriented only to self). She is hypotensive and tachycardic. There is a diffuse, blanching, macular erythroderma resembling a sunburn covering the trunk and extremities, including the palms and soles. The conjunctivae are injected (nonpurulent) and the oral mucosa and tongue are hyperemic (strawberry tongue). The neck is supple. Cardiopulmonary exam shows tachycardia with clear lungs. The abdomen is mildly diffusely tender without peritoneal signs. Pelvic exam reveals a retained tampon, which is removed; there is no purulent cervical discharge. Capillary refill is delayed and extremities are warm. There is no focal neurologic deficit and no nuchal rigidity.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A menstruating tampon user presents with fever, hypotension, and diffuse erythroderma. What are the most critical immediate actions?
options: Remove the tampon , give aggressive IV fluid resuscitation, obtain cultures,
Await blood culture results before any treatment as the first step today
Discharge home with oral fluids and antipyretics as the first step today
Give a single dose of oral acetaminophen and reassess in the waiting room in this clinical context
stages.explanation: This is toxic shock syndrome with septic shock physiology, and the immediate priorities are source control (remove the retained tampon), aggressive IV fluid resuscitation for hypotension, obtaining blood and site cultures, and starting empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics promptly. Discharge home is dangerous in a hypotensive, confused, febrile patient. Awaiting culture results before treating is unacceptable in shock; antibiotics and resuscitation must begin immediately. A single dose of acetaminophen with waiting-room reassessment grossly under-treats life-threatening shock. Early aggressive resuscitation and antibiotics, with removal of the nidus, are the cornerstone of TSS management.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
WBC 16.5 ×10³/µL with bandemia
Platelets 92 ×10³/µL
Creatinine 2.1 mg/dL (elevated)
AST 110 U/L, ALT 98 U/L
Total bilirubin 2.0 mg/dL
Creatine kinase 1400 U/L
Lactate 4.2 mmol/L
Blood cultures: pending
Urinalysis: pyuria without bacteriuria

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The clinical picture and labs (fever, hypotension, diffuse erythroderma, and multiorgan involvement) best fit which diagnosis?
options: Acute viral gastroenteritis
Staphylococcal toxic shock syndrome
Meningococcemia
Anaphylaxis in this clinical context
stages.explanation: Fever, hypotension, diffuse macular erythroderma (including palms/soles), strawberry tongue, and multisystem involvement (renal, hepatic, hematologic, muscular, GI, CNS) in a menstruating tampon user are the classic CDC clinical criteria for staphylococcal toxic shock syndrome, mediated by the superantigen TSST-1. Anaphylaxis causes urticaria, bronchospasm, and angioedema with a clear allergen exposure, not erythroderma with multiorgan failure and high fever. Acute viral gastroenteritis can cause vomiting and diarrhea but not shock with diffuse erythroderma and multiorgan dysfunction. Meningococcemia typically produces a petechial/purpuric (non-blanching) rash and meningismus, whereas this rash is a blanching, diffuse erythroderma without nuchal rigidity.

stages.label: PATHOPHYSIOLOGY
stages.question: What is the underlying mechanism driving the shock and multiorgan dysfunction in this syndrome?
options: Direct viral cytolysis of organs as the immediate next step
Autoantibodies against platelets only as the immediate next step
IgE-mediated mast cell degranulation as the immediate next step
A bacterial superantigen toxin that triggers massive nonspecific T-cell activation
stages.explanation: Toxic shock syndrome is driven by a bacterial superantigen (TSST-1 in staphylococcal TSS, or streptococcal pyrogenic exotoxins in streptococcal TSS) that bridges MHC class II molecules to the T-cell receptor outside the normal antigen groove, causing massive nonspecific polyclonal T-cell activation and a cytokine storm (TNF, IL-1, IL-2), which produces capillary leak, vasodilation, shock, and multiorgan injury. IgE-mediated mast cell degranulation is the mechanism of anaphylaxis, not TSS. Direct viral cytolysis does not explain the superantigen-driven cytokine storm. Autoantibodies against platelets describe immune thrombocytopenia; the thrombocytopenia here is part of the systemic toxin-mediated process, not an isolated autoimmune platelet phenomenon.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Beyond fluids and source control, which antibiotic strategy is most appropriate for staphylococcal toxic shock syndrome?
options: Topical antibiotics only as initial therapy
A single beta-lactam alone as initial therapy
Antifungal therapy alone as initial therapy
Combination therapy including an antistaphylococcal agent plus a protein-synthesis inhibitor such as clindamycin to suppress toxin production
stages.explanation: Optimal antibiotic therapy for staphylococcal TSS combines an antistaphylococcal agent (e.g., vancomycin empirically for possible MRSA, with addition or transition to an antistaphylococcal beta-lactam if methicillin-susceptible) plus a protein-synthesis inhibitor such as clindamycin or linezolid, because clindamycin suppresses ribosomal toxin (TSST-1) production, reducing the superantigen drive. A single beta-lactam alone does not halt toxin synthesis and is suboptimal. Topical antibiotics cannot treat systemic toxin-mediated shock. Antifungal therapy alone is inappropriate; this is a bacterial toxin-mediated illness. IVIG may be considered in severe or refractory cases, but the core regimen pairs antistaphylococcal coverage with a toxin-suppressing agent.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After stabilization, what is the most important counseling point to prevent recurrence?
options: She may resume using high-absorbency tampons left in overnight
No counseling is needed because TSS never recurs with appropriate follow-up
Avoid high-absorbency tampons
She should never menstruate again with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: Menstrual TSS is associated with high-absorbency tampons and prolonged tampon retention, so key prevention counseling is to avoid high-absorbency tampons, change tampons frequently, consider alternatives, and recognize that menstrual TSS can recur, especially without eradication of the colonizing organism. Telling her to resume high-absorbency overnight tampon use directly reproduces the risk factor and is wrong. Advising her to never menstruate again is absurd and not a real intervention. Claiming no counseling is needed because TSS never recurs is false; recurrence is a recognized risk, which is precisely why behavioral counseling (and consideration of decolonization in some cases) is essential at discharge.

pearls: Suspect menstrual TSS in a tampon user with fever, hypotension, diffuse erythroderma (including palms/soles), and multiorgan dysfunction.
Immediate management is source control (remove tampon), aggressive IV fluids, cultures, and prompt empiric antibiotics.
TSS is superantigen-mediated (TSST-1): massive nonspecific T-cell activation and cytokine storm cause capillary leak and shock.
Add clindamycin (or linezolid) to antistaphylococcal coverage to suppress toxin production, and counsel on tampon use to prevent recurrence.

cases.title: Sudden Pelvic Pain and a Fainting Spell
cases.chiefComplaint: Sudden severe lower abdominal pain with a brief loss of consciousness.
cases.history: A 30-year-old woman is brought in after collapsing at home. She reports sudden, sharp right-lower-quadrant pain that began an hour ago while getting up, followed by lightheadedness and a witnessed syncopal episode. She notes mild vaginal spotting over the past two days. Her last menstrual period was about 7 weeks ago, and she describes recent cycles as irregular. A home pregnancy test last week was positive, but she has not yet established prenatal care. She has a history of chlamydial pelvic inflammatory disease treated three years ago and one prior cesarean delivery. She uses no contraception. Medications are a daily prenatal vitamin; she takes no anticoagulants. She has no drug allergies. She smokes half a pack of cigarettes daily and drinks alcohol socially. There is no personal or family history of bleeding disorders. She reports right shoulder discomfort that started after the pain and denies fever, dysuria, or recent trauma. She appears pale and anxious.
cases.exam: The patient is pale, diaphoretic, and uncomfortable. Cardiovascular exam reveals tachycardia with thready peripheral pulses and cool extremities; capillary refill is 3 seconds. The abdomen is diffusely tender with rebound and guarding, most marked in the right lower quadrant. Bowel sounds are diminished. There is referred right shoulder-tip pain. Pelvic exam shows a small amount of dark blood in the vault, a closed cervix, marked cervical motion tenderness, and right adnexal fullness with tenderness. No active brisk vaginal bleeding is seen. The uterus is mildly enlarged and difficult to assess due to guarding. Lungs are clear and there is no peripheral edema.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate immediate first step in management?
