Letrozole for ovulation induction as initial therapy
Metformin as monotherapy for hirsutism
Combined oral contraceptive pills, plus lifestyle modification
stages.explanation: For a patient not seeking pregnancy, combined oral contraceptives are first-line: they regulate cycles, provide endometrial protection against hyperplasia from unopposed estrogen, and reduce androgens (raising SHBG and suppressing ovarian androgen production), improving hirsutism and acne. Lifestyle modification (weight loss, exercise) is foundational and improves insulin resistance and ovulation. Letrozole induces ovulation and is first-line only when fertility is the goal, which it is not here. Metformin helps metabolic parameters and can aid ovulation but is not an effective primary treatment for hirsutism. Spironolactone is a useful antiandrogen add-on but should not be used alone in a sexually active patient because of teratogenicity (feminization of a male fetus) — it requires reliable contraception.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Beyond cosmetic and menstrual concerns, which long-term risk most warrants ongoing screening in this patient?
options: Type 2 diabetes, metabolic syndrome,
Primary ovarian insufficiency as the leading concern
Pheochromocytoma as the leading concern
Osteoporosis from estrogen deficiency
stages.explanation: PCOS is strongly associated with insulin resistance, leading to impaired glucose tolerance, type 2 diabetes, and metabolic syndrome — her HbA1c of 5.9% (prediabetes), elevated triglycerides, and acanthosis nigricans already signal this. Chronic anovulation means prolonged unopposed estrogen exposure, raising the risk of endometrial hyperplasia and endometrial carcinoma, which is why cycle regulation/endometrial protection matters. PCOS patients have estrogen excess, not deficiency, so osteoporosis is not the concern. Primary ovarian insufficiency involves hypoestrogenism and elevated FSH — the opposite hormonal pattern. Pheochromocytoma is unrelated. Ongoing screening for glucose intolerance, lipids, and endometrial protection is the high-yield long-term focus.

pearls: Rotterdam criteria: at least 2 of 3 — oligo/anovulation, hyperandrogenism (clinical or biochemical), polycystic ovaries on ultrasound — after excluding thyroid disease, hyperprolactinemia, nonclassic CAH, and androgen-secreting tumors.
PCOS is the most common cause of anovulatory infertility and is tightly linked to insulin resistance, type 2 diabetes, and metabolic syndrome.
First-line therapy when not pursuing pregnancy is combined OCPs plus lifestyle change; letrozole is first-line when fertility is desired.
Chronic anovulation causes unopposed estrogen, raising endometrial hyperplasia/cancer risk — endometrial protection is essential.

cases.title: Painful Periods Since the Teens
cases.chiefComplaint: Crampy lower abdominal pain with every menstrual period since her first cycle.
cases.history: A 17-year-old nulligravid adolescent presents with her mother for evaluation of painful periods. She describes crampy, midline lower abdominal pain that begins a few hours before or at the onset of menstrual flow and lasts one to two days each cycle. The pain is sometimes accompanied by nausea, loose stools, and headaches, and occasionally causes her to miss part of a school day. Symptoms have been present since menarche at age 12, and her cycles are regular at 28–30 days. She has tried occasional acetaminophen with limited relief. She is not sexually active and denies abnormal vaginal discharge, intermenstrual or postcoital bleeding, dyspareunia, fever, or urinary symptoms. There is no progressive worsening over the years and no pain outside of menses. Past medical history is unremarkable. She takes no daily medications and has no allergies. She does not use tobacco, alcohol, or drugs and is a high school student. Family history is unremarkable with no known endometriosis. Review of systems is otherwise negative.
cases.exam: The patient is a comfortable, well-appearing adolescent in no acute distress. She is afebrile with normal vital signs. The abdomen is soft, nontender, and nondistended with no masses, guarding, or rebound. There is no costovertebral angle tenderness. Given that she is not sexually active and the history is classic, an external genital inspection is performed and is normal; an internal pelvic exam is deferred as it is not required to diagnose primary dysmenorrhea in a virginal adolescent with a typical history. There are no signs suggesting a pelvic mass, infection, or structural abnormality. Overall the examination is entirely normal and reassuring.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which feature most strongly supports primary rather than secondary dysmenorrhea?
options: Pain that began years after menarche and is progressively worsening
Onset of pain with menarche, regular cycles,
An adnexal mass on examination as the primary diagnosis
Deep dyspareunia and pain throughout the cycle
stages.explanation: Primary dysmenorrhea is painful menses without underlying pelvic pathology; the hallmark is pain that begins with or shortly after menarche (once ovulatory cycles establish), occurs with regular cycles, is limited to the menstrual window, and is accompanied by a normal exam — exactly this presentation. Pain that starts years after menarche and worsens over time suggests secondary dysmenorrhea from acquired pathology such as endometriosis or adenomyosis. Deep dyspareunia and noncyclic pelvic pain also point toward secondary causes like endometriosis. An adnexal mass implies structural pathology and mandates further evaluation. The classic temporal pattern plus a benign exam is what defines primary dysmenorrhea.

stages.label: BASELINE WORKUP
stages.content:
Urine hCG: negative
Urinalysis: negative for leukocyte esterase, nitrites, and blood
CBC: within normal limits
Gonorrhea/chlamydia NAAT (offered): not indicated/declined — patient not sexually active
Pelvic ultrasound (obtained for reassurance): normal uterus and ovaries, no masses, normal-appearing pelvis

stages.label: PATHOPHYSIOLOGY
stages.question: What is the underlying mechanism of primary dysmenorrhea?
options: Elevated prolactin suppressing ovulation as the immediate next step
Retrograde flow obstructed by an imperforate hymen as the immediate next step
Excess endometrial prostaglandin production causing uterine hypercontractility
Ectopic endometrial implants on the peritoneum as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Primary dysmenorrhea results from overproduction of endometrial prostaglandins, chiefly PGF2α, during the luteal phase and at menses. Prostaglandins cause intense myometrial contractions, increased uterine tone, and vasoconstriction leading to uterine ischemia and pain, and their systemic effects explain the associated nausea, diarrhea, and headache. This mechanism is why prostaglandin synthesis inhibitors (NSAIDs) are so effective. Ectopic endometrial implants describe endometriosis, a secondary cause. An imperforate hymen causes outflow obstruction with hematocolpos and primary amenorrhea, not cyclic pain with normal menses. Elevated prolactin suppresses ovulation and causes oligomenorrhea, not dysmenorrhea. The prostaglandin hypothesis directly guides the NSAID-based treatment.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first-line treatment for this adolescent?
options: Scheduled NSAIDs started at or just before symptom onset
Opioid analgesics as needed as initial therapy
GnRH agonist therapy as initial therapy
Diagnostic laparoscopy as initial therapy
stages.explanation: NSAIDs are first-line for primary dysmenorrhea because they inhibit cyclooxygenase and reduce prostaglandin production; they are most effective when started at the onset of symptoms (or just before the expected period) and taken on a scheduled basis through the painful days. Diagnostic laparoscopy is invasive and reserved for suspected secondary causes (e.g., endometriosis) or treatment failure, not initial management of a classic case. Opioids are inappropriate for a benign, recurrent condition given dependence and side-effect risks. GnRH agonists are reserved for refractory secondary dysmenorrhea/endometriosis and cause hypoestrogenic side effects unsuitable for first-line use. If NSAIDs are insufficient or contraception is also desired, combined oral contraceptives are the next step.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: If she returns after a 3-month trial of scheduled NSAIDs with persistent disabling pain, what is the most appropriate next step?
options: Reassure her that nothing more can be done in this setting
Immediately refer for hysterectomy in this setting
Initiate hormonal therapy with combined oral contraceptives
Begin chronic opioid therapy as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: When adequate NSAID therapy fails to control primary dysmenorrhea, combined hormonal contraceptives (or other hormonal options like the levonorgestrel IUD) are the next step; they suppress ovulation and thin the endometrium, reducing prostaglandin production and menstrual pain, and offer contraceptive benefit. Hysterectomy is never appropriate for primary dysmenorrhea in a 17-year-old and would eliminate future fertility for a benign condition. Chronic opioids are contraindicated for a recurrent benign disorder. Reassurance alone abandons a patient with disabling symptoms and ignores effective options. If both NSAIDs and hormonal therapy fail, evaluation for secondary causes (such as endometriosis) with possible laparoscopy becomes appropriate.

pearls: Primary dysmenorrhea begins with the onset of ovulatory cycles (within ~6–12 months of menarche), is cyclic with menses, and has a normal pelvic exam.
It is caused by excess endometrial prostaglandins (PGF2α) producing uterine hypercontractility and ischemia.
NSAIDs are first-line and work best when started before or at symptom onset; combined hormonal contraceptives are the next step if NSAIDs fail.
Pain that starts well after menarche, worsens over time, or is associated with dyspareunia/noncyclic pain suggests secondary dysmenorrhea warranting further workup.

cases.title: Years of Pelvic Pain and Pain with Intercourse
cases.chiefComplaint: Chronic pelvic pain, pain with intercourse, and painful bowel movements during menses.
cases.history: A 29-year-old nulligravid woman presents with several years of cyclic and increasingly noncyclic pelvic pain. She describes deep pain with intercourse (dyspareunia), painful bowel movements during her periods (dyschezia), and severe dysmenorrhea that no longer responds well to NSAIDs. The pain has progressively worsened and now occurs on many days of the month. She has been evaluated multiple times over the years and told her exams were normal; she feels dismissed. She and her partner have been trying to conceive for 14 months without success. Menarche was at age 11 with regular cycles. She denies abnormal discharge, fever, hematochezia, or weight loss. Past medical history is unremarkable. Medications: naproxen as needed with limited relief; no hormonal contraception currently. No known drug allergies. She does not smoke or use drugs and drinks rarely. She works as an attorney. Family history is notable for endometriosis in her mother and a sister with infertility. Review of systems is otherwise negative.
cases.exam: The patient is well-appearing and in no acute distress. The abdomen is soft with mild lower-quadrant tenderness but no masses, rebound, or guarding. On pelvic exam the external genitalia and vagina are normal. There is tenderness on palpation of the posterior vaginal fornix, and nodularity is appreciated along the uterosacral ligaments. The uterus is normal in size, slightly retroverted, and mildly fixed/tender on motion. There are no large adnexal masses, though there is fullness and tenderness in the left adnexa. No cervical discharge is seen and there is no cervical motion tenderness suggestive of acute infection. The remainder of the examination, including rectovaginal palpation, reproduces her deep dyspareunia and confirms uterosacral nodularity.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which diagnosis best explains chronic dyspareunia, dyschezia, dysmenorrhea, infertility, and uterosacral nodularity?
options: Primary dysmenorrhea
Endometriosis
Pelvic inflammatory disease
Irritable bowel syndrome
stages.explanation: The triad of dysmenorrhea, deep dyspareunia, and dyschezia, together with infertility, uterosacral nodularity, a fixed/tender retroverted uterus, and adnexal fullness, is classic for endometriosis — ectopic endometrial tissue implanting on the peritoneum, ovaries, and uterosacral ligaments. The family history further supports it. Pelvic inflammatory disease is an acute infection with fever, discharge, and cervical motion tenderness, none of which are present. Primary dysmenorrhea has a normal exam and no progression or infertility, unlike this case. Irritable bowel syndrome causes altered bowel habits but does not produce uterosacral nodularity, dyspareunia, or infertility. The exam findings of nodularity and a fixed uterus are the key physical clues to endometriosis.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: What is the definitive method to confirm the diagnosis of endometriosis?
options: Laparoscopy with direct visualization
Elevated serum CA-125 as the immediate next step
Endometrial biopsy as the immediate next step
Transvaginal ultrasound alone before discharge planning
stages.explanation: Laparoscopy with direct visualization of endometriotic implants (and histologic confirmation/biopsy) is the gold standard for definitively diagnosing endometriosis; it also allows simultaneous treatment via ablation or excision. CA-125 can be elevated in endometriosis but is nonspecific and insensitive, so it cannot confirm the diagnosis. Transvaginal ultrasound is useful to detect endometriomas (ovarian 'chocolate cysts') and deep infiltrating disease but normal imaging does not exclude peritoneal endometriosis, which is often invisible on ultrasound. Endometrial biopsy samples the uterine lining and does not diagnose ectopic implants. Importantly, empiric medical therapy is often started before laparoscopy, but when definitive confirmation is required, laparoscopy is the answer.

stages.label: IMAGING & OPERATIVE FINDINGS
stages.content:
Transvaginal ultrasound: 3.5 cm left ovarian cyst with homogeneous low-level internal echoes ('ground-glass'), suggestive of an endometrioma
CA-125: 62 U/mL (mildly elevated)
Diagnostic laparoscopy: powder-burn peritoneal implants, left ovarian endometrioma, and uterosacral ligament nodularity; adhesions noted
Histology: endometrial glands and stroma confirming endometriosis

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: She wishes to conceive and reports the diagnostic delay was frustrating. What management best addresses both her pain and fertility goals?
options: Surgical excision/ablation of lesions at laparoscopy, then referral for fertility evaluation/assisted reproduction
Continuous combined oral contraceptives indefinitely as initial therapy
Total hysterectomy with bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy as initial therapy
GnRH agonist therapy for 12 months as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Because she desires pregnancy, hormonal suppression (OCPs, progestins, GnRH agonists) is not ideal as those methods prevent conception. Operative laparoscopy to excise/ablate implants and the endometrioma can both reduce pain and improve fertility, and she should then be referred for fertility evaluation and assisted reproductive techniques (e.g., IVF) given 14 months of infertility. Continuous OCPs control pain but block conception, contradicting her goals. GnRH agonists are effective for pain but induce a hypoestrogenic, anovulatory state — incompatible with attempting pregnancy now. Definitive surgery with hysterectomy and oophorectomy eliminates fertility and is reserved for refractory disease in women who have completed childbearing. Surgery plus fertility referral aligns with her dual goals.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: If she later completes childbearing and has recurrent disabling pain despite medical therapy, what is the most definitive option?
options: Endometrial ablation with appropriate follow-up
Long-term opioid therapy with appropriate follow-up
Repeat diagnostic laparoscopy every 6 months
Hysterectomy with bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy
stages.explanation: For women who have completed childbearing with refractory, disabling endometriosis, definitive surgery — hysterectomy with bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy plus excision of visible implants — offers the most durable relief by removing the uterus and the ovarian estrogen source that drives implant growth. Long-term opioids are inappropriate for chronic benign pain and risk dependence. Repeated diagnostic laparoscopies are not a treatment strategy and add surgical risk without lasting benefit. Endometrial ablation targets the endometrial lining for abnormal uterine bleeding and does not treat extrauterine endometriotic implants, so it would not relieve endometriosis pain. Definitive surgery is reserved precisely for this scenario of completed childbearing and refractory symptoms.

pearls: Classic endometriosis presents with the 'three Ds' — dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, dyschezia — plus infertility; exam may show uterosacral nodularity and a fixed, retroverted uterus, though it is often normal, contributing to diagnostic delay.
Laparoscopy with visualization and biopsy is the gold standard; CA-125 and ultrasound (for endometriomas) are supportive but not definitive.
Treatment depends on fertility goals: hormonal suppression for pain when not conceiving, surgical excision/ablation plus assisted reproduction when fertility is desired.
Definitive surgery (hysterectomy with BSO) is reserved for refractory pain after completed childbearing.

cases.title: Heavy, Painful Periods in Her Forties
cases.chiefComplaint: Progressively heavier and more painful menstrual periods over the last two years.
cases.history: A 42-year-old G3P3 woman presents with two years of worsening heavy menstrual bleeding and increasingly severe menstrual cramping. She soaks through a pad every two hours on her heaviest days, passes clots, and now describes a constant pelvic heaviness and dull ache that intensifies with menses. She also notes deep dyspareunia. She feels increasingly fatigued. Her cycles remain regular at about 27 days. She has completed childbearing; all three deliveries were vaginal. She denies intermenstrual or postcoital bleeding, fever, weight loss, or bowel/bladder dysfunction. Past medical history includes well-controlled hypothyroidism. Medications: levothyroxine 75 mcg daily and ibuprofen as needed for cramps with diminishing benefit. No known drug allergies. She does not smoke, drinks occasionally, and works as an accountant. Family history is notable for a sister with fibroids. Her last cervical cancer screening was normal one year ago. Review of systems is significant for fatigue and exertional shortness of breath, suggesting possible anemia from chronic blood loss.
cases.exam: The patient appears mildly pale but is in no distress. Conjunctivae are slightly pale. Cardiopulmonary exam reveals a soft flow murmur and is otherwise normal. The abdomen is soft and nontender without palpable masses. On bimanual pelvic exam the uterus is diffusely and symmetrically enlarged to approximately a 10-week size, with a characteristically soft, 'boggy,' globular consistency, and it is tender to palpation. There are no discrete, firm, irregular masses to suggest separate fibroid nodules, and no focal adnexal masses are palpated. The cervix appears normal with no lesions or discharge. There is no cervical motion tenderness. The uterosacral ligaments are not nodular. The overall picture is a uniformly enlarged, tender, boggy uterus.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which diagnosis best fits a diffusely enlarged, soft, 'boggy,' tender uterus with heavy painful menses in a parous 42-year-old?
options: Uterine leiomyomas
Adenomyosis
Pelvic inflammatory disease
Endometrial carcinoma
stages.explanation: Adenomyosis is endometrial glands and stroma within the myometrium, producing a diffusely and symmetrically enlarged, soft, 'boggy,' globular, and tender uterus, classically in multiparous women in their 40s with heavy menstrual bleeding (menorrhagia) and dysmenorrhea. Leiomyomas typically cause an asymmetrically enlarged, firm, irregular, nontender uterus with discrete nodules — different texture and contour. Endometrial carcinoma in this age group usually presents with abnormal/postmenopausal bleeding and is not characterized by a uniformly boggy tender uterus; she also lacks intermenstrual bleeding. Pelvic inflammatory disease is an acute infection with fever, discharge, and cervical motion tenderness, which she does not have. The uniform, boggy, tender enlargement is the signature of adenomyosis.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Hgb 9.8 g/dL, MCV 74 fL (microcytic)
Ferritin 9 ng/mL (low)
TSH 2.0 mIU/L
Urine hCG: negative
Transvaginal ultrasound: globular uterus, asymmetric myometrial thickening, heterogeneous myometrium with small cysts and indistinct endomyometrial junction; no discrete fibroids; endometrium 6 mm and homogeneous
MRI (when obtained): widened junctional zone >12 mm, consistent with adenomyosis

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: Which imaging finding is most characteristic of this condition and helps distinguish it from leiomyomas?
options: A globular uterus with myometrial cysts
A complex adnexal mass with septations as the immediate next step
A thickened endometrial stripe >11 mm as the immediate next step
Multiple well-circumscribed hypoechoic masses distorting the contour
stages.explanation: On transvaginal ultrasound and especially MRI, adenomyosis shows a globular uterus with heterogeneous myometrium, small myometrial cysts, and a thickened, indistinct junctional zone (widened junctional zone, typically >12 mm on MRI). These features reflect ectopic endometrial tissue within the myometrium. Multiple well-circumscribed hypoechoic masses distorting the uterine contour describe leiomyomas, which is the key distinction. A thickened endometrial stripe points toward endometrial pathology (hyperplasia/carcinoma), not adenomyosis; her endometrium is normal at 6 mm. A complex adnexal mass suggests an ovarian neoplasm, which is unrelated. The globular uterus with junctional-zone thickening is the hallmark imaging clue for adenomyosis.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: She has completed childbearing but wants to avoid major surgery if possible. What is the most appropriate initial management of her bleeding and pain (alongside treating her iron deficiency)?
options: Immediate hysterectomy as initial therapy
GnRH antagonist therapy indefinitely as initial therapy
Levonorgestrel intrauterine device or other hormonal suppression
Endometrial ablation as definitive cure as initial therapy
stages.explanation: For symptomatic adenomyosis in a patient wishing to avoid surgery, hormonal suppression is first-line — the levonorgestrel-releasing IUD is particularly effective at reducing menorrhagia and dysmenorrhea, and combined OCPs or progestins are alternatives; iron repletion addresses her deficiency anemia. Hysterectomy is the only definitive cure and is appropriate for refractory disease or strong patient preference, but it is not the initial step when she wants to avoid major surgery. Endometrial ablation is less reliable in adenomyosis because the disease extends into the myometrium beyond the ablation depth, so it is not a definitive cure. GnRH agents shrink the uterus and reduce symptoms but cause hypoestrogenic effects and bone loss, making them unsuitable for indefinite use. Hormonal suppression with the LNG-IUD is the best initial choice.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: If hormonal therapy fails to control her symptoms, what is the definitive treatment for adenomyosis?
options: Bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy alone
Uterine artery embolization as the gold-standard cure
Hysterectomy
Myomectomy with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: Hysterectomy is the definitive, curative treatment for adenomyosis because it removes the entire affected uterus; it is appropriate here as she has completed childbearing and has refractory symptoms. Myomectomy targets discrete fibroids and is not suited to diffuse adenomyosis, which lacks a clean surgical plane. Uterine artery embolization can improve symptoms in some patients but is not the established gold-standard cure for adenomyosis and has variable durability. Bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy alone removes the ovaries but leaves the diseased uterus in place, so it does not treat the adenomyosis and would cause surgical menopause unnecessarily. When fertility is no longer desired and medical therapy fails, hysterectomy is the definitive answer.

pearls: Adenomyosis classically affects multiparous women in their 40s with menorrhagia and dysmenorrhea and a diffusely enlarged, soft, boggy, tender, globular uterus.
MRI/ultrasound show a globular uterus with myometrial cysts and a thickened junctional zone (>12 mm on MRI); contrast with discrete, firm leiomyomas.
First-line therapy is hormonal suppression (levonorgestrel IUD, OCPs, progestins) plus iron repletion for anemia.
Hysterectomy is the only definitive cure and is reserved for refractory symptoms after childbearing is complete.

cases.title: Heavy Bleeding and Pelvic Pressure
cases.chiefComplaint: Heavy menstrual bleeding with pelvic pressure and increased urinary frequency.
cases.history: A 38-year-old G2P2 woman presents with about a year of progressively heavier menstrual bleeding. She soaks through pads and tampons together and passes large clots, with periods lasting eight days. She also reports a sense of pelvic fullness and pressure, urinary frequency from feeling like her bladder is being pushed on, and mild constipation. She denies intermenstrual or postcoital bleeding, weight loss, or fever, and her cycles remain regular. She has noticed her lower abdomen looks fuller. Her last cervical cancer screening was normal eight months ago. Past medical history is unremarkable. Medications: an over-the-counter iron supplement and ibuprofen during menses. No known drug allergies. She is of African descent (a known risk factor for fibroids), does not smoke, drinks rarely, and works as a nurse. Family history is notable for fibroids in her mother. She has two children, both delivered vaginally, and is not currently planning more pregnancies but wishes to retain her uterus if possible. Review of systems reveals fatigue.
cases.exam: The patient is in no acute distress and appears mildly fatigued. Conjunctivae are slightly pale. Cardiopulmonary exam is normal. The abdomen is soft with a palpable, firm, irregular lower abdominal/pelvic mass arising from the pelvis, nontender, extending toward the umbilicus. On bimanual pelvic exam the uterus is markedly enlarged, firm, and irregular ('lumpy-bumpy') with several discrete protuberant masses, estimated at a 14-week gestational size. There is no uterine tenderness. The cervix appears normal without lesions or discharge, and there is no cervical motion tenderness. The adnexa are difficult to assess due to the enlarged uterus but no separate masses are appreciated. The exam is most notable for an asymmetric, firm, irregularly enlarged, nontender uterus.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which diagnosis is most consistent with an asymmetric, firm, irregularly enlarged, nontender uterus plus heavy bleeding and bulk symptoms?
options: Adenomyosis in this clinical context
Uterine leiomyomas
Endometrial polyp
Ovarian carcinoma
stages.explanation: Uterine leiomyomas (fibroids) are benign smooth-muscle tumors and the most common pelvic tumor in women; they classically produce an asymmetrically enlarged, firm, irregular ('lumpy-bumpy'), nontender uterus with heavy menstrual bleeding and bulk symptoms (pelvic pressure, urinary frequency, constipation), as seen here, with risk factors of African ancestry and family history. Adenomyosis causes a diffusely enlarged, soft, boggy, tender uterus — a different texture and contour. An endometrial polyp causes abnormal bleeding but not a large irregular uterus or bulk symptoms. Ovarian carcinoma presents as an adnexal mass, often with ascites and constitutional symptoms, not an irregular enlarged uterus. The irregular, firm, nontender uterine enlargement is the classic sign of fibroids.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Hgb 10.4 g/dL, MCV 76 fL (microcytic)
Ferritin 11 ng/mL (low)
Urine hCG: negative
TSH 1.8 mIU/L
Transvaginal/transabdominal ultrasound: enlarged uterus with multiple well-circumscribed hypoechoic intramural and subserosal masses, largest 6 cm; one submucosal fibroid impinging on the endometrial cavity; ovaries normal
Endometrial sampling (given age and bleeding): benign endometrium, no hyperplasia

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: She has bothersome bleeding and bulk symptoms with iron deficiency but wants to keep her uterus. Which is the most appropriate management option to discuss first?
options: Medical/uterine-sparing therapy: iron repletion plus options such as a levonorgestrel IUD, GnRH agonist/antagonist, tranexamic acid, or myomectomy/UAE
Observation with no treatment despite anemia as initial therapy
Chemotherapy as initial therapy
Hysterectomy now as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Because she is symptomatic with anemia but wishes to preserve her uterus, uterus-sparing approaches are first-line to discuss: iron repletion plus medical options (levonorgestrel IUD or tranexamic acid for bleeding; GnRH agonists/antagonists to shrink fibroids and control bleeding, often as a bridge) and uterus-preserving procedures (myomectomy, uterine artery embolization). Hysterectomy is definitive but conflicts with her stated wish to keep her uterus, so it is not the first option offered. Observation is reasonable for asymptomatic fibroids, but she has significant symptoms and iron-deficiency anemia, so doing nothing is inappropriate. Chemotherapy has no role in benign leiomyomas. Tailoring uterine-sparing therapy to her symptoms and preferences is the best initial plan.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: She specifically wants to preserve fertility for a possible future pregnancy and has a submucosal fibroid distorting the cavity. Which intervention best fits?
options: Endometrial ablation as the immediate next step
Myomectomy
Uterine artery embolization in this setting
Hysterectomy as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Myomectomy preserves the uterus and fertility; a submucosal fibroid distorting the endometrial cavity is well suited to hysteroscopic resection, which can improve bleeding and fertility outcomes. Uterine artery embolization is generally not recommended for women desiring future pregnancy because of concerns about reduced ovarian reserve and pregnancy/placental complications. Endometrial ablation destroys the endometrium and is contraindicated when future pregnancy is desired. Hysterectomy eliminates the possibility of pregnancy entirely. Therefore, in a patient who wants to retain fertility, myomectomy is the procedure of choice, with the approach (hysteroscopic, laparoscopic, or open) determined by fibroid location and size.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which finding would be most concerning for a malignant process (leiomyosarcoma) rather than benign fibroids?
options: Improvement in size during pregnancy as the leading concern
Calcified fibroids in an older woman as the leading concern
Slow, steady growth of fibroids over several years as the leading concern
Rapid uterine/mass enlargement, especially new growth in a postmenopausal woman not on hormone therapy
stages.explanation: Rapid enlargement of a uterine mass — particularly new or growing fibroids in a postmenopausal woman not on hormone therapy (when fibroids should be regressing due to estrogen withdrawal) — raises concern for leiomyosarcoma, a rare malignant smooth-muscle tumor, and warrants further evaluation. Slow, steady growth over years is typical of benign fibroids. Fibroids are estrogen-responsive and often grow during pregnancy and regress after menopause, so the patterns of pregnancy-related change or postmenopausal calcification are expected benign behaviors. Although leiomyosarcoma is uncommon and most rapidly enlarging masses are still benign, the high-yield red flag is rapid growth, especially after menopause without hormonal stimulation.

pearls: Leiomyomas are the most common pelvic tumors in women; classic signs are an asymmetric, firm, irregular, nontender, enlarged uterus with menorrhagia and bulk symptoms. Risk factors include African ancestry and family history.
Asymptomatic fibroids can be observed; symptomatic patients are treated based on symptoms and fertility/uterine-preservation goals.
Uterine-sparing options include LNG-IUD, tranexamic acid, GnRH agonists/antagonists, myomectomy, and uterine artery embolization (avoid UAE and ablation when future fertility is desired).
Rapid uterine growth, especially in a postmenopausal woman off hormones, raises concern for leiomyosarcoma.

cases.title: Frequent, Heavy Bleeding in a Teenager
cases.chiefComplaint: Heavy, irregular vaginal bleeding for two weeks with dizziness and fatigue.
cases.history: A 15-year-old presents to the ED with her mother for two weeks of heavy, irregular vaginal bleeding. She reports soaking a pad every one to two hours at times and passing clots, with no clear pattern to her cycles. Her menarche was 10 months ago, and since then her periods have been unpredictable in timing and amount, sometimes skipping a month and other times bleeding for weeks. Today she felt lightheaded and nearly fainted when standing, prompting the visit. She denies abdominal pain, fever, easy bruising, gum bleeding, or a family history of bleeding disorders, and reports she has never been sexually active. She has no chronic illnesses. Medications: none. No known drug allergies. She does not use tobacco, alcohol, or drugs and is a high school student. Family history is unremarkable, with no thyroid disease or coagulopathy. Review of systems is positive for fatigue and lightheadedness and negative for galactorrhea, weight change, heat/cold intolerance, hirsutism, or acne, making thyroid disease and PCOS less likely on history.

vitals.BP: 104/64 mmHg (sitting); 92/58 with HR rise on standing

cases.exam: The patient is pale and tired-appearing but alert and oriented, with mild orthostatic signs. Conjunctivae and palmar creases are pale. Cardiopulmonary exam shows a regular tachycardia with a soft systolic flow murmur and clear lungs. The abdomen is soft, nontender, and without masses or organomegaly. There is no skin petechiae, ecchymosis, or mucosal bleeding to suggest a coagulopathy. External genital inspection shows blood at the introitus from the vaginal vault with no external lesions or trauma; given she is not sexually active and the presentation is consistent with a hormonal etiology, an internal speculum exam is deferred unless bleeding cannot be otherwise explained. There is no thyromegaly. The overall picture is of significant anemia from heavy bleeding without signs of trauma, infection, or coagulopathy.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely cause of this perimenarchal teenager's heavy irregular bleeding?
options: Anovulatory bleeding from an immature hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis
Threatened miscarriage as the primary diagnosis
Uterine leiomyoma as the primary diagnosis
Endometrial carcinoma as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: In the first 1–2 years after menarche, the hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian (HPO) axis is immature, so cycles are frequently anovulatory. Without ovulation there is no corpus luteum and no progesterone to stabilize and shed the endometrium uniformly, leading to unopposed estrogen, a thickened unstable endometrium, and irregular, often heavy bleeding — abnormal uterine bleeding due to ovulatory dysfunction. This is the most common cause at the perimenarchal extreme. Endometrial carcinoma is essentially unheard of at this age. Pregnancy/threatened miscarriage must be excluded with a pregnancy test, but she reports no sexual activity. Leiomyomas are rare in adolescents and cause heavy but typically more regular bleeding with an enlarged uterus. The immature HPO axis is the classic explanation here.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Urine hCG: negative
Hgb 7.6 g/dL, Hct 23%, MCV 72 fL (microcytic)
Platelets 280 ×10³/µL
PT/INR and aPTT: normal
Ferritin 6 ng/mL (low)
TSH 2.2 mIU/L; prolactin normal
von Willebrand panel: pending (sent given heavy bleeding since menarche)
Type and screen sent

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: Beyond a pregnancy test (already negative), which screening is especially important in an adolescent with heavy menstrual bleeding since menarche?
options: Screening for an inherited bleeding disorder
CA-125 as the immediate next step
Endometrial biopsy as the immediate next step
Pelvic CT scan as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Adolescents presenting with heavy menstrual bleeding from menarche should be screened for an inherited bleeding disorder — von Willebrand disease is the most common, found in a meaningful fraction of teens with severe menorrhagia — along with a CBC, platelets, and coagulation studies. This is why a von Willebrand panel was sent here. CA-125 is a nonspecific ovarian tumor marker with no role in this setting. Endometrial biopsy is used to evaluate for hyperplasia/carcinoma in older patients (typically over 45, or younger with risk factors), not in a 15-year-old. A pelvic CT scan is not the appropriate study; if imaging is needed, pelvic ultrasound is preferred. Excluding a coagulopathy is the key additional workup in adolescents.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: She is hemodynamically borderline with Hgb 7.6 g/dL and active heavy bleeding. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Discharge on scheduled NSAIDs alone as initial therapy
Emergent hysterectomy as initial therapy
Observation with reassurance only as initial therapy
Stabilize and start high-dose hormonal therapy to control bleeding, plus iron
stages.explanation: Acute heavy uterine bleeding with significant anemia and orthostasis requires stabilization — IV fluids, blood transfusion as needed — and prompt hormonal control of bleeding. High-dose estrogen (IV or oral) or high-dose combined oral contraceptives (taper over days) stabilize the endometrium and stop bleeding; oral progestins are an alternative. Iron supplementation treats the iron-deficiency anemia, and tranexamic acid can adjunctively reduce bleeding. NSAIDs alone are insufficient for this severity. Hysterectomy is never appropriate for anovulatory bleeding in a 15-year-old and would destroy fertility for a self-limited, hormonally treatable condition. Observation alone is unsafe given her hemodynamic borderline status and Hgb of 7.6. Stabilization plus hormonal therapy is the correct acute management.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Once bleeding is controlled and she is stable, what is the most appropriate longer-term plan?
options: Maintenance hormonal therapy to regulate cycles, continued iron repletion,
Endometrial ablation with appropriate follow-up
Lifelong GnRH agonist therapy with appropriate follow-up
No follow-up needed since this resolves on its own with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: After acute control, maintenance hormonal therapy — combined OCPs or cyclic progestins — regulates cycles and prevents recurrent heavy anovulatory bleeding while the HPO axis matures, and continued iron repletion corrects the deficiency anemia, with outpatient follow-up to monitor hemoglobin and review any bleeding-disorder results. Telling her no follow-up is needed is unsafe given recurrent bleeding risk and anemia. Lifelong GnRH agonist therapy causes hypoestrogenism and bone loss and is inappropriate in an adolescent. Endometrial ablation destroys the endometrium and eliminates future fertility, so it is contraindicated in a teenager. Bridging her with maintenance hormonal therapy and iron until her cycles mature is the appropriate, fertility-preserving plan.

cases.diagnosis: Abnormal uterine bleeding due to ovulatory dysfunction (anovulatory bleeding)
pearls: At the perimenarchal extreme, an immature HPO axis causes anovulation; unopposed estrogen yields an unstable endometrium and irregular heavy bleeding — the most common cause of AUB in adolescents.
Always exclude pregnancy first, and screen adolescents with heavy menses since menarche for inherited bleeding disorders (von Willebrand disease is most common).
Acute severe bleeding is managed by hemodynamic stabilization plus hormonal control (high-dose estrogen or OCPs; progestins/tranexamic acid as adjuncts) and iron repletion.
Maintenance hormonal therapy regulates cycles until the HPO axis matures; ablation and hysterectomy are inappropriate in adolescents.

cases.title: Bleeding After Menopause
cases.chiefComplaint: Two episodes of vaginal bleeding three years after her last menstrual period.
cases.history: A 55-year-old G2P2 postmenopausal woman presents with two episodes of light vaginal bleeding over the past month. Her last menstrual period was three years ago, and she has had no bleeding since until now. She describes spotting to light bleeding without pain. She denies postcoital bleeding, vaginal dryness severe enough to suggest atrophy alone, weight loss, or abdominal pain. She had late menopause and an early menarche, never used hormonal contraception, and is nulliparous-adjacent risk factors aside, has two children. Notably she has a history of obesity, type 2 diabetes, and chronic anovulation when younger. Past medical history: obesity (BMI 34), type 2 diabetes on metformin, hypertension on lisinopril. She took tamoxifen briefly years ago for a benign breast condition (an additional risk factor). No hormone replacement therapy. No known drug allergies. She does not smoke, drinks occasionally. Family history includes colon cancer in her father (Lynch-related concern) and breast cancer in an aunt. Review of systems is otherwise negative.
cases.exam: The patient is well-appearing and in no distress, with central obesity. Cardiopulmonary exam is normal. The abdomen is soft, obese, and nontender without palpable masses. On pelvic exam the external genitalia and vagina show mild atrophic changes; a small amount of old blood is present in the vaginal vault, and the source appears to be the cervical os. The cervix is normal in appearance without visible lesions or polyps. Bimanual exam reveals a normal-sized, nontender, mobile uterus and no adnexal masses or tenderness. There is no inguinal lymphadenopathy. There is no cervical motion tenderness. The exam does not reveal an obvious local source such as a vulvar, vaginal, or cervical lesion, focusing attention on the endometrium as the likely source of bleeding.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What principle should guide the initial approach to any postmenopausal bleeding?
options: Reassure and observe since postmenopausal bleeding is almost always benign atrophy
Postmenopausal bleeding is endometrial cancer until proven otherwise
Start hormone replacement therapy empirically as the first step today
Repeat cervical cytology and stop if normal as the first step today
stages.explanation: Any postmenopausal bleeding must be treated as endometrial carcinoma until proven otherwise and requires prompt evaluation, because although atrophy is the most common cause, roughly 5–10% or more of postmenopausal bleeding reflects endometrial cancer or its precursor (hyperplasia with atypia). Simply reassuring and observing risks missing a malignancy. Empiric hormone replacement therapy does not evaluate the bleeding and could worsen estrogen-driven pathology. A Pap test screens for cervical, not endometrial, disease and a normal result does not exclude endometrial cancer. The mandatory step is endometrial evaluation (transvaginal ultrasound and/or endometrial biopsy). This patient also carries multiple risk factors (obesity, diabetes, prior tamoxifen, possible Lynch), heightening concern.

stages.label: IMAGING & BIOPSY
stages.content:
Transvaginal ultrasound: endometrial stripe 12 mm, heterogeneous (postmenopausal threshold for concern is >4 mm)
Urine hCG: not applicable (postmenopausal)
Endometrial biopsy: complex (atypical) endometrial hyperplasia — endometrial intraepithelial neoplasia (EIN); no frank carcinoma identified on sampling
CBC and metabolic panel: unremarkable; HbA1c 7.4%

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The biopsy shows atypical endometrial hyperplasia (EIN). Why is this finding particularly important?
options: It indicates a cervical rather than endometrial process in this clinical context
It is a benign condition requiring no treatment as the primary diagnosis
It confirms a hormone-secreting ovarian tumor as the primary diagnosis
It carries a high risk of progression to,
stages.explanation: Atypical endometrial hyperplasia (endometrial intraepithelial neoplasia) is a true precancerous lesion: it carries a substantial risk of progressing to endometrial carcinoma, and notably a large proportion of patients with atypical hyperplasia on biopsy already harbor a concurrent endometrial carcinoma found at hysterectomy. This is what makes it so important and distinguishes it from hyperplasia without atypia, which has a much lower cancer risk. It is not benign and cannot be ignored. The atypia arises in the endometrium, not the cervix. It does not by itself confirm a hormone-secreting ovarian tumor, although chronic unopposed estrogen (from obesity-related peripheral aromatization, anovulation, or tamoxifen) is the underlying driver. The pre-malignant nature dictates aggressive management.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: She has completed childbearing and is a reasonable surgical candidate. What is the preferred definitive treatment for atypical endometrial hyperplasia?
options: Cyclic progestin therapy with repeat biopsy in one year
Total hysterectomy
Radiation therapy as initial therapy
Observation with serial ultrasound as initial therapy
stages.explanation: For atypical endometrial hyperplasia (EIN) in a patient who has completed childbearing and can tolerate surgery, total hysterectomy is the preferred definitive treatment because of the high risk of progression and the substantial chance of an occult concurrent carcinoma; bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy is typically considered, especially in postmenopausal women. Progestin therapy (oral or levonorgestrel IUD) with surveillance biopsies is reserved for women who wish to preserve fertility or who are poor surgical candidates — not the preferred route here since she has finished childbearing. Radiation is not used for hyperplasia. Observation alone is unsafe given the malignant potential. Hysterectomy both treats the lesion and allows full pathologic staging if cancer is found.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: If instead she strongly desired to avoid surgery (e.g., prohibitive surgical risk), what is the most appropriate alternative for atypical hyperplasia?
options: No treatment, routine follow-up only with appropriate follow-up
Estrogen-only therapy with appropriate follow-up
High-dose progestin therapy with close surveillance endometrial sampling
Combined estrogen-progestin oral contraceptives at standard dose without monitoring
stages.explanation: When surgery must be avoided (fertility preservation or prohibitive operative risk), high-dose progestin therapy — a levonorgestrel-releasing IUD or systemic oral progestins — is the alternative for atypical hyperplasia; progestins oppose estrogen and can induce regression, but it demands close surveillance with serial endometrial sampling because of the ongoing cancer risk. Offering no treatment is unsafe given the precancerous nature. Estrogen-only therapy is contraindicated because unopposed estrogen drives the very pathology causing the hyperplasia. Standard-dose combined oral contraceptives without monitoring provide insufficient progestin effect and omit the mandatory surveillance, so they are inadequate. The key is potent progestin therapy plus rigorous follow-up sampling.

cases.diagnosis: Endometrial hyperplasia with atypia (endometrial intraepithelial neoplasia)
pearls: Postmenopausal bleeding is endometrial cancer until proven otherwise; evaluate with transvaginal ultrasound (stripe >4 mm is concerning) and/or endometrial biopsy.
Atypical hyperplasia (EIN) is precancerous: high progression risk and a large fraction harbor concurrent carcinoma at hysterectomy.
Preferred treatment for atypical hyperplasia after childbearing is total hysterectomy (with consideration of BSO).
Progestin therapy (LNG-IUD or oral) with surveillance biopsy is the option for fertility preservation or poor surgical candidates; never use unopposed estrogen.

cases.title: Postmenopausal Bleeding in a Patient with Diabetes
cases.chiefComplaint: Recurrent vaginal bleeding that began several weeks ago, more than a decade after menopause.
cases.history: A 63-year-old G0 nulliparous woman presents with several weeks of intermittent vaginal bleeding, ranging from spotting to heavier flow with small clots. Her last menstrual period was at age 51. She has had no prior postmenopausal bleeding until now. She denies pain, fever, weight loss, or bowel/bladder symptoms, though she notes increasing fatigue. Notable history includes lifelong nulliparity, early menarche, obesity, type 2 diabetes, and hypertension — a cluster of estrogen-excess and metabolic risk factors. Past medical history: obesity (BMI 38), type 2 diabetes on metformin and insulin, hypertension, and hyperlipidemia. She has never used hormonal contraception or hormone therapy. Medications: metformin, basal insulin, lisinopril, atorvastatin. No known drug allergies. She does not smoke, drinks rarely, and is retired. Family history includes endometrial and colon cancer in relatives, raising concern for a possible hereditary syndrome (Lynch). Review of systems is otherwise unremarkable apart from fatigue. She is anxious about the recurrent bleeding and seeks evaluation.
cases.exam: The patient is obese and well-appearing, in no acute distress. Conjunctivae are slightly pale. Cardiopulmonary exam is normal aside from mild hypertension. The abdomen is obese, soft, and nontender without palpable masses or organomegaly. On pelvic exam the external genitalia and vagina show mild atrophy; dark blood is noted in the vault originating from the cervical os, with no visible vulvar, vaginal, or cervical lesion or polyp. The bimanual exam reveals a uterus that is normal-to-mildly enlarged, mobile, and nontender; no discrete adnexal masses are appreciated, though the exam is limited by body habitus. There is no inguinal or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy. The lack of a visible lower-tract source again directs attention to the endometrium as the source of bleeding.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given her risk profile and postmenopausal bleeding, what is the most appropriate initial diagnostic step?
options: Start combined hormone replacement therapy as the first step today
Empiric antibiotics for presumed endometritis as the first step today
Endometrial biopsy to evaluate for endometrial cancer
Reassurance and follow-up in 6 months as the first step today
stages.explanation: Postmenopausal bleeding mandates endometrial evaluation, and endometrial biopsy (often paired with transvaginal ultrasound) is the appropriate initial diagnostic step to assess for endometrial carcinoma — especially in this high-risk patient with obesity, diabetes, nulliparity, early menarche, and a family history suggesting Lynch syndrome. Reassurance and delayed follow-up risk missing a curable cancer. Empiric antibiotics treat a presumed infection she has no evidence of (no fever, discharge, or tenderness) and would delay diagnosis. Starting hormone replacement therapy neither evaluates the bleeding nor is appropriate before cancer is excluded, and added estrogen could worsen an estrogen-driven malignancy. Tissue sampling is essential to make the diagnosis.

stages.label: IMAGING & BIOPSY
stages.content:
Transvaginal ultrasound: thickened, heterogeneous endometrium measuring 16 mm with irregular echotexture
Endometrial biopsy: endometrioid adenocarcinoma, FIGO grade 1
CBC: Hgb 10.8 g/dL (mild anemia)
HbA1c 8.1%
Chest imaging and exam: no overt distant disease; further staging workup arranged

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: This is the most common gynecologic malignancy. Which type and mechanism does her profile best fit?
options: Type I endometrioid adenocarcinoma driven by unopposed estrogen excess
Cervical squamous cell carcinoma as the primary diagnosis
Uterine sarcoma as the primary diagnosis
Type II (serous) carcinoma from a p53 pathway, unrelated to estrogen
stages.explanation: Endometrial carcinoma is the most common gynecologic malignancy, and her presentation fits Type I (endometrioid) adenocarcinoma, which arises from prolonged unopposed estrogen stimulation — driven here by obesity (peripheral aromatization of androgens to estrogen in adipose tissue), nulliparity, early menarche/late menopause, diabetes, and chronic anovulation. Type I tumors are typically lower grade and estrogen-related, matching her grade 1 endometrioid histology. Type II (serous/clear cell) carcinomas are estrogen-independent, higher grade, associated with p53 mutations and endometrial atrophy, often in older thinner women — not her profile. Uterine sarcoma is rare and presents differently (rapid growth, distinct histology). Cervical squamous carcinoma is HPV-related and arises from the cervix, not the endometrium. Her risk factors classically point to estrogen-driven Type I disease.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the cornerstone of treatment for clinically early-stage endometrial carcinoma?
options: Observation until symptoms worsen as initial therapy
Surgical staging with total hysterectomy
Primary chemoradiation, avoiding surgery as initial therapy
Progestin therapy alone as definitive cure as initial therapy
stages.explanation: The cornerstone of treatment for endometrial carcinoma is surgical: total hysterectomy with bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy, along with surgical staging including lymph node assessment (and peritoneal evaluation), which both removes the tumor and determines the stage that guides adjuvant therapy. Adjuvant radiation and/or chemotherapy are added based on stage and risk factors but are not the primary modality for early disease. Progestin therapy alone is reserved for select young patients with grade 1 disease wishing to preserve fertility or for nonsurgical candidates — not as a definitive cure for most patients. Observation is inappropriate for a diagnosed malignancy. For this patient, surgical staging is the definitive initial treatment.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Given her family history of endometrial and colon cancers, what additional evaluation is warranted?
options: Immediate colectomy with appropriate follow-up
Evaluation for Lynch syndrome
No additional testing is needed with appropriate follow-up
BRCA1/2 testing only with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: Her personal endometrial cancer plus a family history of endometrial and colon cancers raises concern for Lynch syndrome (hereditary nonpolyposis colorectal cancer), so the tumor should undergo mismatch repair (MMR) immunohistochemistry/microsatellite instability (MSI) testing, with referral for genetic counseling and germline testing if indicated. Identifying Lynch syndrome affects her cancer surveillance (e.g., colonoscopy) and that of relatives. Doing no additional testing misses a heritable, actionable syndrome. BRCA1/2 testing relates chiefly to breast and ovarian cancer and is not the targeted evaluation for the endometrial–colon cancer pattern of Lynch. Immediate colectomy is not indicated without evidence of colon cancer; appropriate management is genetic evaluation and intensified screening. Screening for Lynch syndrome is the warranted next step.

pearls: Endometrial cancer is the most common gynecologic malignancy; postmenopausal bleeding is the cardinal symptom and must always be evaluated with biopsy/ultrasound.
Type I (endometrioid) cancer is driven by unopposed estrogen — obesity, nulliparity, early menarche/late menopause, diabetes, chronic anovulation, and tamoxifen are key risk factors.
Treatment of early-stage disease is surgical staging with total hysterectomy and bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy plus lymph node assessment; adjuvant therapy depends on stage.
A personal/family history of endometrial and colon cancer should prompt evaluation for Lynch syndrome with MMR/MSI testing and genetic counseling.

cases.title: Bleeding After Intercourse in a 45-Year-Old
cases.chiefComplaint: I keep bleeding a little after sex and sometimes in between my periods.
cases.history: A 45-year-old G3P3 woman presents with three months of intermittent postcoital bleeding and occasional intermenstrual spotting. The bleeding is light, bright red, and not associated with pain. She also notes a thin, foul-smelling, blood-tinged vaginal discharge over the past several weeks. She denies weight loss, flank pain, or urinary or bowel changes. Her menses remain regular. She has never had a Pap smear because she lacked insurance for years and 'felt fine.' She became sexually active at 16, has had multiple lifetime partners, and used condoms inconsistently. She has never received HPV vaccination. PMH is unremarkable. Medications: none. No allergies. She smokes one pack of cigarettes per day (25 pack-years) and drinks socially. She works as a restaurant server and is recently uninsured. Family history is noncontributory. She is in a new monogamous relationship and is worried because the bleeding embarrasses her partner.
cases.exam: Well-appearing, no acute distress, not pale. Abdomen soft, non-tender, no palpable masses, no costovertebral angle tenderness. Speculum exam reveals a 2.5 cm friable, ulcerated, exophytic lesion on the anterior cervical lip that bleeds readily on contact; a small amount of malodorous serosanguineous discharge is present. No active heavy bleeding. Bimanual exam shows a firm, somewhat irregular cervix; the uterus is normal size and mobile, no adnexal masses. Rectovaginal exam reveals smooth, non-nodular parametria with no extension to the pelvic sidewall. Inguinal lymph nodes are not enlarged.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A friable, exophytic cervical lesion is identified on speculum exam. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Perform a punch biopsy of the visible cervical lesion
Repeat the Pap smear in 12 months and reassure her
Start topical estrogen cream for presumed atrophic changes
Prescribe empiric antibiotics for cervicitis and reassess in 6 weeks
stages.explanation: A grossly visible, friable cervical lesion must be biopsied directly; a Pap smear is a screening test for asymptomatic women and can be falsely negative when necrotic tumor obscures cells, so it cannot rule out invasive cancer. Direct punch biopsy of the lesion provides tissue for histologic diagnosis and is the correct next step. Repeating cytology in a year dangerously delays diagnosis of a suspicious mass. Empiric antibiotics treat presumed infection but ignore a lesion with classic malignant features and waste critical time. Topical estrogen addresses atrophic vaginitis, which does not produce a friable exophytic mass in a menstruating 45-year-old. Any visible lesion warrants biopsy regardless of cytology results.

stages.label: BIOPSY & WORKUP
stages.content:
Cervical punch biopsy: invasive squamous cell carcinoma, moderately differentiated
HPV testing: positive for high-risk HPV (type 16)
Hemoglobin 11.8 g/dL
Creatinine 0.8 mg/dL
Urinalysis: no hematuria
Pelvic exam under anesthesia: lesion confined to cervix, ~2.5 cm; no vaginal, parametrial, or pelvic sidewall involvement
Pelvic MRI: 2.6 cm enhancing cervical mass; no parametrial invasion; no pelvic lymphadenopathy
Chest imaging: clear

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Based on a 2.6 cm tumor confined to the cervix with no parametrial or sidewall involvement and negative nodes on imaging, this is best classified as which FIGO stage category?
options: Stage IIB (parametrial invasion) in this clinical context
Stage IA (microscopic disease only)
Stage IB
Stage IVA (invasion of bladder or rectum)
stages.explanation: A clinically visible tumor confined to the cervix is FIGO stage IB; the 2.6 cm size places it in the IB2 subcategory (2-4 cm). Stage IA is reserved for microscopic, biopsy-only disease not visible to the naked eye, which does not fit a 2.6 cm exophytic mass. Stage IIB requires parametrial invasion, explicitly absent on exam and MRI. Stage IVA requires invasion of the bladder or rectal mucosa, also absent here. Cervical cancer is staged using FIGO criteria, now incorporating imaging and pathology; correct staging drives whether treatment is surgical (early stage) or chemoradiation (locally advanced).

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: For this woman with a stage IB squamous cell carcinoma confined to the cervix and no nodal disease, which treatment is most appropriate?
options: Simple hysterectomy alone as initial therapy
Cryotherapy ablation of the cervical lesion
Radical hysterectomy with pelvic lymphadenectomy
Primary chemotherapy with carboplatin and paclitaxel alone
stages.explanation: Early-stage cervical cancer (IB) confined to the cervix is treated with radical hysterectomy plus pelvic lymphadenectomy, which removes the uterus, parametria, upper vagina, and regional nodes; definitive chemoradiation is an equally effective alternative. Cryotherapy is an ablative treatment for preinvasive disease (CIN) and cannot treat invasive cancer. A simple hysterectomy does not remove the parametria or nodes and is inadequate for frankly invasive stage IB disease. Chemotherapy alone is not curative for localized cervical cancer; cytotoxic chemotherapy is used as a radiosensitizer with radiation for advanced disease or in the palliative/metastatic setting, not as monotherapy for early operable disease.

stages.label: PREVENTION
stages.question: Which intervention would most likely have prevented this patient from developing invasive cervical cancer?
options: Long-term combined oral contraceptive use in this setting
Prophylactic antibiotics for recurrent vaginitis
Routine cervical cancer screening with Pap
Annual transvaginal ultrasound surveillance
stages.explanation: The overwhelming majority of cervical cancers occur in under-screened or never-screened women; this patient never had a Pap test. Routine screening with cytology and high-risk HPV testing detects precancerous CIN and early disease when it is curable, and is the single most effective prevention strategy (alongside HPV vaccination). Transvaginal ultrasound evaluates the uterus and ovaries but does not detect cervical dysplasia or early cancer. Combined oral contraceptives are actually associated with a modestly increased cervical cancer risk with long-term use and confer no protection. Prophylactic antibiotics do nothing to prevent HPV-driven neoplasia. Screening plus HPV vaccination are the cornerstones of prevention.

cases.diagnosis: Invasive squamous cell carcinoma of the cervix (FIGO stage IB), HPV-16 associated
pearls: Any grossly visible cervical lesion must be biopsied directly; a Pap smear can be falsely negative and does not replace biopsy of a visible mass.
Postcoital bleeding in an unscreened woman is cervical cancer until proven otherwise.
Persistent high-risk HPV (especially types 16 and 18) drives nearly all cervical squamous cell carcinoma; smoking is a synergistic cofactor.
Early-stage disease (IA-IB) is managed surgically with radical hysterectomy and lymphadenectomy, while locally advanced disease is treated with concurrent chemoradiation.

cases.title: Abnormal Pap Result in a 31-Year-Old
cases.chiefComplaint: My doctor said my Pap smear came back abnormal and sent me here for a closer look.
cases.history: A 31-year-old G1P1 woman is referred to colposcopy clinic after a routine cervical cancer screen returned abnormal. Her co-test showed high-grade squamous intraepithelial lesion (HSIL) cytology with a positive high-risk HPV result genotyped to HPV 16. She is asymptomatic, with no abnormal bleeding, discharge, or pain, and normal regular menses. Her last Pap two years prior was reported as ASC-US with a positive HPV test, but she missed the recommended follow-up after moving. She was never vaccinated against HPV. She became sexually active at 18, has had six lifetime partners, and is currently in a monogamous relationship. PMH is unremarkable. Medications: a combined oral contraceptive pill. No allergies. She smokes half a pack of cigarettes daily. She drinks alcohol occasionally and uses no other drugs. She works as a graphic designer. Family history is noncontributory. She has completed childbearing but has not decided definitively against future pregnancy and is anxious about what the procedure will involve.
cases.exam: Well-appearing and in no distress. Abdomen soft and non-tender with no palpable masses. External genitalia normal without lesions or condylomata. Speculum exam shows a grossly normal-appearing cervix with no visible mass, ulceration, or friability, and physiologic clear discharge. Bimanual exam reveals a normal-sized, mobile, non-tender uterus and no adnexal masses or tenderness. No inguinal lymphadenopathy. There is no cervical motion tenderness.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: With HSIL cytology and HPV-16 positivity but a grossly normal cervix, what is the most appropriate next step in this clinic?
options: Empiric cryotherapy of the entire cervix as the first step today
Colposcopy with directed biopsies of any acetowhite or abnormal areas
Repeat the Pap smear in 12 months as the first step today
Proceed directly to total hysterectomy as the first step today
stages.explanation: HSIL cytology carries a substantial risk of underlying high-grade dysplasia or occult cancer and warrants colposcopy with directed biopsies to obtain histologic confirmation and map the lesion. Colposcopy uses acetic acid (and often Lugol iodine) to highlight abnormal acetowhite epithelium, punctation, mosaicism, and atypical vessels for targeted biopsy. Hysterectomy is never the initial response to an abnormal Pap without a tissue diagnosis and is far too aggressive for preinvasive disease. Repeating cytology in a year inappropriately delays evaluation of a high-grade result. Empiric ablation without first confirming histology and excluding invasive cancer is improper because ablation destroys tissue needed for diagnosis. Colposcopy with biopsy is the standard of care.

stages.label: COLPOSCOPY & BIOPSY
stages.content:
Colposcopy: satisfactory exam, entire squamocolumnar junction visualized
Dense acetowhite epithelium with coarse punctation at the 2-o'clock to 5-o'clock position; no atypical vessels, no ulceration
Directed cervical biopsy: cervical intraepithelial neoplasia grade 3 (CIN 3), no invasion identified
Endocervical curettage: negative for dysplasia
Negative pregnancy test

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The biopsy shows CIN 3. Which statement best characterizes this finding?
options: It is a benign reactive change requiring no further treatment
It is a high-grade precancerous lesion confined to the epithelium
It is an invasive cancer that has breached the basement membrane
It represents low-grade dysplasia that will almost always regress
stages.explanation: CIN 3 is a high-grade squamous intraepithelial lesion in which dysplastic cells occupy the full thickness of the epithelium but have not breached the basement membrane; it encompasses carcinoma in situ and is a true cancer precursor. It is not invasive cancer, which by definition penetrates the basement membrane into the stroma; the endocervical curettage and biopsy showed no invasion. It is not a benign reactive change and cannot be ignored, as a meaningful proportion progresses to invasive cancer if untreated. It is not low-grade (CIN 1) disease, which is associated with transient HPV infection and frequently regresses; CIN 3 has a high progression risk and warrants treatment.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: For this 31-year-old with biopsy-proven CIN 3 and a fully visualized squamocolumnar junction, what is the most appropriate management?
options: Excisional treatment with a loop electrosurgical excision procedure
Observation with repeat co-testing in 6 months as initial therapy
Total abdominal hysterectomy as initial therapy
HPV vaccination as definitive treatment of the lesion
stages.explanation: Confirmed CIN 3 in a non-pregnant adult is treated, and an excisional procedure such as LEEP (or cold-knife conization) is preferred over ablation because it removes the transformation zone while providing a specimen to exclude occult invasion. Observation is inappropriate for high-grade CIN 3 given its substantial progression risk. Hysterectomy is not first-line for CIN 3 and is overtreatment, particularly in a woman who has not firmly ruled out future fertility; it is reserved for select cases (e.g., recurrent disease, positive margins not amenable to re-excision, or coexisting indications). HPV vaccination prevents new infections and reduces recurrence risk but does not treat an established lesion. LEEP is the standard excisional treatment.

stages.label: FOLLOW-UP
stages.question: After a LEEP with negative margins for CIN 3, what is the most appropriate post-treatment surveillance?
options: Annual transvaginal ultrasound for 5 years before discharge planning
HPV-based testing at 6 months, then continued surveillance
No further screening is needed because the lesion was removed
Immediate repeat LEEP in 3 months regardless of results
stages.explanation: Even after excision with negative margins, women treated for CIN 3 remain at elevated long-term risk of recurrence and of developing cervical cancer, so HPV-based surveillance (co-testing) beginning around 6 months post-treatment and continuing for many years is recommended. Stopping screening entirely is unsafe given the persistent risk. Transvaginal ultrasound images the uterus and adnexa but cannot detect cervical dysplasia or recurrence and is not a surveillance tool here. A routine repeat LEEP in the absence of recurrent disease or positive margins is unnecessary and exposes the patient to risks such as cervical stenosis and preterm birth in future pregnancies. HPV-based co-testing is the correct surveillance strategy.

cases.diagnosis: Cervical intraepithelial neoplasia grade 3 (CIN 3, high-grade squamous intraepithelial lesion), HPV-16 associated
pearls: HSIL cytology mandates colposcopy with directed biopsy; never ablate or treat without histologic confirmation that excludes invasion.
CIN 3 (carcinoma in situ) is full-thickness epithelial dysplasia without basement membrane invasion and is a true precancer requiring treatment.
Excision (LEEP or cold-knife cone) is preferred over ablation for high-grade CIN because it yields a specimen to rule out occult invasive cancer.
Treated CIN 3 confers prolonged elevated cancer risk, so HPV-based surveillance must continue for years even after negative margins.

cases.title: Months of Bloating and Feeling Full Quickly
cases.chiefComplaint: My belly has been bloated for months and I get full after just a few bites.
cases.history: A 61-year-old G2P2 postmenopausal woman presents with four months of progressive abdominal bloating, early satiety, and a sense of increasing abdominal girth despite a 4 kg weight loss. She describes vague lower abdominal and pelvic discomfort and increased urinary frequency. She initially attributed the symptoms to 'getting older' and changes in diet. She denies vaginal bleeding, fevers, or hematochezia, though she notes mild constipation. Menopause occurred at age 52; she never used hormone therapy. Menarche was at age 11 and she is nulliparous by choice until two late pregnancies. She has never used oral contraceptives. PMH includes hypertension treated with lisinopril 20 mg daily and hyperlipidemia on atorvastatin 20 mg daily. No allergies. She does not smoke and drinks wine occasionally. She works as an accountant. Family history is striking: her mother had breast cancer at 48 and a maternal aunt had ovarian cancer; genetic testing was never pursued. She is increasingly anxious that her clothes no longer fit around the waist.
cases.exam: Appears mildly fatigued but in no acute distress. Abdomen is distended with a fluid wave and shifting dullness consistent with ascites; there is mild diffuse lower abdominal tenderness without rebound or guarding. A firm, irregular, fixed mass is palpable in the right lower quadrant/pelvis. No hepatosplenomegaly appreciated beneath the ascites. Pelvic exam reveals a firm, nodular, fixed right adnexal mass; the uterus is small and atrophic. Rectovaginal exam confirms a nodular cul-de-sac. No inguinal or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy. Lungs are clear; mild dependent lower-extremity edema is present.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: In this postmenopausal woman with bloating, early satiety, ascites, and a fixed pelvic mass, what is the most appropriate initial diagnostic study?
options: Transvaginal and abdominal pelvic ultrasound with a serum CA-125
Colonoscopy as the sole initial workup as the first step today
Upper endoscopy to evaluate early satiety in this clinical context
Empiric trial of a proton pump inhibitor and high-fiber diet
stages.explanation: The combination of bloating, early satiety, abdominal/pelvic pain, and urinary symptoms in a postmenopausal woman is a classic ovarian cancer symptom cluster, and the presence of ascites with a fixed pelvic mass heightens concern. Transvaginal/abdominal ultrasound characterizes the adnexal mass (size, solid components, septations, vascularity), and serum CA-125 aids risk assessment, making this the correct first step. Upper endoscopy and a PPI trial pursue a gastric cause and ignore the palpable pelvic mass and ascites. Colonoscopy alone evaluates the colon and may be part of a metastatic workup later, but it is not the appropriate initial study for a pelvic mass with ascites. Pelvic imaging plus CA-125 directly targets the most likely diagnosis.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
CA-125  640 U/mL (normal <35)
Transvaginal/abdominal ultrasound: 9 cm complex right adnexal mass with solid components, thick septations, and internal vascularity; moderate-to-large volume ascites
CT abdomen/pelvis: complex right pelvic mass, omental caking, peritoneal implants, moderate ascites; no liver parenchymal lesions; no pleural effusion
Hemoglobin 10.9 g/dL
Albumin 3.1 g/dL
CEA 2.1 ng/mL (normal)
CA 19-9 18 U/mL (normal)

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Imaging shows a complex solid-cystic adnexal mass with omental caking, peritoneal implants, and a markedly elevated CA-125. Which feature most strongly favors a primary epithelial ovarian carcinoma over a metastatic gastrointestinal tumor?
options: The patient's postmenopausal status alone as the primary diagnosis
A markedly elevated CA-125 with normal CEA
The presence of ascites as the primary diagnosis
The presence of bilateral pleural effusions as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: A dominant complex adnexal mass with peritoneal carcinomatosis and omental caking in the setting of a very high CA-125 with normal CEA and CA 19-9 favors a primary epithelial ovarian (or tubal/peritoneal) carcinoma; gastrointestinal primaries metastatic to the ovary (Krukenberg tumors from gastric or colon cancer) more often show elevated CEA/CA 19-9 and bilateral ovarian involvement. Ascites is nonspecific and occurs with both. Postmenopausal status raises malignancy risk generally but does not distinguish primary from metastatic ovarian disease. Pleural effusions are absent here and, when present, indicate stage IV disease rather than tissue of origin. The marker pattern plus the adnexal-dominant carcinomatosis points to primary ovarian epithelial cancer.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given suspected advanced epithelial ovarian carcinoma with carcinomatosis, what is the most appropriate management approach?
options: Therapeutic paracentesis alone with hospice referral as initial therapy
Pelvic radiation as primary therapy as initial therapy
Image-guided percutaneous biopsy of the ovary followed by observation as initial therapy
Cytoreductive surgery with comprehensive staging, plus platinum-based chemotherapy
stages.explanation: Advanced epithelial ovarian cancer is treated with maximal cytoreductive surgery (aiming for no residual disease) combined with platinum/taxane chemotherapy; when upfront optimal debulking is not feasible or the patient is a poor surgical candidate, neoadjuvant chemotherapy with interval debulking is the accepted alternative. Percutaneous biopsy of an intact ovarian mass is generally avoided in operable disease because of rupture/seeding concerns, and observation is never appropriate for carcinomatosis. Radiation is not primary therapy for epithelial ovarian cancer, which is chemosensitive and surgically managed. Paracentesis and hospice alone forgo potentially effective, even life-prolonging, treatment in a patient without evidence of untreatable end-stage disease. Surgery plus platinum-based chemotherapy is the standard of care.

stages.label: RISK FACTORS
stages.question: Given her family history of early breast cancer and ovarian cancer, which recommendation is most appropriate?
options: No genetic evaluation is warranted because she already has cancer as the immediate next step
Recommend prophylactic mastectomy before any genetic testing as the immediate next step
Reassure her that ovarian cancer is never hereditary as the immediate next step
Germline genetic testing for BRCA1/BRCA2 , which has therapeutic
stages.explanation: All women with epithelial ovarian cancer should be offered germline genetic testing for BRCA1/BRCA2 and other hereditary cancer genes regardless of family history; in this woman with early-onset breast cancer and ovarian cancer in relatives, the pretest probability is especially high. Identifying a BRCA mutation has direct therapeutic implications (PARP inhibitor maintenance therapy) and critical implications for at-risk relatives (cascade testing, risk-reducing salpingo-oophorectomy). Having cancer does not obviate testing; it strengthens the indication. Ovarian cancer is frequently hereditary, with BRCA mutations accounting for a substantial fraction, so blanket reassurance is incorrect. Prophylactic mastectomy is a downstream consideration only after testing and counseling, never a substitute for it.

stages.label: HISTOLOGY
stages.question: Surgical pathology returns. Which histologic subtype accounts for the majority of epithelial ovarian cancers and is most strongly associated with BRCA mutations and a markedly elevated CA-125?
options: High-grade serous carcinoma
Endometrioid carcinoma
Clear cell carcinoma
Mucinous carcinoma in this setting
