stages.explanation: High-grade serous carcinoma is the most common epithelial ovarian cancer subtype, frequently arises from the fallopian tube fimbria, is strongly associated with BRCA1/BRCA2 mutations and TP53 alterations, and characteristically presents with a very high CA-125 and advanced-stage disease with carcinomatosis, exactly as seen here. Mucinous carcinoma is less common, often unilateral and large, and tends to elevate CEA more than CA-125; a GI primary must be excluded. Clear cell carcinoma is associated with endometriosis and a worse chemosensitivity profile but is far less common. Endometrioid carcinoma is also linked to endometriosis and may coexist with endometrial cancer, but it does not predominate. High-grade serous is the dominant, BRCA-associated subtype.

cases.diagnosis: Advanced epithelial ovarian carcinoma (likely high-grade serous) with peritoneal carcinomatosis
pearls: Bloating, early satiety, pelvic/abdominal pain, and urinary urgency are the classic ovarian cancer symptom cluster, often dismissed as benign GI complaints until advanced.
A postmenopausal woman with a complex adnexal mass, ascites, and elevated CA-125 has ovarian cancer until proven otherwise.
Elevated CEA/CA 19-9 with bilateral ovarian masses should raise suspicion for a metastatic GI primary (Krukenberg tumor) rather than primary ovarian cancer.
Every woman with epithelial ovarian cancer warrants germline BRCA testing for treatment (PARP inhibitors) and family cascade screening.

cases.title: Sudden Severe One-Sided Pelvic Pain
cases.chiefComplaint: I had sudden, terrible pain on my right side that came out of nowhere a few hours ago.
cases.history: A 22-year-old G0 woman presents to the emergency department with three hours of sudden-onset, severe, sharp right lower quadrant and pelvic pain that began abruptly while she was at the gym. The pain is intermittent, comes in waves, and radiates to her right flank and groin. She has vomited twice and feels nauseated. She denies fever, vaginal bleeding, dysuria, or diarrhea. Her last menstrual period was two weeks ago and is regular. She is sexually active with one partner and uses condoms. She recalls being told months ago of a 'cyst' on her right ovary on an ultrasound done for unrelated abdominal pain. PMH is otherwise unremarkable. Medications: none. No allergies. She does not smoke, drinks alcohol occasionally, and uses no drugs. She is a college student and competitive volleyball player. Family history is noncontributory. She rates the pain 9 out of 10 and is unable to find a comfortable position on the stretcher, repeatedly drawing her knees up.
cases.exam: Appears in significant distress, restless and writhing with pain. Abdomen is soft with marked tenderness in the right lower quadrant; there is voluntary guarding but no rebound or rigidity. A vague, tender fullness is palpable in the right adnexal region. Pelvic exam reveals severe right adnexal tenderness with a palpable, enlarged, tender right adnexa; the cervix appears normal with no discharge and only mild cervical motion tenderness. The uterus is normal-sized and the left adnexa is non-tender. Bowel sounds are present. No costovertebral angle tenderness.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: In this woman with acute severe unilateral pelvic pain and a tender adnexal fullness, what is the single most important immediate test before imaging?
options: Erythrocyte sedimentation rate
Pelvic CT with contrast
Serum CA-125 as the first step today
Urine or serum beta-hCG
stages.explanation: Any woman of reproductive age with acute pelvic pain must have a pregnancy test first, because ectopic pregnancy is a life-threatening cause that radically changes management and must be excluded before proceeding. The beta-hCG result is also needed to interpret subsequent imaging and to plan any intervention. CA-125 is nonspecific and unhelpful in acute torsion, often falsely elevated by any peritoneal irritation, and never the immediate priority. An ESR is a nonspecific inflammatory marker that does not direct acute management. Pelvic CT exposes a young woman to radiation and is not the preferred study for adnexal pathology; transvaginal ultrasound with Doppler is the imaging of choice once pregnancy status is known. Pregnancy testing comes first.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Urine beta-hCG: negative
WBC 11.6 ×10³/µL
Hemoglobin 13.4 g/dL
Urinalysis: unremarkable
Transvaginal ultrasound with Doppler: enlarged right ovary measuring 6.5 cm with peripheral displacement of follicles; an 5 cm cystic structure within it; the ovary appears edematous
Doppler: markedly diminished/absent arterial and venous flow to the right ovary; normal flow to the left ovary
Small amount of free pelvic fluid

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The ultrasound shows an enlarged, edematous right ovary with peripherally displaced follicles and absent Doppler flow. What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Pelvic inflammatory disease
Ruptured ectopic pregnancy
Acute appendicitis
Ovarian (adnexal) torsion
stages.explanation: An enlarged, edematous ovary with peripherally displaced follicles ('string of pearls') and absent or markedly diminished Doppler flow, in the setting of sudden severe colicky unilateral pain with nausea, is the classic picture of ovarian torsion, usually precipitated by an underlying mass/cyst that acts as a lead point. A ruptured ectopic is excluded by the negative pregnancy test. Appendicitis can mimic right-sided pain but would not produce an enlarged ovary with absent ovarian Doppler flow, and typically shows a dilated noncompressible appendix. PID causes bilateral pain, cervical motion tenderness, discharge, and often fever, not an isolated avascular enlarged ovary. The imaging and presentation point squarely to torsion.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Start broad-spectrum IV antibiotics and reassess as monotherapy
Admit for observation and repeat ultrasound in 24 hours
Emergent diagnostic laparoscopy with detorsion of the ovary
Discharge with oral analgesics and outpatient gynecology follow-up
stages.explanation: Ovarian torsion is a surgical emergency; prolonged ischemia causes irreversible necrosis and loss of the ovary, so emergent laparoscopy with detorsion to restore perfusion is indicated. In young women, the ovary is untwisted and conserved whenever possible (even if it appears dusky), and the underlying cyst is addressed (cystectomy) once perfusion returns. Observation with delayed imaging risks losing the ovary while waiting. Discharge with analgesia leaves a surgical emergency untreated. Antibiotics treat infection, not a mechanical vascular emergency, and would only delay definitive operative care. Note that Doppler flow can sometimes be present in early or intermittent torsion, so a normal Doppler does not exclude it when the clinical picture fits; here flow is absent and surgery is clearly indicated.

stages.label: INTRAOPERATIVE DECISION
stages.question: At laparoscopy, the right ovary is twisted and appears dusky/cyanotic with a 5 cm benign-appearing cyst. In this 22-year-old, what is the preferred intraoperative management?
options: Close without intervention and plan elective surgery later in this setting
Detorsion with ovarian conservation
Immediate oophorectomy because the ovary looks ischemic in this setting
Detorsion followed by fixation of both ovaries to the pelvic sidewall as routine
stages.explanation: In a young woman desiring fertility preservation, the ovary should be detorsed and conserved whenever feasible, even when it appears dusky or cyanotic, because the gross appearance correlates poorly with viability and many such ovaries recover function after reperfusion; the lead-point cyst is then removed by cystectomy. Immediate oophorectomy based on appearance alone sacrifices a potentially viable ovary and is reserved for frankly necrotic or malignant findings. Routine bilateral oophoropexy is not standard; fixation may be considered selectively (e.g., recurrent torsion or absent contralateral ovary) but is not performed reflexively on every case. Closing without treating leaves the torsion and cyst unaddressed. Conservative detorsion with cystectomy is preferred.

cases.diagnosis: Ovarian (adnexal) torsion of the right ovary, with an underlying ovarian cyst as the lead point
pearls: Sudden severe unilateral pelvic pain with nausea and an enlarged, edematous ovary on ultrasound is torsion until proven otherwise; an underlying cyst or mass is the usual lead point.
Always obtain a beta-hCG first in any reproductive-age woman with acute pelvic pain to exclude ectopic pregnancy.
Doppler flow may be preserved in early or intermittent torsion, so normal flow does NOT exclude the diagnosis when the clinical picture fits.
Torsion is a surgical emergency: detorse and conserve the ovary (even if it looks dusky) to preserve fertility, removing the underlying cyst.

cases.title: Sharp Pelvic Pain After Intercourse
cases.chiefComplaint: I suddenly got a sharp pain low in my belly during sex a few hours ago and it hasn't fully gone away.
cases.history: A 25-year-old G0 woman presents to the emergency department with sudden-onset sharp right lower quadrant pain that began during intercourse approximately four hours ago. The pain was initially intense but has since dulled to a persistent ache; it does not radiate. She has mild nausea but no vomiting, no fever, no vaginal bleeding, no dysuria, and no diarrhea. Her last menstrual period was about two weeks ago, and her cycles are regular at 28 days. She is sexually active with one partner and uses combined oral contraceptives inconsistently, occasionally missing pills. She has had no prior episodes like this. PMH is unremarkable; she has had no surgeries. Medications: combined oral contraceptive pill (used inconsistently). No allergies. She does not smoke, drinks socially, and uses no drugs. She works as a barista and exercises regularly. Family history is noncontributory. She is mildly anxious but states the pain is now tolerable, around 4 out of 10, and improves when she lies still.
cases.exam: Well-appearing, comfortable at rest, in no acute distress. Abdomen is soft with mild tenderness in the right lower quadrant; no rebound, no guarding, and no rigidity. No palpable masses. Pelvic exam reveals mild right adnexal tenderness without a discrete mass; the cervix appears normal with no discharge and no significant cervical motion tenderness. The uterus is normal-sized and non-tender, and the left adnexa is non-tender. Bowel sounds are normal. There is no costovertebral angle tenderness. Skin is warm and well perfused with no pallor.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: For this hemodynamically stable woman with acute pelvic pain after intercourse, what is the most appropriate initial workup?
options: Immediate exploratory laparotomy
Discharge with reassurance and no testing
Urine beta-hCG and transvaginal ultrasound
CT abdomen/pelvis with oral contrast as the first study
stages.explanation: In any reproductive-age woman with acute pelvic pain, the first steps are a pregnancy test (to exclude ectopic) and pelvic ultrasound to characterize the adnexa and look for free fluid; this combination is the appropriate initial workup. Exploratory laparotomy is far too aggressive for a stable patient before any imaging or pregnancy testing. CT exposes a young woman to unnecessary radiation and is not the preferred modality for adnexal/gynecologic pathology, where transvaginal ultrasound is superior. Discharging without any evaluation is unsafe because ectopic pregnancy, torsion, and other serious causes have not been excluded. Beta-hCG plus ultrasound efficiently narrows the differential while keeping radiation exposure low.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Urine beta-hCG: negative
Hemoglobin 12.8 g/dL
WBC 8.4 ×10³/µL
Transvaginal ultrasound: right ovary with a collapsing complex cyst showing internal echoes/retracting clot; normal arterial and venous Doppler flow to both ovaries
Moderate free fluid in the pelvis/cul-de-sac without internal echoes suggestive of large hemoperitoneum
Uterus normal; no adnexal torsion features

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given a negative pregnancy test, a collapsing complex ovarian cyst, free pelvic fluid, and normal Doppler flow, what is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Ectopic pregnancy
Ruptured ovarian cyst
Acute appendicitis
Ovarian torsion
stages.explanation: Mid-cycle acute pain precipitated by intercourse, a negative pregnancy test, a collapsing/complex ovarian cyst with free pelvic fluid, and preserved Doppler flow are classic for a ruptured ovarian (hemorrhagic) cyst, often a ruptured corpus luteum cyst around two weeks into the cycle. Torsion would show an enlarged, edematous ovary with diminished or absent Doppler flow, which is not present here. Ectopic pregnancy is excluded by the negative beta-hCG. Appendicitis typically presents with periumbilical pain migrating to the right lower quadrant, anorexia, fever, and a dilated appendix on imaging, not a collapsing ovarian cyst with pelvic free fluid. The clinical and sonographic picture points to a ruptured hemorrhagic cyst.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: She remains hemodynamically stable with a normal hemoglobin and tolerable pain. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Admit for IV antibiotics for presumed pelvic infection
Conservative management with analgesia
Emergent surgical exploration and cystectomy
Methotrexate administration as initial therapy
stages.explanation: A ruptured hemorrhagic cyst in a hemodynamically stable woman with a normal hemoglobin and controlled pain is managed conservatively with oral analgesia, reassurance, and outpatient follow-up, as most resolve spontaneously. Surgery (laparoscopy with hemostasis) is reserved for hemodynamic instability, signs of significant ongoing hemorrhage, or a falling hemoglobin, none of which are present. Antibiotics are not indicated because this is a hemorrhagic, not infectious, process; there is no fever, leukocytosis, or cervical motion tenderness. Methotrexate treats ectopic pregnancy, which has been excluded by the negative beta-hCG. Conservative outpatient management with return precautions is correct.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which return precaution is most important to give this patient at discharge?
options: Return only if she develops a fever with appropriate follow-up
Return only after she misses her next menstrual period based on current risk
Return immediately for worsening pain, dizziness, lightheadedness, or fainting
No return precautions are necessary with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: The principal risk after a ruptured hemorrhagic cyst is ongoing intraperitoneal bleeding, so the patient should return immediately for worsening pain, dizziness, lightheadedness, near-syncope, or syncope, which signal hemodynamic compromise requiring re-evaluation and possible intervention. Limiting return precautions to fever misses the key concern of hemorrhage, which is not primarily an infectious process. Waiting until a missed period delays evaluation of an acute bleeding complication and is irrelevant given the negative pregnancy test. Providing no precautions is unsafe for any patient discharged with an acute condition that can deteriorate. Clear hemorrhage-focused return precautions are the most important counseling point.

cases.diagnosis: Ruptured ovarian (hemorrhagic) cyst, likely a corpus luteum cyst
pearls: Mid-cycle acute pelvic pain after intercourse with a negative pregnancy test and free pelvic fluid suggests a ruptured hemorrhagic (corpus luteum) cyst.
Preserved Doppler flow and a normal-sized (non-edematous) ovary help distinguish a ruptured cyst from ovarian torsion.
Stable patients with normal hemoglobin are managed conservatively with analgesia; surgery is reserved for instability or significant ongoing hemorrhage.
Discharge counseling must emphasize return for worsening pain, dizziness, or syncope, which signal ongoing intraperitoneal bleeding.

cases.title: Incidental Pelvic Mass in a 27-Year-Old
cases.chiefComplaint: An ultrasound for something else showed a mass on my ovary and I'm worried it's cancer.
cases.history: A 27-year-old G0 woman is referred to gynecology after an abdominal ultrasound, obtained for vague intermittent right-sided discomfort and bloating, revealed a right adnexal mass. She has only mild, occasional dull right lower quadrant discomfort and no acute pain, no fever, and no abnormal bleeding. Her menses are regular and her last period was 10 days ago. She is sexually active with one partner and uses an intrauterine device for contraception. She has no prior surgeries. PMH is unremarkable. Medications: none other than a daily multivitamin. No allergies. She does not smoke, drinks rarely, and uses no drugs. She works as a teacher and runs recreationally. Family history is noncontributory, with no breast or ovarian cancer. She is anxious about the word 'mass' and asks repeatedly whether she needs her ovary removed and whether this will affect her ability to have children in the future.
cases.exam: Well-appearing and in no distress. Abdomen is soft and non-tender with no rebound or guarding; a vague fullness is palpable in the right lower quadrant without a discretely tender mass. Pelvic exam reveals a mobile, non-tender right adnexal mass approximately 6 cm in size; the uterus is normal-sized and the left adnexa is normal. There is no cervical motion tenderness and no abnormal discharge. No inguinal lymphadenopathy. Bowel sounds are normal and there is no costovertebral angle tenderness.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: An adnexal mass is identified. What is the most appropriate imaging study to characterize it?
options: Plain abdominal radiograph
Whole-body PET scan
Diagnostic laparoscopy without imaging
Transvaginal pelvic ultrasound
stages.explanation: Transvaginal ultrasound is the first-line imaging modality for characterizing an adnexal mass; it defines size, cystic versus solid components, septations, and specific tissue features that suggest a benign diagnosis. A plain abdominal radiograph is insensitive, though it may incidentally show a tooth or calcification in a dermoid; it cannot characterize the mass adequately. A whole-body PET scan is for staging known malignancy and is inappropriate for an undiagnosed presumed-benign adnexal mass in a young woman. Diagnostic laparoscopy is an invasive procedure that should follow, not precede, noninvasive characterization. Ultrasound is the correct next step and often points directly to the diagnosis based on classic sonographic features.

stages.label: IMAGING & LABS
stages.content:
Transvaginal ultrasound: 6 cm well-circumscribed right ovarian cystic mass containing a hyperechoic Rokitansky nodule, areas of acoustic shadowing consistent with calcification/teeth, and echogenic fat with fat-fluid levels
Normal arterial and venous Doppler flow to both ovaries; ovary not enlarged or edematous
Left ovary normal; no free fluid
beta-hCG: negative
CA-125: 16 U/mL (normal)
LDH, AFP: within normal limits

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Ultrasound shows a cystic ovarian mass with fat, calcification/teeth, and a Rokitansky nodule. What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Tubo-ovarian abscess
Serous cystadenocarcinoma
Endometrioma in this clinical context
Mature cystic teratoma
stages.explanation: Fat, calcification or teeth, and a Rokitansky (dermoid) protuberance within a cystic ovarian mass are pathognomonic of a mature cystic teratoma (dermoid cyst), a benign germ cell tumor containing tissues from all three germ layers. An endometrioma classically shows homogeneous low-level internal echoes ('ground glass') without fat or teeth. A serous cystadenocarcinoma is typically a complex solid-cystic mass with septations and vascular solid components, usually in older women, and is not characterized by fat and teeth. A tubo-ovarian abscess presents with fever, pelvic pain, leukocytosis, and a complex inflammatory adnexal mass, none of which fit this asymptomatic patient with normal markers. The imaging is classic for a dermoid.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: For this 27-year-old with a 6 cm mature cystic teratoma desiring future fertility, what is the most appropriate management?
options: Observation alone indefinitely with no intervention
Unilateral salpingo-oophorectomy as initial therapy
Laparoscopic ovarian cystectomy with ovarian preservation
Total hysterectomy with bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy
stages.explanation: A symptomatic or sizeable (commonly cited threshold around 5-6 cm) dermoid in a young woman is typically managed with laparoscopic ovarian cystectomy, which removes the tumor while preserving ovarian tissue and fertility; care is taken to avoid spillage of cyst contents, which can cause chemical peritonitis. Indefinite observation is reasonable only for small asymptomatic dermoids, and this mass is larger, symptomatic, and carries torsion risk. Unilateral salpingo-oophorectomy unnecessarily sacrifices the entire ovary in a young woman when cystectomy preserves function. Hysterectomy with bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy is grossly excessive for a benign tumor and would eliminate fertility. Ovary-sparing cystectomy is the fertility-preserving standard.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which complication is this patient at increased risk of because of the dermoid, and is an important reason to consider removal?
options: Ectopic pregnancy
Pelvic inflammatory disease
Ovarian torsion
Endometrial hyperplasia
stages.explanation: Mature cystic teratomas are among the most common lead points for ovarian torsion because their weight and mobility predispose the ovary to twisting on its pedicle; this torsion risk is a key reason to consider removal of larger dermoids. Ectopic pregnancy relates to tubal factors and prior pelvic infection, not to a dermoid. Endometrial hyperplasia results from unopposed estrogen exposure (e.g., from granulosa cell tumors or anovulation), not from a dermoid, which is hormonally inactive. Pelvic inflammatory disease is an ascending sexually transmitted infection unrelated to a benign germ cell tumor. The principal mass-related complications of a dermoid are torsion and, less commonly, rupture causing chemical peritonitis.

cases.diagnosis: Mature cystic teratoma (dermoid cyst) of the right ovary
pearls: Fat, calcification/teeth, and a Rokitansky nodule on ultrasound are pathognomonic for a mature cystic teratoma (dermoid).
Dermoids are benign germ cell tumors containing tissue from all three germ layers and are the most common ovarian neoplasm in young women.
Their weight and mobility make dermoids a leading lead point for ovarian torsion; rupture can cause chemical peritonitis.
Larger or symptomatic dermoids in young women are managed with ovary-sparing laparoscopic cystectomy, avoiding spillage of contents.

cases.title: Lower Belly Pain in a Sexually Active 21-Year-Old
cases.chiefComplaint: I've had pain low in my belly for a few days and it really hurts during sex now.
cases.history: A 21-year-old G0 woman presents to the emergency department with four days of progressively worsening bilateral lower abdominal and pelvic pain, now accompanied by deep dyspareunia and an increasingly malodorous yellow vaginal discharge. She reports subjective fevers and chills over the past day. Her last menstrual period ended a week ago. She is sexually active with three partners over the past few months and uses condoms inconsistently; she has not been tested for sexually transmitted infections recently. She had chlamydia treated two years ago. She denies vaginal bleeding, dysuria is mild, and she has had no nausea, vomiting, or diarrhea. PMH is otherwise unremarkable. Medications: none. No allergies. She smokes occasionally, drinks alcohol on weekends, and uses no other drugs. She is a college student. Family history is noncontributory. She is worried because the pain now interferes with walking and she has missed two days of classes.
cases.exam: Appears uncomfortable but not toxic. Abdomen is soft with bilateral lower quadrant tenderness; mild voluntary guarding, no rebound or rigidity. Pelvic exam reveals mucopurulent cervical discharge, a friable cervix that bleeds with swab contact, and marked cervical motion tenderness. There is bilateral adnexal tenderness without a discrete mass. The uterus is mildly tender. No costovertebral angle tenderness. There is no right upper quadrant tenderness. Bowel sounds are normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: This sexually active young woman has lower abdominal pain, cervical motion tenderness, adnexal tenderness, and mucopurulent cervicitis with fever. What is the most appropriate immediate action?
options: Discharge with a topical antifungal cream as the first step today
Order an outpatient CT scan and follow up in one week as the first step today
Obtain a pregnancy test, then begin empiric antibiotic therapy for pelvic inflammatory disease
Wait for culture results before any treatment as the first step today
stages.explanation: The CDC recommends empiric treatment for pelvic inflammatory disease in a sexually active young woman with pelvic/lower abdominal pain and at least one minimum clinical criterion (cervical motion, uterine, or adnexal tenderness) once other causes are excluded, because the diagnosis is clinical and the threshold to treat is intentionally low to prevent sequelae. A pregnancy test is obtained first to exclude ectopic pregnancy and guide therapy. Waiting for cultures delays treatment and risks tubal damage, infertility, and abscess. A topical antifungal treats candidiasis, which does not cause this picture. Deferring with an outpatient CT and a one-week follow-up dangerously delays care for a treatable infection with serious complications. Prompt empiric therapy is correct.

stages.label: LABS & TESTING
stages.content:
Urine beta-hCG: negative
WBC 13.8 ×10³/µL with left shift
Nucleic acid amplification test (NAAT): positive for Neisseria gonorrhoeae and Chlamydia trachomatis
Wet mount: numerous WBCs; no clue cells, no trichomonads, no hyphae
Transvaginal ultrasound: mildly thickened, fluid-filled tubes bilaterally; no discrete tubo-ovarian abscess; small amount of free pelvic fluid
HIV and syphilis testing: pending

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given the clinical picture and positive NAAT, which sequela is this patient at greatest long-term risk of developing if PID recurs or goes inadequately treated?
options: Tubal-factor infertility
Endometrial cancer
Ovarian torsion in this clinical context
Cervical cancer in this clinical context
stages.explanation: Pelvic inflammatory disease causes scarring and damage to the fallopian tubes, leading to tubal-factor infertility, chronic pelvic pain, and a markedly increased risk of ectopic pregnancy; risk rises with each recurrent episode. Cervical cancer is driven by persistent high-risk HPV infection, not by gonococcal/chlamydial PID. Endometrial cancer is related to unopposed estrogen exposure (obesity, anovulation, tamoxifen), not pelvic infection. Ovarian torsion is a mechanical complication usually associated with an ovarian mass, not a consequence of PID. The hallmark long-term sequelae of PID are infertility, ectopic pregnancy, and chronic pelvic pain due to tubal scarring, which is why prompt and complete treatment is emphasized.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: She is well enough for outpatient management. Which empiric regimen best reflects current PID treatment, and what additional step is essential?
options: Oral metronidazole alone; no partner treatment needed as initial therapy
Ceftriaxone IM plus oral doxycycline plus metronidazole, with treatment of recent sexual partners
A single dose of fluconazole; advise abstinence only as initial therapy
Oral doxycycline alone for 7 days with no follow-up as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Current outpatient PID therapy is an intramuscular cephalosporin (ceftriaxone) to cover gonorrhea plus doxycycline to cover chlamydia, with metronidazole added for anaerobic coverage; partners from the preceding 60 days must be evaluated and treated to prevent reinfection. Metronidazole alone fails to cover gonorrhea and chlamydia. Fluconazole treats candidiasis and is irrelevant here. Doxycycline alone omits gonococcal and anaerobic coverage and neglects partner management and follow-up. Combination therapy plus expedited partner treatment and counseling on barrier protection and STI testing (including HIV/syphilis) is the comprehensive, guideline-concordant approach to PID.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which finding would most strongly indicate that this patient requires hospital admission and parenteral therapy rather than outpatient treatment?
options: A tubo-ovarian abscess on imaging or failure to respond to oral therapy
A history of treated chlamydia two years ago with appropriate follow-up
Mild improvement in pain after the first antibiotic dose
A positive chlamydia NAAT result with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: Indications for inpatient parenteral PID therapy include a tubo-ovarian abscess, inability to tolerate or failure of oral therapy, pregnancy, severe illness with high fever or vomiting, or a surgical emergency that cannot be excluded (e.g., appendicitis). A tubo-ovarian abscess or lack of response to outpatient treatment clearly meets these criteria. Improvement after the first dose is reassuring and supports continued outpatient care. A positive chlamydia NAAT confirms the diagnosis but does not by itself mandate admission. A remote history of treated chlamydia is a risk factor but not an admission criterion. Recognizing abscess or treatment failure is key to escalating care.

cases.diagnosis: Pelvic inflammatory disease due to Neisseria gonorrhoeae and Chlamydia trachomatis
pearls: PID is a clinical diagnosis; treat empirically in a sexually active young woman with pelvic pain plus cervical motion, uterine, or adnexal tenderness, after excluding pregnancy.
Outpatient regimen: IM ceftriaxone + oral doxycycline + metronidazole, with expedited partner treatment and STI counseling.
Long-term sequelae of PID are tubal-factor infertility, ectopic pregnancy, and chronic pelvic pain from tubal scarring.
Admit for parenteral therapy if there is a tubo-ovarian abscess, pregnancy, severe illness, oral therapy failure, or an unexcluded surgical abdomen.

cases.title: Persistent Fever Despite Antibiotics on the Ward
cases.chiefComplaint: I've been on IV antibiotics for two days but I still have fevers and my pelvis hurts even more.
cases.history: A 24-year-old G1P1 woman was admitted two days ago with pelvic inflammatory disease and started on intravenous antibiotics after presenting with fever, lower abdominal pain, and a positive gonorrhea/chlamydia NAAT. Despite 48 hours of parenteral therapy she continues to spike fevers, and her right-sided pelvic pain has intensified. She reports worsening nausea and decreased appetite. Her last period was three weeks ago. She is sexually active with two partners and uses no reliable contraception. She had one prior episode of PID a year ago. PMH is otherwise unremarkable. Medications: currently inpatient cefotetan and doxycycline (broadened today). No allergies. She does not smoke, drinks occasionally, and uses no drugs. She works as a hair stylist and has a 2-year-old child at home. Family history is noncontributory. She is increasingly frustrated and frightened that she is 'getting worse instead of better' on treatment.
cases.exam: Appears ill and uncomfortable, flushed. Abdomen is soft but with marked right lower quadrant tenderness and voluntary guarding; a tender, palpable fullness is appreciated in the right adnexal region. Pelvic exam reveals purulent cervical discharge, cervical motion tenderness, and a tender, fluctuant right adnexal mass; the left adnexa is mildly tender. The uterus is tender. No right upper quadrant tenderness. Bowel sounds are present but reduced. No costovertebral angle tenderness. Skin is warm; capillary refill is brisk.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: She has failed to defervesce after 48 hours of appropriate IV antibiotics for PID and now has a tender adnexal mass. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Discharge home on oral antibiotics as the first step today
Begin antifungal therapy as the first step today
Stop all antibiotics and observe as the first step today
Obtain pelvic imaging to evaluate for a tubo-ovarian abscess
stages.explanation: Failure to improve after 48-72 hours of appropriate parenteral PID therapy, especially with a new or enlarging tender adnexal mass, should prompt imaging to evaluate for a tubo-ovarian abscess (TOA) or other complication. Transvaginal ultrasound (or CT) characterizes a complex, thick-walled, multiloculated adnexal collection. Discharging a febrile, tachycardic patient who is deteriorating is unsafe. Stopping antibiotics in a worsening infection would allow progression to sepsis. Antifungal therapy is not indicated; the organisms identified are bacterial. Imaging to identify a TOA is the pivotal next step because its presence changes management, potentially requiring drainage or surgery in addition to antibiotics.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 18.6 ×10³/µL with bandemia
Hemoglobin 11.2 g/dL
CRP 16 mg/dL
Lactate 1.8 mmol/L
Blood cultures: pending
Transvaginal/pelvic ultrasound: 7 cm complex, thick-walled, multiloculated right adnexal collection with internal debris and septations consistent with a tubo-ovarian abscess; the normal right ovary cannot be separately identified
CT abdomen/pelvis: 7.2 cm rim-enhancing multiloculated right adnexal abscess; no free air; no other source

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Imaging confirms a 7 cm complex multiloculated rim-enhancing right adnexal collection. What is the diagnosis, and what is the most appropriate immediate management?
options: Ovarian torsion; emergent detorsion as the primary diagnosis
Hemorrhagic cyst; observe with analgesia as the primary diagnosis
Ectopic pregnancy; give methotrexate as the primary diagnosis
Tubo-ovarian abscess
stages.explanation: A complex, thick-walled, multiloculated rim-enhancing adnexal collection in a woman with PID who fails antibiotics is a tubo-ovarian abscess. Management is broad-spectrum IV antibiotics that ensure robust anaerobic coverage (e.g., adding metronidazole or using a regimen such as ampicillin-sulbactam plus doxycycline), with image-guided drainage planned if she does not improve within the next 48-72 hours. A hemorrhagic cyst does not produce rim enhancement, fever, or leukocytosis with bandemia. Ovarian torsion shows an enlarged, edematous, often avascular ovary, not a rim-enhancing pus collection. Ectopic pregnancy is excluded clinically and by the recent menses/absence of pregnancy; methotrexate is irrelevant. Antibiotics with anaerobic coverage plus a drainage plan is correct.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: After broadening antibiotics, she remains febrile and tachycardic 24 hours later, with the abscess unchanged in size. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Image-guided (percutaneous/transvaginal) drainage of the abscess
Continue the same antibiotics for another week without intervention
Perform an immediate total hysterectomy with bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy
Switch to oral antibiotics and discharge as initial therapy
stages.explanation: When a tubo-ovarian abscess fails to respond to antibiotics, minimally invasive image-guided drainage (transvaginal, transabdominal, or transrectal) combined with continued antibiotics is the preferred next step and is highly effective while preserving fertility. Continuing identical therapy in a non-responding, septic-trending patient risks rupture and overwhelming sepsis. Switching to oral antibiotics and discharging a deteriorating patient with an undrained abscess is unsafe. Aggressive surgery such as hysterectomy with bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy is reserved for a ruptured abscess with diffuse peritonitis, hemodynamic instability, or failure of drainage, and is inappropriate as a first-line intervention in a young woman desiring fertility. Drainage with antibiotics is the correct escalation.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which acute complication of a tubo-ovarian abscess would constitute a true surgical emergency requiring immediate operative intervention?
options: Persistent low-grade fever after drainage
Transient leukocytosis as the leading concern
Abscess rupture with diffuse peritonitis
Mild residual pelvic tenderness on discharge
stages.explanation: Rupture of a tubo-ovarian abscess causes spillage of purulent material into the peritoneal cavity, producing diffuse peritonitis, profound sepsis, and potentially septic shock; this is a life-threatening surgical emergency requiring immediate laparotomy with drainage and frequently removal of the involved adnexa. Persistent low-grade fever after drainage may reflect ongoing but controlled inflammation and is managed medically. Mild residual tenderness at discharge is expected during convalescence and does not signal catastrophe. Transient leukocytosis is a nonspecific finding seen during infection and recovery. Recognizing abscess rupture with peritonitis and hemodynamic collapse as the surgical emergency is the key high-yield point for this complication.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After successful drainage and clinical improvement, which discharge counseling point is most important for this patient?
options: She should plan for elective hysterectomy as the only way to prevent recurrence with appropriate follow-up
She no longer needs to complete oral antibiotics once fever resolves with appropriate follow-up
Complete the full antibiotic course, ensure partner treatment
She can resume unprotected intercourse immediately after discharge with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: Discharge counseling must stress completing the full antibiotic course, treating recent sexual partners, and undergoing STI screening (including HIV and syphilis), plus barrier protection to prevent reinfection; the patient should also understand that PID and tubo-ovarian abscess increase her long-term risk of tubal-factor infertility, ectopic pregnancy, and chronic pelvic pain. Stopping antibiotics early when febrile symptoms resolve risks relapse and incomplete eradication. Elective hysterectomy is not a routine preventive measure in a young woman and would eliminate fertility unnecessarily. Resuming unprotected intercourse immediately risks reinfection and transmission, especially before partner treatment is confirmed. Comprehensive counseling on adherence, partner treatment, and reproductive consequences is the priority.

cases.diagnosis: Tubo-ovarian abscess complicating pelvic inflammatory disease
pearls: Suspect a tubo-ovarian abscess when a patient with PID fails to defervesce after 48-72 hours of appropriate IV antibiotics or develops a tender adnexal mass.
TOA management is broad-spectrum IV antibiotics with strong anaerobic coverage; add image-guided drainage if there is no clinical improvement.
Minimally invasive drainage is preferred over surgery to preserve fertility; aggressive surgery is reserved for failure or rupture.
A ruptured TOA with diffuse peritonitis and septic shock is a surgical emergency requiring immediate operative intervention.

cases.title: Right Upper Belly Pain in a 23-Year-Old
cases.chiefComplaint: I have sharp pain under my right ribs that's worse when I breathe in.
cases.history: A 23-year-old G0 woman presents to the emergency department with two days of sharp right upper quadrant pain that worsens with deep inspiration and movement, occasionally radiating to the right shoulder. She initially attributed it to a possible gallbladder problem after a heavy meal. On further questioning she also reports a week of mild lower abdominal pain, deep dyspareunia, and an increased malodorous vaginal discharge, which she had not connected to the new pain. Her last menstrual period ended five days ago. She is sexually active with multiple recent partners and uses condoms inconsistently. She had untreated chlamydia exposure (a partner notified her) a few weeks ago but never got tested. She denies jaundice, dark urine, pale stools, or prior episodes. PMH is unremarkable. Medications: none. No allergies. She does not smoke, drinks socially, and uses no drugs. She is a graduate student. Family history is noncontributory. She is anxious that she has gallstones and asks whether she will need her gallbladder removed.
cases.exam: Uncomfortable, guarding the right upper abdomen. Abdomen is soft; there is right upper quadrant tenderness that worsens with inspiration, but the Murphy sign is equivocal and there is no palpable gallbladder. Importantly, there is also bilateral lower abdominal tenderness. Pelvic exam reveals mucopurulent cervical discharge, cervical motion tenderness, and bilateral adnexal tenderness without a discrete mass. No jaundice or scleral icterus. Lungs are clear with no focal findings; no chest-wall tenderness. Bowel sounds are normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A young woman with RUQ pain mimicking cholecystitis also has signs of pelvic infection. What is the most appropriate initial workup?
options: Pregnancy test, RUQ ultrasound with LFTs,
HIDA scan as the sole study as the first step today
Right upper quadrant ultrasound and liver function tests only
Discharge with reassurance for presumed musculoskeletal pain
stages.explanation: Because the RUQ pain mimics biliary disease but coexists with pelvic findings, the workup must evaluate both: a pregnancy test (to exclude ectopic), RUQ ultrasound with LFTs (to assess the gallbladder/liver), and a pelvic exam with gonorrhea/chlamydia NAAT (to evaluate for PID). Limiting the evaluation to the RUQ alone would miss the pelvic infection driving a perihepatitis. A HIDA scan investigates only the gallbladder and is not an appropriate sole study in a patient with concurrent pelvic signs and fever. Discharging as musculoskeletal pain ignores fever, tachycardia, cervical motion tenderness, and discharge. A combined hepatobiliary and pelvic evaluation is required to capture Fitz-Hugh-Curtis syndrome.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Urine beta-hCG: negative
WBC 13.1 ×10³/µL with left shift
AST 30 U/L, ALT 28 U/L, alkaline phosphatase 78 U/L, total bilirubin 0.7 mg/dL (all normal)
RUQ ultrasound: normal gallbladder wall, no stones, no pericholecystic fluid, no biliary dilation
NAAT: positive for Chlamydia trachomatis; gonorrhea negative
Wet mount: numerous WBCs
Pelvic ultrasound: mild bilateral tubal thickening; no abscess

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Liver chemistries and gallbladder imaging are normal, but she has chlamydial PID plus pleuritic RUQ pain. What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Fitz-Hugh-Curtis syndrome
Acute viral hepatitis as the primary diagnosis
Right lower lobe pneumonia in this clinical context
Acute cholecystitis as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Fitz-Hugh-Curtis syndrome is perihepatitis caused by inflammation of the liver capsule and adjacent peritoneum complicating PID, most often from Chlamydia trachomatis; it produces pleuritic RUQ pain that mimics cholecystitis but with normal gallbladder imaging and normal-to-minimally-abnormal liver chemistries, alongside signs of pelvic infection. Acute cholecystitis is excluded by the normal gallbladder ultrasound without stones, wall thickening, or pericholecystic fluid. Acute viral hepatitis would cause markedly elevated transaminases, which are normal here. Right lower lobe pneumonia can cause referred upper abdominal pain but the lungs are clear and there are no respiratory findings. The combination of perihepatitis features with chlamydial PID defines Fitz-Hugh-Curtis syndrome.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management of Fitz-Hugh-Curtis syndrome in this patient?
options: Antiviral therapy for hepatitis as initial therapy
Laparoscopic cholecystectomy as initial therapy
Endoscopic retrograde cholangiopancreatography (ERCP) as initial therapy
Treat the underlying PID with appropriate antibiotics
stages.explanation: Fitz-Hugh-Curtis syndrome is a manifestation of PID, so treatment is directed at the underlying pelvic infection with the same antibiotic regimens used for PID (ceftriaxone plus doxycycline, with metronidazole for anaerobic coverage), along with analgesia; the perihepatitis resolves as the infection is treated, and partner treatment and STI counseling are essential. Cholecystectomy is inappropriate because the gallbladder is normal and is not the source of pain. Antiviral therapy is for viral hepatitis, which is not present given normal transaminases. ERCP investigates and treats biliary obstruction, which is absent here. Recognizing the syndrome as PID-related perihepatitis and treating the infection (not the liver or gallbladder) is the key management principle.

stages.label: PATHOPHYSIOLOGY
stages.question: Which finding would be most characteristic of Fitz-Hugh-Curtis syndrome if laparoscopy were performed?
options: Cirrhotic nodular liver with portal hypertension in this setting
'Violin-string' fibrinous adhesions between the liver capsule
Gallstones impacted in the cystic duct as the immediate next step
A perforated duodenal ulcer as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: The classic laparoscopic finding in Fitz-Hugh-Curtis syndrome is 'violin-string' (or 'piano-wire') fibrinous adhesions stretching between the anterior liver capsule and the parietal peritoneum/diaphragm, reflecting healed perihepatic inflammation. Gallstones in the cystic duct would indicate cholecystitis/biliary disease, which is excluded by normal imaging. A cirrhotic nodular liver with portal hypertension reflects chronic liver disease unrelated to acute perihepatitis. A perforated duodenal ulcer would show a perforation with peritoneal contamination and free air, not perihepatic adhesions. The violin-string adhesions are the pathognomonic structural correlate of the perihepatitis seen in Fitz-Hugh-Curtis syndrome.

stages.label: PREVENTION
stages.question: Beyond treating this episode, which intervention is most important to prevent recurrence and complications?
options: Annual abdominal CT surveillance as the immediate next step
Lifelong daily oral antibiotics as the immediate next step
Routine prophylactic cholecystectomy as the immediate next step
Treatment of recent sexual partners
stages.explanation: Because Fitz-Hugh-Curtis syndrome arises from a sexually transmitted pelvic infection, preventing recurrence depends on treating recent sexual partners (expedited partner therapy where appropriate), counseling on consistent condom use, and regular STI screening; this reduces reinfection and the cumulative risk of tubal damage, infertility, and ectopic pregnancy. Prophylactic cholecystectomy is pointless because the gallbladder is not involved. Lifelong daily antibiotics are not indicated, promote resistance, and do not address behavioral transmission risk. Annual abdominal CT surveillance provides no preventive benefit and unnecessarily exposes a young woman to radiation. Partner treatment plus barrier protection and screening is the cornerstone of preventing recurrent chlamydial PID and its perihepatic complication.

cases.diagnosis: Fitz-Hugh-Curtis syndrome (chlamydial perihepatitis) complicating pelvic inflammatory disease
pearls: Fitz-Hugh-Curtis syndrome is perihepatitis from PID (often chlamydial) presenting as pleuritic RUQ pain that mimics cholecystitis but with a normal gallbladder and normal liver chemistries.
Look for concurrent pelvic signs (cervical motion tenderness, discharge, adnexal tenderness) in any young woman with RUQ pain and an unremarkable biliary workup.
The classic laparoscopic finding is 'violin-string' fibrinous adhesions between the liver capsule and the abdominal wall/diaphragm.
Treatment targets the underlying PID with standard antibiotics plus partner treatment and STI counseling, not the gallbladder or liver.

cases.title: Fishy-Smelling Vaginal Discharge
cases.chiefComplaint: I've had a thin grayish discharge with a fishy smell that's worse after sex.
cases.history: A 28-year-old G1P1 woman presents with two weeks of a thin, grayish-white vaginal discharge accompanied by an unpleasant fishy odor that she notices most after intercourse. She denies vulvar itching, burning, external irritation, dysuria, or pelvic pain. There is no abnormal bleeding. Her last menstrual period ended one week ago and her cycles are regular. She is sexually active with one partner and recently stopped using condoms after starting an oral contraceptive; she reports a new partner three months ago. She douches occasionally to feel 'cleaner,' especially after her period. PMH is unremarkable. Medications: combined oral contraceptive pill. No allergies. She does not smoke, drinks occasionally, and uses no drugs. She works as a nurse. Family history is noncontributory. She is mildly embarrassed by the odor and worried it could be a sexually transmitted infection, asking whether her partner needs treatment.
cases.exam: Well-appearing and in no distress. Abdomen is soft and non-tender. External genitalia appear normal without erythema, edema, excoriation, or lesions. Speculum exam reveals a thin, homogeneous, grayish-white discharge thinly coating the vaginal walls; there is no vulvovaginal erythema and no cottage-cheese-like plaques. The cervix appears normal without friability or mucopurulent discharge. Bimanual exam reveals a normal-sized, non-tender uterus, no adnexal tenderness, and no cervical motion tenderness. No inguinal lymphadenopathy.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: For this thin gray malodorous discharge without itching or inflammation, what is the most appropriate office-based diagnostic step?
options: Empiric oral fluconazole without testing
Serum CA-125 as the first step today
Vaginal pH testing, a whiff test with KOH,
Pelvic ultrasound as the first step today
stages.explanation: The bedside evaluation of vaginal discharge consists of measuring vaginal pH, performing the whiff (amine) test by adding potassium hydroxide, and examining a saline wet mount under the microscope; these inexpensive tests rapidly distinguish bacterial vaginosis, candidiasis, and trichomoniasis. Empiric fluconazole treats candidiasis, but the thin gray discharge without itching or erythema is not typical of yeast, and treating empirically without testing risks misdiagnosis. Pelvic ultrasound evaluates structural pelvic anatomy, not vaginal flora, and is not indicated for uncomplicated discharge. CA-125 is an ovarian cancer marker and is entirely irrelevant to evaluating vaginal discharge. The pH, whiff test, and wet mount are the correct, high-yield first steps.

stages.label: OFFICE TESTING
stages.content:
Vaginal pH: 5.0 (elevated; normal 3.8-4.5)
Whiff (amine) test: positive — fishy odor on addition of KOH
Saline wet mount: numerous clue cells (epithelial cells studded with adherent coccobacilli with obscured borders); few white blood cells
No motile trichomonads seen; no pseudohyphae or budding yeast on KOH prep

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Using the Amsel criteria, which combination of findings confirms the diagnosis here?
options: Cottage-cheese discharge, normal pH, and pseudohyphae in this clinical context
Thin homogeneous discharge, vaginal pH >4.5, positive whiff test,
Frothy yellow-green discharge, motile flagellated organisms, and a strawberry cervix
Vulvar erythema, thick white discharge, and itching as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Bacterial vaginosis is diagnosed clinically when at least three of the four Amsel criteria are met: a thin homogeneous discharge, vaginal pH greater than 4.5, a positive whiff (amine) test, and clue cells on microscopy; this patient meets all four. Vulvar erythema, thick white discharge, and itching describe vulvovaginal candidiasis, which has a normal pH and shows pseudohyphae. A frothy yellow-green discharge with motile flagellated organisms and a strawberry cervix describes trichomoniasis. Cottage-cheese discharge with normal pH and pseudohyphae again indicates candidiasis. The thin gray discharge with elevated pH, positive whiff test, and clue cells confirms bacterial vaginosis by Amsel criteria.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate treatment, and does her male partner require treatment?
options: Oral fluconazole; treat the partner as initial therapy
Oral or vaginal metronidazole
Single-dose intramuscular ceftriaxone; treat the partner as initial therapy
No treatment; bacterial vaginosis never requires therapy as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Symptomatic bacterial vaginosis is treated with metronidazole (oral or intravaginal gel) or clindamycin; routine treatment of male partners is not recommended because it does not reduce recurrence. Fluconazole treats candidiasis, not bacterial vaginosis. Intramuscular ceftriaxone treats gonorrhea and is not therapy for BV. Although asymptomatic BV in nonpregnant women may not always require treatment, this patient is symptomatic and bothered, so therapy is indicated; saying BV never requires treatment is incorrect. Patients should also be advised to avoid douching, which disrupts the protective lactobacilli-dominant flora and predisposes to recurrence. Metronidazole without partner treatment is the correct approach.

stages.label: COUNSELING
stages.question: Which counseling point is most accurate and important for this patient?
options: Bacterial vaginosis is a classic sexually transmitted infection requiring partner notification by law
Douching regularly will prevent recurrence as the immediate next step
Avoid douching, because it disrupts normal vaginal flora
She should avoid all future use of oral contraceptives as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Patients should be counseled to avoid douching because it depletes protective hydrogen-peroxide-producing lactobacilli and promotes the polymicrobial overgrowth characteristic of bacterial vaginosis, increasing recurrence risk; she should also know that BV during pregnancy is associated with preterm birth and other adverse outcomes, which is why symptomatic pregnant patients are treated. Douching does not prevent recurrence; it worsens it. BV is associated with sexual activity and new partners but is not classified as a classic reportable STI requiring legal partner notification, and routine partner treatment is not advised. Oral contraceptives do not cause BV and need not be discontinued. Avoiding douching and understanding pregnancy implications are the key counseling points.

pearls: Bacterial vaginosis is diagnosed by Amsel criteria: thin homogeneous discharge, vaginal pH >4.5, positive whiff (amine) test, and clue cells on wet mount (3 of 4 needed).
It reflects loss of protective lactobacilli and overgrowth of anaerobes (e.g., Gardnerella); douching is a key modifiable risk factor.
Treat symptomatic BV with metronidazole or clindamycin; routine treatment of male partners is not recommended.
BV in pregnancy is associated with preterm birth, so symptomatic pregnant patients should be treated.

cases.title: Itching and Thick Vaginal Discharge
cases.chiefComplaint: Vaginal itching and thick white discharge for five days.
cases.history: A 26-year-old woman presents with five days of intense vulvar and vaginal pruritus accompanied by a thick, white, clumpy discharge. She reports vulvar burning and dyspareunia but denies any malodor. She also notes some burning with urination that she attributes to irritated skin rather than internal pain. She completed a 10-day course of amoxicillin-clavulanate three weeks ago for sinusitis. She has had two similar episodes in the past four months, each self-treated with over-the-counter antifungals with only partial relief. Her menstrual cycles are regular and her last period was two weeks ago. She is sexually active with one male partner and uses combined oral contraceptives. PMH is otherwise unremarkable; she takes no chronic medications and has no known drug allergies. She drinks alcohol socially and does not smoke. Family history is notable for type 2 diabetes in both parents. On review of systems she reports increased thirst and nocturia over the past two months and feels she has lost a few pounds without trying.
cases.exam: She is well-appearing and in no distress. Abdominal exam is soft and nontender without masses. External genitalia show vulvar erythema and edema with excoriations from scratching. Speculum exam reveals thick, white, curd-like discharge adherent to the vaginal walls; the vaginal mucosa is erythematous. There is no foul odor and the cervix appears normal without mucopurulent discharge or friability. Bimanual exam shows no cervical motion tenderness and no adnexal masses or tenderness. No inguinal lymphadenopathy is appreciated.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which bedside test most directly confirms the suspected diagnosis?
options: Whiff test with 10% KOH added to discharge
Nucleic acid amplification test for gonorrhea and chlamydia
Vaginal pH measurement alone as the first step today
Saline and 10% KOH microscopy of the discharge
stages.explanation: Microscopy with saline and 10% KOH is the key bedside test: KOH lyses epithelial cells and reveals budding yeast and pseudohyphae diagnostic of candidiasis, while the wet mount excludes trichomonads and clue cells. The whiff test is used for bacterial vaginosis and trichomoniasis and is typically negative in candidiasis, so it does not confirm the diagnosis. Vaginal pH in candidiasis is normal (3.8-4.5), so pH alone is nonspecific and cannot confirm yeast. NAAT for gonorrhea and chlamydia evaluates cervicitis, not the curd-like discharge and pruritus seen here, and would miss the actual pathogen. Microscopy directly visualizes the organism and rapidly distinguishes among the common causes of vaginitis.

stages.label: MICROSCOPY
stages.content:
Vaginal pH: 4.2
10% KOH prep: budding yeast with pseudohyphae visualized
Saline wet mount: no trichomonads, no clue cells, few WBCs
Whiff test: negative
Urine dipstick: glucose 3+, no nitrites, no leukocyte esterase

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate treatment for this uncomplicated episode?
options: Single oral dose of fluconazole 150 mg
Intramuscular ceftriaxone plus oral doxycycline
Oral metronidazole 500 mg twice daily for 7 days
Topical clindamycin cream for 7 nights as monotherapy
stages.explanation: A single 150 mg oral dose of fluconazole (or a short course of topical azole) is first-line for uncomplicated vulvovaginal candidiasis and is highly effective against Candida albicans. Oral metronidazole treats trichomoniasis and bacterial vaginosis, not yeast, and would not relieve her symptoms. Ceftriaxone plus doxycycline treats gonococcal and chlamydial cervicitis, which is not present here given the normal cervix and absence of mucopurulent discharge. Topical clindamycin is a therapy for bacterial vaginosis and has no antifungal activity. Because her microscopy confirms yeast and the episode is uncomplicated, a single azole dose is the appropriate, guideline-concordant choice.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Given her recurrent infections and systemic symptoms, what is the most important next step?
options: Empirically treat her sexual partner with fluconazole in this setting
Obtain a hemoglobin A1c
Switch her contraceptive method to a copper IUD in this setting
Start weekly suppressive fluconazole for six months without further workup
stages.explanation: Recurrent candidiasis plus polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss, and 3+ urine glucose strongly suggests undiagnosed diabetes mellitus, so checking hemoglobin A1c and fasting glucose is the priority; hyperglycemia and glycosuria promote yeast overgrowth and recurrence. Treating her male partner is not recommended because candidiasis is not classified as a sexually transmitted infection and partner treatment does not reduce recurrence. Switching to a copper IUD does not address the underlying metabolic driver and is not indicated. Starting six months of suppressive fluconazole may be considered for true recurrent VVC, but doing so before identifying and treating an obvious reversible cause such as diabetes would miss the central problem driving her recurrences.

stages.label: PATIENT EDUCATION
stages.question: Which statement about her condition is most accurate to share with the patient?
options: Controlling blood sugar can reduce the frequency of recurrent yeast infections
Yogurt douching is proven to prevent future yeast infections in this setting
She should abstain from sex permanently to avoid reinfection in this setting
Recurrent yeast infections always indicate HIV infection in this setting
stages.explanation: Glycemic control reduces glycosuria and lowers the risk of recurrent candidiasis, so optimizing blood sugar is accurate and actionable counseling for this patient with likely new diabetes. Yogurt or probiotic douching lacks robust evidence and douching disrupts normal flora, so it is not a proven preventive measure. Permanent sexual abstinence is unnecessary because candidiasis is not sexually transmitted. While recurrent or severe candidiasis can occasionally be a clue to immunosuppression including HIV, recurrence does not always indicate HIV; in this patient the far more likely explanation is uncontrolled diabetes, making that the appropriate teaching point.

cases.diagnosis: Vulvovaginal candidiasis with newly suspected diabetes mellitus
pearls: Candidiasis produces thick white "cottage cheese" discharge with a normal vaginal pH (3.8-4.5) and a negative whiff test.
KOH microscopy showing budding yeast and pseudohyphae confirms the diagnosis and excludes mimics.
Recurrent vulvovaginal candidiasis should prompt evaluation for diabetes, immunosuppression, or recent antibiotic use.
Candidiasis is not a sexually transmitted infection, so routine partner treatment is not indicated.

cases.title: Frothy Discharge and Vaginal Irritation
cases.chiefComplaint: Malodorous frothy vaginal discharge with itching for two weeks.
cases.history: A 30-year-old woman presents with two weeks of a copious, frothy, yellow-green vaginal discharge with a noticeable foul odor. She reports vulvar itching, burning, and discomfort during intercourse, as well as some dysuria. She denies fever, abdominal pain, or abnormal bleeding. Her menstrual cycles are regular. She is sexually active with a new male partner over the past two months and reports inconsistent condom use. She has no history of sexually transmitted infections that she knows of. PMH is unremarkable. She takes no daily medications, uses no hormonal contraception, and has no known drug allergies. She does not smoke and drinks alcohol occasionally. Family history is noncontributory. On review of systems she notes the discharge is heaviest after her period. She has not been pregnant. She has not received any recent antibiotics and has not changed soaps or detergents. Her partner has not reported any symptoms, though she has not discussed her symptoms with him.
cases.exam: She is comfortable and afebrile. Abdomen is soft and nontender without rebound or guarding. External genitalia show mild vulvar erythema. Speculum exam reveals a copious, frothy, yellow-green discharge pooling in the vaginal vault with a malodorous quality. The cervix demonstrates punctate hemorrhages giving a "strawberry" appearance. There is no mucopurulent endocervical discharge and the cervix is not friable on light contact beyond the punctate lesions. Bimanual exam reveals no cervical motion tenderness and no adnexal masses or tenderness. No inguinal lymphadenopathy.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial diagnostic test?
options: Endometrial biopsy as the first step today
Blood cultures as the first step today
Pelvic ultrasound as the first step today
Vaginal pH plus saline wet mount microscopy
stages.explanation: Vaginal pH plus a saline wet mount is the appropriate first step: trichomoniasis raises the pH above 4.5 and the wet mount can reveal motile flagellated trichomonads, rapidly clinching the diagnosis. NAAT is more sensitive and can confirm, but among the listed choices microscopy with pH is the standard bedside evaluation for vaginitis. Endometrial biopsy evaluates abnormal uterine bleeding or endometrial pathology and has no role in acute vaginitis. Pelvic ultrasound assesses structural or adnexal pathology, not the cause of discharge. Blood cultures are reserved for suspected bacteremia or sepsis, which this well-appearing, afebrile patient does not have. The frothy discharge and strawberry cervix point clearly toward trichomoniasis.

stages.label: MICROSCOPY
stages.content:
Vaginal pH: 6.0
Saline wet mount: motile flagellated protozoa identified
Whiff test: positive
KOH prep: no budding yeast or pseudohyphae
Urine dipstick: trace leukocyte esterase, no nitrites

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the recommended treatment for this patient?
options: Single oral dose of fluconazole 150 mg as monotherapy
Oral metronidazole 500 mg twice daily for 7 days
Topical metronidazole gel intravaginally for 5 days
Single intramuscular dose of ceftriaxone
stages.explanation: Oral metronidazole 500 mg twice daily for 7 days is now the preferred regimen for trichomoniasis in women, having demonstrated superior cure rates compared with the older single 2 g dose. Fluconazole treats candidiasis and has no activity against the protozoan Trichomonas vaginalis. Topical intravaginal metronidazole gel achieves inadequate concentrations to eradicate trichomoniasis, which can also infect the urethra and Skene glands, so systemic therapy is required. A single dose of ceftriaxone targets gonorrhea and would not treat this protozoal infection. Systemic oral metronidazole reliably reaches all infected sites and is the guideline-concordant choice.

stages.label: PARTNER MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate counseling regarding her partner and behavior?
options: Only treat the partner if he develops symptoms as the immediate next step
Treat her sexual partner
She may resume unprotected sex immediately after taking the first dose in this setting
No partner treatment is needed because trichomoniasis is not sexually transmitted
stages.explanation: Trichomoniasis is a sexually transmitted infection, so all current partners should be treated simultaneously and the couple should abstain from sex until both have completed therapy and are asymptomatic to prevent reinfection ("ping-pong" transmission). Waiting until the partner is symptomatic is inadequate because men are frequently asymptomatic carriers who will reinfect her. Saying no partner treatment is needed is incorrect, as untreated partners are the main source of recurrence. Resuming unprotected sex immediately risks reinfection and onward transmission before the medication has eradicated the organism. Expedited partner therapy is appropriate where permitted.

stages.label: ASSOCIATED TESTING
stages.question: Given this diagnosis, which additional step is most appropriate?
options: Schedule a colposcopy for the strawberry cervix
Begin suppressive antiviral therapy in this setting
Order a fasting lipid panel as the immediate next step
Screen for other STIs including HIV, gonorrhea, chlamydia,
stages.explanation: A new diagnosis of trichomoniasis warrants screening for coexisting sexually transmitted infections, including HIV, gonorrhea, chlamydia, and syphilis, because shared risk factors and increased HIV acquisition risk accompany trichomoniasis. A fasting lipid panel is unrelated to STI care. Suppressive antiviral therapy applies to recurrent genital herpes, not a protozoal infection. The strawberry cervix is a hallmark of trichomoniasis and resolves with treatment; it does not represent cervical dysplasia, so colposcopy is not indicated for this finding. Comprehensive STI screening and partner notification are the appropriate complementary steps.

pearls: Trichomoniasis classically causes a frothy, malodorous yellow-green discharge with an elevated vaginal pH (>4.5) and a positive whiff test.
The "strawberry cervix" (colpitis macularis) is specific but seen in a minority of cases.
Preferred treatment is oral metronidazole 500 mg twice daily for 7 days; topical therapy is inadequate.
Treat all sexual partners and screen for coexisting STIs including HIV.

cases.title: Routine Screening in a Young Woman
cases.chiefComplaint: Asymptomatic; presents for a routine well-woman visit and contraception refill.
cases.history: A 19-year-old woman presents for a routine well-woman visit and to refill her combined oral contraceptive pills. She has no genitourinary complaints: no abnormal discharge, no dysuria, no pelvic pain, and no abnormal bleeding. She became sexually active at age 16 and has had three lifetime male partners, including a new partner over the past three months. Condom use is inconsistent. She has never been tested for sexually transmitted infections and has never been pregnant. Her menses are regular on the pill. PMH is unremarkable, she takes no other medications, and she has no known drug allergies. She does not smoke, drinks alcohol occasionally at social events, and denies illicit drug use. Family history is noncontributory. As part of routine care for a sexually active woman under 25, the clinician recommends STI screening, and a first-catch urine nucleic acid amplification test for gonorrhea and chlamydia is collected along with HIV and syphilis serologies. She has no current symptoms but agrees to screening.
cases.exam: She is a well-appearing young woman in no distress. Abdomen is soft, nontender, and without masses or organomegaly. External genitalia appear normal. Speculum exam shows a normal-appearing cervix without mucopurulent discharge, friability, or lesions; scant clear physiologic discharge is present. Bimanual exam reveals no cervical motion tenderness, no adnexal masses, and no adnexal tenderness. There is no inguinal lymphadenopathy. The overall pelvic exam is unremarkable, consistent with an asymptomatic patient presenting for routine screening.

stages.label: SCREENING
stages.question: Why is STI screening recommended for this asymptomatic patient?
options: Annual chlamydia and gonorrhea screening is recommended for all sexually active women younger than 25
All adults should be screened annually regardless of age or risk as the immediate next step
Screening is unnecessary because she has no symptoms as the immediate next step
Screening is only indicated once she becomes pregnant as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Guidelines recommend annual screening for chlamydia and gonorrhea in all sexually active women younger than 25 because infection is common and frequently asymptomatic in this group, and untreated chlamydia can lead to pelvic inflammatory disease and infertility. It is not accurate that all adults need annual screening regardless of risk; women 25 and older are screened based on risk factors. Limiting screening to pregnancy would miss the large reservoir of asymptomatic infection in young women. The absence of symptoms does not exclude infection, since chlamydia is most often silent, which is precisely why routine screening is essential.

stages.label: LAB RESULTS
stages.content:
Urine NAAT: Chlamydia trachomatis DETECTED
Urine NAAT: Neisseria gonorrhoeae not detected
HIV 4th-generation antigen/antibody: nonreactive
RPR: nonreactive
Urine pregnancy test: negative

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the recommended first-line treatment for her infection?
options: Single intramuscular dose of penicillin G benzathine
Oral doxycycline 100 mg twice daily for 7 days
Oral fluconazole 150 mg single dose as monotherapy
Topical clindamycin cream for 7 nights
stages.explanation: Doxycycline 100 mg orally twice daily for 7 days is now the preferred first-line treatment for uncomplicated genital chlamydia, as it has higher cure rates than single-dose azithromycin, particularly for rectal infection. Azithromycin 1 g remains an acceptable alternative, especially when adherence is a concern or in pregnancy. Intramuscular penicillin G benzathine treats syphilis, not chlamydia. Topical clindamycin treats bacterial vaginosis and has no role here. Fluconazole treats candidiasis and is ineffective against an intracellular bacterium like Chlamydia trachomatis. Doxycycline is the guideline-concordant choice for this nonpregnant patient.

stages.label: PARTNER MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which approach to her partners and follow-up is most appropriate?
options: Repeat the NAAT in 2 weeks to confirm cure before treating in this setting
Treat the patient only; partners do not require treatment in this setting
Offer expedited partner therapy, advise abstinence for 7 days,
Begin lifelong suppressive antibiotics as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Recent sexual partners should be treated to prevent reinfection and transmission, and where legal, expedited partner therapy (providing treatment or a prescription for the partner without a separate exam) is recommended. The couple should abstain from sex for 7 days after single-dose therapy or until a 7-day regimen is complete and symptoms resolve. A test of reinfection (not cure) is advised at 3 months. Treating the patient alone leaves her at high risk of reinfection. A test of cure at 2 weeks is not routinely recommended for uncomplicated chlamydia in nonpregnant adults because NAAT can stay positive from residual nucleic acid. Lifelong suppressive antibiotics are never indicated for chlamydia.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: If left untreated, this infection most directly increases her risk of which complication?
options: Endometrial carcinoma as the leading concern
Ovarian torsion as the leading concern
Pelvic inflammatory disease with tubal infertility
Cervical fibroids as the leading concern
stages.explanation: Untreated chlamydial cervicitis can ascend to cause pelvic inflammatory disease, leading to tubal scarring that increases the risk of infertility, chronic pelvic pain, and ectopic pregnancy; this is the principal rationale for screening and treating asymptomatic young women. Endometrial carcinoma is associated with unopposed estrogen and is unrelated to chlamydia. Ovarian torsion results from an enlarged or cystic ovary on a mobile pedicle, not from cervicitis. "Cervical fibroids" are uncommon and unrelated to infection. The ascending tubal damage from untreated chlamydia is the key reproductive complication that screening aims to prevent.

pearls: Screen all sexually active women under 25 annually for chlamydia and gonorrhea; most infections are asymptomatic.
Doxycycline 100 mg twice daily for 7 days is preferred first-line therapy; azithromycin 1 g is an alternative.
Treat partners (expedited partner therapy where allowed) and retest at 3 months for reinfection, not cure.
Untreated chlamydia can ascend to PID, causing tubal infertility and ectopic pregnancy.

cases.title: Vaginal Discharge and Pelvic Discomfort
cases.chiefComplaint: Yellow vaginal discharge, dysuria, and lower abdominal discomfort for four days.
cases.history: A 20-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with four days of yellow vaginal discharge, dysuria, and mild lower abdominal discomfort. She also reports intermenstrual spotting and pain with intercourse. She denies high fever, nausea, or vomiting. Her last menstrual period was 10 days ago and was normal. She is sexually active with two male partners over the past month and uses condoms inconsistently. She has a prior history of chlamydia treated one year ago. She is not pregnant by her account and uses a progestin-only pill. PMH is otherwise unremarkable, she takes no other medications, and she has no known drug allergies. She does not smoke and uses alcohol occasionally. Family history is noncontributory. On review of systems she notes no joint pains, skin lesions, or eye symptoms currently but reports that her right wrist felt sore yesterday. She has no known immunodeficiency. She has not traveled recently and has not used antibiotics in the past several months.
cases.exam: She is uncomfortable but nontoxic. Abdomen is soft with mild suprapubic tenderness and no rebound or guarding. Speculum exam reveals a mucopurulent yellow endocervical discharge and the cervix bleeds easily when touched with a swab (friability). External genitalia are normal without ulcers or vesicles. Bimanual exam elicits mild cervical motion tenderness without a palpable adnexal mass; there is mild bilateral adnexal tenderness. Skin shows no pustular lesions and there is no joint effusion, warmth, or erythema; the right wrist has full painless range of motion on exam today. No inguinal lymphadenopathy.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial diagnostic test for the cervical findings?
options: Pelvic CT with contrast as the first step today
Endometrial biopsy as the first step today
Vaginal pH and KOH whiff test alone
Nucleic acid amplification testing for gonorrhea
stages.explanation: NAAT for Neisseria gonorrhoeae and Chlamydia trachomatis is the most sensitive and appropriate test for mucopurulent cervicitis with a friable cervix, and it can be performed on endocervical or vaginal swabs or urine. pH and whiff testing evaluate vaginitis (bacterial vaginosis, trichomoniasis) but do not diagnose gonococcal or chlamydial cervicitis. Pelvic CT is not the initial test for cervicitis and exposes the patient to unnecessary radiation; imaging is reserved for suspected abscess or complications. Endometrial biopsy plays no role in acute cervicitis. NAAT identifies the causative organisms and guides targeted therapy while STI co-testing proceeds in parallel.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Endocervical Gram stain: gram-negative intracellular diplococci within neutrophils
NAAT: Neisseria gonorrhoeae DETECTED; Chlamydia trachomatis DETECTED
Urine pregnancy test: negative
WBC: 9.8 ×10³/µL
HIV antigen/antibody: pending

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the recommended treatment for her gonococcal cervicitis with concurrent chlamydia?
options: Oral ciprofloxacin 500 mg single dose as initial therapy
Oral azithromycin 1 g single dose only as initial therapy
Penicillin G benzathine 2.4 million units intramuscular as initial therapy
Ceftriaxone 500 mg intramuscular single dose plus doxycycline 100 mg twice daily for 7 days
stages.explanation: Current guidelines recommend ceftriaxone 500 mg IM as a single dose for uncomplicated gonococcal infection; because chlamydia is confirmed (and often coexists), doxycycline 100 mg twice daily for 7 days is added to cover it. Azithromycin alone undertreats gonorrhea and would not reliably eradicate it. Fluoroquinolones such as ciprofloxacin are no longer used for gonorrhea due to widespread resistance. Penicillin G benzathine treats syphilis, not gonorrhea or chlamydia.
