cases.chiefComplaint: A 33-year-old woman presents for evaluation after three consecutive first-trimester miscarriages.
cases.history: A 33-year-old G3P0 woman presents with her partner after three consecutive pregnancy losses. All three were spontaneous, the first at 7 weeks and the next two at 9 and 10 weeks; one was confirmed by ultrasound to have had cardiac activity before demise. She conceives easily and has regular 28-day cycles. She reports no recurrent infections, but she recalls one prior episode of left calf swelling and pain a few years ago that was never formally worked up. She denies arthralgias, malar rash, photosensitivity, oral ulcers, or Raynaud phenomenon currently. PMH is otherwise unremarkable. She takes a prenatal vitamin and folic acid 400 mcg daily and has no known drug allergies. She does not smoke, drinks rarely, and works as an attorney. Family history includes a mother with lupus and a sister with two miscarriages. Her partner is healthy with a normal karyotype reported elsewhere. She is emotionally exhausted by the losses and is seeking an explanation and a plan before attempting again. Her BMI is 24 kg/m2 and her cycles remain ovulatory.
cases.exam: She is well-appearing and in no distress. The skin shows no malar rash, photosensitive lesions, or livedo reticularis. There are no oral ulcers. The thyroid is normal. Cardiopulmonary examination is unremarkable. The abdomen is soft and nontender. On pelvic examination the external genitalia, vagina, and cervix are normal, the uterus is normal in size and mobile, and there are no adnexal masses. There is no joint swelling, tenderness, or synovitis. The left calf is nontender today without swelling, erythema, or a palpable cord, and there is no leg-length or circumference asymmetry. Neurologic examination is normal. No lymphadenopathy is appreciated.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Recurrent pregnancy loss workup is indicated. Which evaluation is most appropriate as part of the standard panel?
options: Immediate empiric IVF with preimplantation genetic testing as the first step today
Thrombophilia testing for factor V Leiden as the single most important test in this clinical context
Only reassurance, since most recurrent losses have no identifiable cause as the first step today
Antiphospholipid antibody testing, parental karyotypes, uterine cavity assessment,
stages.explanation: Recurrent pregnancy loss (typically two or more, certainly three) warrants a structured workup: antiphospholipid antibody testing (the most important treatable cause), parental karyotypes for balanced translocations, evaluation of the uterine cavity (e.g., sonohysterogram/hysteroscopy) for anomalies, and endocrine screening (TSH, prolactin, and assessment for diabetes). Reassurance alone is inappropriate after three losses, especially with a possible prior thrombotic event and a family history of autoimmunity. Inherited thrombophilias such as factor V Leiden are not routinely tested as the primary cause of recurrent early loss and are far less established than antiphospholipid syndrome. Empiric IVF with preimplantation genetic testing does not address an untreated antiphospholipid syndrome and is not first-line before the standard evaluation.

stages.label: LABORATORY RESULTS
stages.content:
Lupus anticoagulant: positive (confirmed on dilute Russell viper venom time with mixing studies)
Anticardiolipin IgG: 60 GPL units (high positive)
Anti-beta-2 glycoprotein I IgG: 48 units (high positive)
Parental karyotypes: both normal
Sonohysterogram: normal uterine cavity
TSH 2.0 mIU/L, prolactin 12 ng/mL, hemoglobin A1c 5.2%

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The lupus anticoagulant and antibodies are positive. What is required to formally diagnose antiphospholipid syndrome?
options: Confirmation requires a lupus diagnosis first as the primary diagnosis
Persistent antibody positivity on repeat testing at least 12 weeks apart, plus clinical criteria
A single positive antibody test is sufficient for diagnosis as the primary diagnosis
A positive ANA in addition to the antibodies as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Antiphospholipid syndrome (APS) requires BOTH a laboratory criterion — a positive lupus anticoagulant, anticardiolipin, or anti-beta-2 glycoprotein I antibody confirmed persistently on two occasions at least 12 weeks apart — AND a clinical criterion, namely vascular thrombosis or defined pregnancy morbidity (such as three or more consecutive early losses, a fetal death at or beyond 10 weeks, or severe preeclampsia/placental insufficiency). A single positive test can be transient (e.g., from infection) and is insufficient. A positive ANA is not required and is nonspecific. APS can be primary; it does not require a prior diagnosis of systemic lupus erythematosus, although it may occur secondary to it. Her three losses plus persistent high-titer antibodies (after repeat confirmation) would meet criteria.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Antibodies remain positive on repeat testing 12 weeks later, confirming obstetric APS. When she conceives again, what is the recommended treatment to improve live-birth rates?
options: High-intensity warfarin throughout pregnancy as initial therapy
Low-dose aspirin plus prophylactic-dose heparin
Prednisone monotherapy as initial therapy
Combined oral contraceptive plus aspirin as initial therapy
stages.explanation: For obstetric APS, the evidence-based regimen is low-dose aspirin combined with prophylactic-dose heparin (low-molecular-weight heparin is preferred over unfractionated heparin for convenience and safety), started in early pregnancy; this combination improves live-birth rates compared with aspirin alone. Warfarin is teratogenic (warfarin embryopathy) and is avoided in pregnancy, particularly in the first trimester. Combined oral contraceptives are contraindicated in APS because estrogen further increases thrombotic risk, and they obviously do not support a pregnancy. Prednisone is not the standard therapy for obstetric APS and adds risks (gestational diabetes, preterm birth) without the benefit that heparin plus aspirin provides. She also needs counseling about her elevated maternal thrombotic risk.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: She is now pregnant on aspirin and heparin. APS pregnancies are high risk; which surveillance plan is most appropriate during the pregnancy?
options: Discontinue heparin in the second trimester once the first-trimester loss window has passed
Weekly lupus anticoagulant titers to adjust therapy as the immediate next step
Serial growth ultrasounds
Routine prenatal care identical to a low-risk pregnancy as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Obstetric APS pregnancies are at increased risk of placental insufficiency, fetal growth restriction, preeclampsia, and stillbirth, so they require enhanced surveillance: serial growth ultrasounds, antenatal testing (e.g., Doppler studies and nonstress tests in the third trimester), and vigilant blood pressure and proteinuria monitoring for preeclampsia. Treating her like a low-risk pregnancy ignores these documented risks. Repeating lupus anticoagulant titers serially does not guide management; the diagnosis is already established and antibody levels are not titrated to adjust therapy. Stopping heparin in the second trimester is dangerous — therapy continues through pregnancy and the postpartum period (when thrombotic risk is highest), and losses in APS can occur well beyond the first trimester.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Given her prior possible deep vein thrombosis and APS, which additional consideration is most important for her care?
options: Aspirin alone fully eliminates her thrombotic risk as the leading concern
She should avoid all anticoagulation because of bleeding risk as the leading concern
She has a high lifetime thrombosis risk
She is at low risk for thrombosis and needs no special precautions as the leading concern
stages.explanation: APS carries a substantial lifetime risk of venous and arterial thrombosis. If her prior calf event is confirmed to have been a deep vein thrombosis (thrombotic APS), she warrants therapeutic-dose anticoagulation during pregnancy (rather than only prophylactic dosing) and typically indefinite anticoagulation outside of pregnancy, given the high recurrence risk. Saying she is low risk and needs no precautions ignores the diagnosis. Avoiding all anticoagulation would expose her to dangerous thrombosis; the bleeding risk is managed, not a reason to withhold indicated therapy. Aspirin alone does not eliminate thrombotic risk in APS. Clarifying her thrombotic history is therefore pivotal, as it distinguishes purely obstetric APS (prophylactic heparin) from thrombotic APS (therapeutic anticoagulation).

cases.diagnosis: Recurrent pregnancy loss due to antiphospholipid syndrome
pearls: Recurrent pregnancy loss workup includes antiphospholipid antibodies, parental karyotypes, uterine cavity assessment, and thyroid/prolactin screening.
APS requires persistent antibody positivity (lupus anticoagulant, anticardiolipin, or anti-beta-2 glycoprotein I) on testing 12 weeks apart PLUS clinical criteria.
Obstetric APS is treated with low-dose aspirin plus prophylactic low-molecular-weight heparin to improve live-birth rates; warfarin is teratogenic.
A prior thrombotic event upgrades management to therapeutic anticoagulation in pregnancy and indefinite anticoagulation otherwise.

cases.title: Urine Leakage with Coughing and Exercise
cases.chiefComplaint: A 54-year-old woman reports involuntary urine leakage when she coughs, sneezes, or exercises.
cases.history: A 54-year-old G3P3 woman presents with two years of involuntary urine loss triggered by coughing, sneezing, laughing, and exercise such as jumping or jogging. She loses small spurts of urine with these activities but denies a sudden, overwhelming urge preceding the leakage. She wears one to two pads daily. She has three children, all delivered vaginally, including one with a large baby and a forceps-assisted delivery. She is perimenopausal with irregular periods and reports some vaginal dryness. She denies dysuria, hematuria, frequency beyond normal, nocturia, or recurrent urinary tract infections. She has no neurologic symptoms. PMH includes obesity and mild osteoarthritis. Medications are acetaminophen as needed; she takes no diuretics. She has no known drug allergies. She drinks three cups of coffee daily and is a former smoker who quit five years ago. Family history is noncontributory. The leakage is affecting her willingness to exercise and participate in social activities, and she is seeking treatment. Her BMI is 33 kg/m2.
cases.exam: She is well-appearing with a BMI of 33. The abdomen is soft and nontender without a palpable bladder or masses. On pelvic examination the vaginal tissues show mild atrophy. With a comfortably full bladder, a cough stress test in the lithotomy position produces immediate, brief leakage of urine from the urethral meatus coincident with the cough. There is mild anterior vaginal wall descent (a small cystocele) but no significant prolapse beyond the hymen. The urethra is mobile with straining. Neurologic exam of the lower extremities is normal, including sensation and reflexes; perineal sensation and anal sphincter tone are intact. A post-void residual measured by bladder scan is 40 mL.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial evaluation to support the diagnosis and exclude confounders?
options: Urodynamic testing before any treatment as the first step today
Urinalysis, post-void residual,
Renal ultrasound to assess for obstruction in this clinical context
Cystoscopy to inspect the bladder as the first step today
stages.explanation: Uncomplicated stress urinary incontinence is a clinical diagnosis. The basic office evaluation includes a focused history, urinalysis (to exclude infection or hematuria), a post-void residual (to exclude incomplete emptying/overflow), and a cough stress test to demonstrate the sign of stress incontinence — all of which she has. Urodynamic testing is not required before initial or even surgical treatment in straightforward, demonstrable stress incontinence; it is reserved for mixed, complex, or prior-failed cases. Cystoscopy is indicated for hematuria, suspected fistula or foreign body, or recurrent infections, not routine SUI. Renal ultrasound is unnecessary without evidence of obstruction or elevated residual. The positive cough stress test with a normal residual strongly supports stress incontinence.

stages.label: OFFICE TESTING
stages.content:
Urinalysis: no leukocyte esterase, no nitrites, no blood, no glucose
Urine culture: pending, no symptoms of infection
Post-void residual (bladder scan): 40 mL
Cough stress test: positive — immediate leakage coincident with cough
Urethral mobility: present (positive Q-tip/straining angle change)

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Overflow incontinence as the primary diagnosis
Urinary tract infection causing incontinence
Urge urinary incontinence (overactive bladder)
Stress urinary incontinence
stages.explanation: Leakage that occurs precisely with increases in intra-abdominal pressure (cough, sneeze, exertion) without a preceding urge, confirmed by a positive cough stress test and a mobile urethra, defines stress urinary incontinence. It results from urethral hypermobility and/or intrinsic sphincter deficiency, commonly after vaginal deliveries. Urge incontinence presents with sudden urgency and leakage of larger volumes, often with frequency and nocturia, which she denies. Overflow incontinence is excluded by her low post-void residual; it presents with continuous dribbling and a distended bladder. A urinary tract infection is excluded by a negative urinalysis and absence of irritative symptoms. Her clinical and exam findings are classic for stress incontinence.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first-line management?
options: Midurethral sling surgery as initial therapy
Pelvic floor muscle exercises, weight loss,
Oxybutynin as initial therapy
Indwelling urinary catheter as initial therapy
stages.explanation: First-line management of stress incontinence is conservative: supervised pelvic floor muscle training (Kegel exercises), weight loss (especially relevant given her obesity), reduction of bladder irritants such as caffeine, and consideration of a pessary or continence device. These measures are effective, low-risk, and recommended before surgery. A midurethral sling is the most effective surgical option but is reserved for patients who fail or decline conservative therapy. Oxybutynin is an antimuscarinic for urge incontinence and does not treat stress incontinence. An indwelling catheter is inappropriate, would not correct the problem, and introduces infection risk. Topical vaginal estrogen may help associated atrophy but is adjunctive rather than the primary treatment for the stress component.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: After several months of diligent pelvic floor therapy and weight loss she still has bothersome leakage and desires definitive treatment. What is the most effective surgical option?
options: Midurethral sling placement
Sacral neuromodulation in this setting
Anterior colporrhaphy alone
Botulinum toxin injection into the detrusor
stages.explanation: The midurethral sling is the most effective and standard surgical treatment for stress urinary incontinence, providing high cure rates by supporting the midurethra during increases in abdominal pressure. Anterior colporrhaphy repairs a cystocele but is not an effective treatment for stress incontinence and should not be used for that indication. Sacral neuromodulation and intradetrusor botulinum toxin are therapies for refractory urge incontinence/overactive bladder, not stress incontinence, and would not address her sphincteric/urethral support problem. Urethral bulking agents are an alternative, less invasive option with lower efficacy. Given her demonstrable stress incontinence that failed conservative therapy, the midurethral sling is the appropriate definitive procedure.

pearls: Stress incontinence is leakage with increased intra-abdominal pressure (cough, sneeze, exertion) without preceding urge; a positive cough stress test confirms it.
Uncomplicated SUI is a clinical diagnosis; urodynamics are reserved for mixed, complex, or prior-failed cases.
First-line therapy is conservative: pelvic floor muscle training, weight loss, and reducing bladder irritants.
The midurethral sling is the most effective surgical treatment when conservative measures fail.

cases.title: Sudden Urinary Urgency and Nighttime Trips to the Bathroom
cases.chiefComplaint: A 66-year-old woman reports sudden, strong urges to urinate with occasional leakage before she reaches the toilet.
cases.history: A 66-year-old G2P2 woman presents with one year of sudden, intense urges to urinate that she struggles to defer, sometimes leaking a moderate volume before reaching the bathroom. She urinates frequently during the day (about every 1.5 hours) and wakes three times nightly to void. The leakage is not triggered by coughing or exertion but by the sudden urge itself, often provoked by running water or arriving home ('key-in-the-lock'). She denies dysuria, hematuria, fever, or recurrent infections. She has no significant prolapse symptoms and no neurologic complaints. PMH includes hypertension and type 2 diabetes (well controlled). Medications are lisinopril 20 mg daily and metformin 1000 mg twice daily; she takes no diuretics. She has no known drug allergies. She drinks four cups of coffee and a cola daily and limited water. She is a nonsmoker and drinks alcohol occasionally. Family history is noncontributory. She is postmenopausal and not on hormone therapy. The symptoms disrupt her sleep and limit her outings, and she wants relief. Cognition is intact.
cases.exam: She is well-appearing and ambulates independently. The abdomen is soft and nontender with no palpable bladder. On pelvic examination there is mild vaginal atrophy and no significant prolapse beyond the hymen. A cough stress test with a full bladder does not produce leakage. The urethra and bladder neck are unremarkable. Neurologic examination of the lower extremities is normal with intact sensation, normal reflexes, and intact perineal sensation and anal sphincter tone. There is no costovertebral angle tenderness. A post-void residual by bladder scan is 30 mL. Mental status is normal and gait is steady.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial step in evaluating her symptoms?
options: Start an antimuscarinic empirically without any testing as the first step today
Obtain a urinalysis and post-void residual, review medications and fluid intake, and consider a voiding diary
Refer immediately for urodynamic testing as the first step today
Order cystoscopy to rule out bladder cancer as the first step today
stages.explanation: The initial evaluation of overactive bladder is clinical: a urinalysis to exclude infection and hematuria, a post-void residual to exclude incomplete emptying, a review of medications and fluid/caffeine intake (she drinks excessive caffeine), and often a bladder/voiding diary to document frequency, volumes, and triggers. This identifies reversible contributors before treatment. Starting an antimuscarinic without excluding infection, retention, or hematuria can mask serious disease and may be inappropriate. Urodynamics are not required for uncomplicated overactive bladder and are reserved for refractory or diagnostically unclear cases. Cystoscopy is indicated for hematuria or other red flags; her urinalysis is needed first and there is no painless hematuria documented yet to mandate immediate cystoscopy.

stages.label: OFFICE TESTING
stages.content:
Urinalysis: negative for leukocyte esterase, nitrites, blood, and glucose
Urine culture: no growth
Post-void residual (bladder scan): 30 mL
Cough stress test: negative
Bladder diary: voids every 1-2 hours, 3 nocturia episodes, urgency with several urge-related leaks per day; high caffeine intake noted

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Urge urinary incontinence
Urinary tract infection
Overflow incontinence
Stress urinary incontinence
stages.explanation: Sudden, hard-to-defer urgency with urge-associated leakage, urinary frequency, nocturia, and characteristic triggers (running water, arriving home), with a negative cough stress test and low post-void residual, defines urge urinary incontinence due to overactive bladder (detrusor overactivity). Stress incontinence is excluded by the negative cough stress test and the absence of exertional leakage. Overflow incontinence is excluded by the low post-void residual; it features chronic retention and dribbling. A urinary tract infection is excluded by a negative urinalysis and culture and the absence of dysuria. Her excessive caffeine intake is a modifiable contributor. The picture is classic for overactive bladder with urgency incontinence.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
options: Behavioral therapy: bladder training, timed voiding, fluid
Oral antimuscarinic medication as initial therapy
Intradetrusor botulinum toxin injection as initial therapy
Sacral neuromodulation as initial therapy
stages.explanation: First-line treatment of overactive bladder is behavioral: bladder training with scheduled/timed voiding and urge suppression, reducing caffeine and excessive fluids, weight management, and pelvic floor muscle training. These are effective, low-risk, and recommended before pharmacotherapy, and her high caffeine intake is an obvious target. Antimuscarinics (and beta-3 agonists like mirabegron) are second-line pharmacologic options added when behavioral measures are insufficient. Intradetrusor botulinum toxin and sacral neuromodulation are third-line therapies reserved for refractory cases that fail both behavioral and pharmacologic treatment. Starting with an invasive or third-line therapy would skip safer, effective first steps that also address her modifiable contributors.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: After behavioral measures she has improved but still has bothersome urgency. She is considering an antimuscarinic. Which counseling point is most important for this 66-year-old?
options: Antimuscarinics have no meaningful side effects in older adults as the immediate next step
She should use an antimuscarinic plus mirabegron together as initial therapy as the immediate next step
Antimuscarinics are first-line over all behavioral therapy as the immediate next step
Antimuscarinics can cause dry mouth, constipation, and cognitive effects
stages.explanation: In older adults, antimuscarinics carry anticholinergic side effects — dry mouth, constipation, blurred vision, urinary retention, and importantly cognitive impairment and possible dementia risk with cumulative anticholinergic burden — so they must be used cautiously. Mirabegron, a beta-3 adrenergic agonist, is an effective alternative that avoids anticholinergic effects (monitor blood pressure), making it attractive in the elderly. Claiming antimuscarinics have no meaningful side effects is false and dangerous. Combination antimuscarinic-plus-mirabegron therapy may be used in refractory cases but is not appropriate initial pharmacotherapy. Antimuscarinics are not first-line over behavioral therapy; behavioral measures remain the foundation, with pharmacotherapy layered on for residual symptoms.

pearls: Overactive bladder presents with sudden urgency, frequency, nocturia, and urge-related leakage with a negative cough stress test and normal post-void residual.
Initial workup excludes infection and retention and reviews modifiable contributors like caffeine and medications.
Behavioral therapy (bladder training, fluid/caffeine modification, pelvic floor exercises) is first-line before pharmacotherapy.
In older adults, weigh anticholinergic burden of antimuscarinics; mirabegron (beta-3 agonist) avoids cognitive side effects.

cases.title: Sensation of a Vaginal Bulge and Pelvic Pressure
cases.chiefComplaint: A 68-year-old woman reports a feeling of something bulging out of her vagina along with pelvic pressure.
cases.history: A 68-year-old G4P4 woman presents with a one-year sensation of a bulge protruding from the vagina, worse at the end of the day and with prolonged standing or lifting, and relieved by lying down. She feels pelvic pressure and sometimes must press on the bulge or splint the vagina to fully empty her bladder. She reports occasional incomplete emptying and a slow stream but denies frank incontinence currently. She denies vaginal bleeding, discharge, dysuria, or fever. She had four vaginal deliveries, including two large infants. She is postmenopausal and not on hormone therapy. PMH includes chronic constipation, obesity, and hypertension on amlodipine 5 mg daily. She has chronic cough from prior smoking (quit 8 years ago). She has no known drug allergies. She does not drink alcohol. Family history is noncontributory. She is sexually active and concerned about the impact on intimacy. She is otherwise functional and independent. Her BMI is 32 kg/m2 and she is seeking options short of major surgery if possible.
cases.exam: She is well-appearing. The abdomen is soft and nontender. On pelvic examination with Valsalva in the lithotomy and standing positions, there is descent of the anterior vaginal wall (cystocele) to about 1 cm beyond the hymen, consistent with a stage II-III anterior compartment prolapse. The cervix/uterus descends slightly but the apex remains above the hymen, and the posterior wall shows only mild descent. The vaginal epithelium is atrophic and there is no ulceration or erosion of the prolapsed tissue. A cough stress test is negative at rest but the urethra is kinked by the cystocele. Post-void residual is 90 mL. Neurologic exam of the perineum and lower extremities is normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial assessment for her symptomatic prolapse?
options: Immediate referral for hysterectomy as the first step today
CT abdomen/pelvis to evaluate the bulge as the first step today
Examination with a validated staging system including Valsalva, plus a post-void residual
Pelvic MRI to grade the prolapse as the first step today
stages.explanation: Pelvic organ prolapse is evaluated clinically by examining the vaginal compartments during Valsalva (often standing) and quantifying descent with a validated system such as the POP-Q, identifying which compartment(s) are involved. A post-void residual screens for incomplete emptying (hers is mildly elevated at 90 mL due to urethral kinking), and symptom impact guides management. Pelvic MRI and CT are not needed for routine diagnosis and staging, which are clinical. Immediate referral for hysterectomy is premature — many women are managed conservatively or with a pessary, and surgery is individualized, often uterus-sparing. The exam shows a predominant anterior compartment (cystocele) prolapse, and her elevated residual and splinting confirm clinically significant prolapse.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most accurate diagnosis?
options: Uterine procidentia (complete uterine prolapse)
Anterior vaginal wall prolapse (cystocele)
Vaginal wall cyst as the primary diagnosis
Rectocele (posterior compartment prolapse)
stages.explanation: The examination shows predominant descent of the anterior vaginal wall to beyond the hymen — a cystocele (anterior compartment prolapse) — with associated urethral kinking causing incomplete emptying and the need to splint. A rectocele involves the posterior wall, which shows only mild descent here. Uterine procidentia is complete eversion with the uterus protruding entirely outside the introitus; her apex remains above the hymen, so this is not procidentia. A vaginal wall cyst is a discrete cystic mass, not a reducible bulge that worsens with Valsalva and improves when supine. Her reproducible, position-dependent anterior bulge with splinting is classic for a cystocele.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: She wants to avoid surgery if possible. What is the most appropriate first-line management option to offer?
options: A vaginal pessary, along with pelvic floor muscle training
Observation only with no intervention as initial therapy
Anterior colporrhaphy as initial therapy
Total vaginal hysterectomy with apical suspension as initial therapy
stages.explanation: For symptomatic prolapse in a woman who prefers to avoid surgery, a vaginal pessary is the first-line nonsurgical treatment — it mechanically supports the prolapse, relieves symptoms, and is effective and reversible. This is paired with pelvic floor muscle training and addressing contributors (constipation, chronic cough, weight, and vaginal atrophy with topical estrogen to reduce erosion risk). Observation alone is reasonable only for minimally symptomatic prolapse, but she is bothered and has functional impact. Anterior colporrhaphy and hysterectomy with apical suspension are surgical options reserved for women who decline or fail a pessary, or who prefer definitive surgery; offering surgery first ignores her stated preference and the effective conservative option.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: She tries a pessary but cannot retain it and finds it uncomfortable, and she now wants surgical repair. Which principle should guide the surgical plan?
options: A hysterectomy alone corrects prolapse without need for apical suspension as the immediate next step
Apical support is the cornerstone of durable repair,
Repairing only the anterior wall reliably prevents all future prolapse as the immediate next step
Mesh is mandatory for all prolapse repairs as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Durable prolapse surgery hinges on adequate apical support; without resuspending the apex (uterosacral or sacrospinous ligament fixation, or sacrocolpopexy), isolated anterior repairs have high recurrence rates. The plan should address the apex and the anterior defect together and evaluate for occult (masked) stress incontinence, which a large cystocele can hide by kinking the urethra and may unmask after repair. Repairing only the anterior wall does not reliably prevent recurrence because the apical defect remains. Hysterectomy alone removes the uterus but does not provide apical support — the vault must still be suspended. Mesh is not mandatory; native-tissue repairs are standard for most, with mesh (e.g., sacrocolpopexy) selectively used given regulatory and complication considerations.

pearls: Prolapse is diagnosed and staged clinically with Valsalva using a validated system (POP-Q); a cystocele is anterior wall descent.
A vaginal pessary is the first-line nonsurgical treatment for symptomatic prolapse and is effective and reversible.
Address contributors: constipation, chronic cough, obesity, and atrophy (topical estrogen reduces pessary-related erosion).
Durable surgical repair requires apical support; a large cystocele can mask occult stress incontinence that may appear after repair.

cases.title: Constant Wetness After a Recent Hysterectomy
cases.chiefComplaint: A 40-year-old woman reports continuous leakage of fluid from the vagina since a hysterectomy two weeks ago.
cases.history: A 40-year-old G3P3 woman presents with continuous, painless leakage of clear fluid from the vagina that began about ten days after a total abdominal hysterectomy performed for symptomatic fibroids. She describes constant dampness requiring multiple pads, present both day and night, without any sense of urgency or relationship to position or activity; she also still voids normally per urethra. She denies fever, flank pain, dysuria, or vaginal bleeding. The hysterectomy was reportedly difficult, with extensive adhesions and significant intraoperative blood loss. She has no prior pelvic radiation. PMH is otherwise unremarkable. Medications are an oral iron supplement and acetaminophen as needed; she has no known drug allergies. She does not smoke or drink alcohol and works as a teacher. Family history is noncontributory. She is distressed and embarrassed by the constant wetness and odor and is worried something went wrong during surgery. She has not had intercourse since the operation. She is otherwise recovering and afebrile.
cases.exam: She is well-appearing but anxious, with a faint urine odor. The abdomen is soft with a healing low transverse incision; it is nontender without distension or mass, and there is no flank tenderness. On speculum examination there is a pool of clear, watery fluid in the vaginal vault near the apex; the vaginal cuff sutures are intact without obvious dehiscence, and a small area of granulation tissue is noted at the cuff. No active bleeding or purulent discharge is seen. The clear fluid appears to be welling up continuously from near the cuff rather than from the urethra. The urethra and bladder neck appear normal externally. Neurologic exam is normal. The leakage is continuous and not provoked by cough or Valsalva specifically.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the best initial step to confirm that the continuous leakage is urine from a vesicovaginal fistula?
options: Start empiric antibiotics for vaginitis as the first step today
Reassure her that this is normal postoperative discharge as the first step today
Perform a dye test , and/or check fluid creatinine
Send the vaginal fluid for culture as the first step today
stages.explanation: When continuous painless vaginal fluid leakage follows pelvic surgery, a vesicovaginal fistula must be confirmed. A dye test — instilling methylene blue (or sterile milk) into the bladder, with a tampon or direct inspection to see blue staining/leakage in the vagina — confirms a bladder-vaginal communication; checking the vaginal fluid creatinine (markedly higher than serum if it is urine) is a complementary, simple confirmatory test. Culturing the fluid does not establish the source. Empiric antibiotics for vaginitis ignore the mechanism and delay diagnosis. Reassurance is inappropriate and incorrect: normal postoperative discharge is not continuous watery leakage with a urine odor welling from the cuff. The dye test localizes and confirms the fistula.

stages.label: DIAGNOSTIC TESTING
stages.content:
Vaginal fluid creatinine 62 mg/dL (serum creatinine 0.8 mg/dL) — consistent with urine
Dye test: methylene blue instilled into bladder; blue staining appears at the vaginal apex
Tampon (three-swab/dye) test: proximal/upper tampon stains blue
Cystoscopy: small fistulous opening in the posterior bladder near the trigone/vault
CT urogram: no ureteral injury or hydronephrosis; contrast tracks from bladder to vagina

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the diagnosis, and what does the three-swab (tampon dye) test help distinguish?
options: Urethrovaginal fistula; the test confirms urethral leakage as the primary diagnosis
Vesicovaginal fistula
Normal cuff granulation tissue; the test rules out fistula as the primary diagnosis
Ureterovaginal fistula; the test localizes a ureteral injury as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: The findings confirm a vesicovaginal fistula: high vaginal fluid creatinine identifies the fluid as urine, methylene blue instilled into the bladder appears in the vagina, and cystoscopy shows the fistulous tract — a classic complication of difficult hysterectomy. In the three-swab (tampon) test, blue dye is placed in the bladder; staining of an upper/proximal swab indicates a vesicovaginal communication, whereas staining only with a separately administered systemic dye (e.g., phenazopyridine turning a swab orange) without bladder blue would suggest a ureterovaginal fistula. The CT urogram here shows no ureteral injury, supporting a bladder source. It is not urethrovaginal (leakage is continuous, not just with voiding) and is clearly not benign granulation tissue.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: The fistula is small and recently formed (about two weeks postoperative). What is the most appropriate initial management?
options: Permanent urinary diversion as initial therapy
Observation with pads alone and no catheter as initial therapy
Continuous bladder drainage with an indwelling catheter for several weeks, as small recent fistulas may heal spontaneously
Immediate open surgical repair within 24 hours as initial therapy
stages.explanation: For a small, recently diagnosed (early postoperative) vesicovaginal fistula, a trial of continuous bladder drainage with an indwelling catheter for several weeks (often 2-6 weeks) is appropriate, because some small fistulas close spontaneously once the bladder is kept decompressed and the tissue is allowed to heal. Immediate open repair is not first-line for a fresh fistula; operating on acutely inflamed, edematous tissue increases failure rates, and definitive repair is typically planned after inflammation subsides if catheter drainage fails. Permanent urinary diversion is reserved for irreparable or radiation-related fistulas, not a small surgical fistula. Observation with pads alone abandons the chance of spontaneous healing and leaves the patient symptomatic; bladder decompression is the key initial maneuver.

stages.label: DEFINITIVE TREATMENT
stages.question: After several weeks of catheter drainage the fistula persists. What principle guides definitive surgical repair?
options: Surgical repair with tension-free, well-vascularized, multilayer closure after inflammation has resolved
Repair should be delayed until at least one year regardless of cause as the immediate next step
A single-layer rapid closure is preferred to minimize operative time as the immediate next step
Repair must always be done through an abdominal approach with mesh as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Definitive repair of a vesicovaginal fistula follows surgical principles: operate once acute inflammation and infection have resolved, achieve a tension-free, watertight, multilayer closure with healthy well-vascularized tissue, and interpose a tissue flap (e.g., Martius labial fat pad or omentum) when the tissue is poor or the fistula is complex or recurrent, followed by postoperative bladder drainage. Waiting a full year regardless of cause is not required for nonradiation fistulas; timing is individualized once tissue is healthy. A single-layer rapid closure compromises durability and increases recurrence. The approach (vaginal versus abdominal) is selected by fistula location, size, and surgeon expertise — it is not mandatorily abdominal, and mesh is not required (and is generally avoided) for these repairs.

stages.label: PREVENTION
stages.question: What intraoperative practice most reduces the risk of bladder and ureteral injury during difficult hysterectomy?
options: Careful identification of the bladder
Always performing a supracervical hysterectomy to avoid the bladder as the immediate next step
Avoiding any cystoscopy because it prolongs the operation as the immediate next step
Routinely leaving the bladder distended throughout the procedure as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: The best protection against fistula is preventing and promptly recognizing lower urinary tract injury: meticulous identification of the bladder and ureters, sharp dissection to mobilize the bladder off the cervix/vagina (especially with dense adhesions), and intraoperative cystoscopy to detect bladder or ureteral injury so it can be repaired immediately rather than presenting later as a fistula. Avoiding cystoscopy removes a key detection tool and increases the chance of a missed injury. Leaving the bladder distended makes it more vulnerable to injury during dissection; the bladder is generally drained. A supracervical hysterectomy is not always appropriate or indicated and does not reliably prevent injury; the operation is dictated by the indication, not by injury avoidance alone.

pearls: Continuous painless vaginal urine leakage after pelvic surgery suggests a vesicovaginal fistula; difficult hysterectomy is a classic cause.
Confirm with a dye test (methylene blue in the bladder) and elevated vaginal fluid creatinine; the three-swab test distinguishes vesicovaginal from ureterovaginal fistula.
Small, early fistulas may heal with several weeks of continuous catheter bladder drainage before considering surgery.
Definitive repair uses tension-free, well-vascularized multilayer closure after inflammation resolves, with a tissue interposition flap for complex cases.
